lexis_case_text: Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Passes, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 216479 Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Passes, Inc., 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 207031 , 2024 WL 5257030 (Nov. 14, 2024) CONSUELO B. MARSHALL ORDER RE: DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO DISMISS COMPLAINT The matter before the Court is Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Complaint pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) . (Dkt. No. 44 (the "Motion").) I. BACKGROUND On September 27, 2024, Plaintiff filed the Complaint in this action which names Passes, Inc. (" Passes ") and Lucy Guo ("Guo") as defendants, and asserts the following four causes of action: (1) copyright infringement, 17 U.S.C. § 501 , against all Defendants; (2) violation of California Comprehensive Computer Access and Fraud Act, Cal. Pen. Code § 502 against all Defendants; (3) violation of California Unfair Competition Law, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 against all Defendants; and (4) breach of contract against Defendant Passes . Plaintiff's claims arise from Defendants' alleged "abuse" of the Services Agreement entered between the parties by allegedly stealing over 100,000 of Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies of celebrities and influencers, and alleged creation of a competing online website called " Passes Wiki" which featured Plaintiff's data and copyrighted biographies. II. STATEMENT OF THE LAW Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) allows a court to dismiss a complaint for "failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted." Dismissal of a complaint can be based on either a lack of a cognizable legal theory or the absence of sufficient facts alleged under a cognizable legal theory. Balistreri v. Pacifica Police Dep't , 901 F.2d 696 , 699 (9th Cir. 1990) . On a motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, courts accept as true all well-pleaded allegations of material fact and construes them in a light most favorable to the non-moving party. Manzarek v. St. Paul Fire & Marine Ins. Co. , 519 F.3d 1025 , 1031-32 (9th Cir. 2008) . To survive a motion to dismiss, the complaint "must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to 'state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.'" Ashcroft v. Iqbal , 556 U.S. 662 , 663 , 129 S. Ct. 1937 , 173 L. Ed. 2d 868 , (2009) (quoting Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly , 550 U.S. 544 , 570 , 127 S. Ct. 1955 , 167 L. Ed. 2d 929 (2007) ). A formulaic recitation of the elements of a cause of action will not suffice, and labels and conclusions are insufficient to meet the plaintiff's obligation to provide the grounds of entitlement to relief. Twombly , 550 U.S. at 555 . "Factual allegations must be enough to raise a right to relief above the speculative level." Id. If a complaint cannot be cured by additional factual allegations, dismissal without leave to amend is proper. Id. A court may consider the allegations contained in the pleadings, exhibits attached to or referenced in the complaint, and matters properly subject to judicial notice in ruling on a motion to dismiss. Tellabs, Inc. v. Makor Issues & Rights, Ltd. , 551 U.S. 308 , 322 , 127 S. Ct. 2499 , 168 L. Ed. 2d 179 (2007) ; In re Stac Elec. Sec. Litig. , 89 F.3d 1399 , 1405 n.4 (9th Cir. 1996) U.S. v. Ritchie , 342 F.3d 903 , 908 (9th Cir. 2003) . III. DISCUSSION A. Request for Judicial Notice Defendants request that the Court take judicial notice of the following: 1. Ex. 1 - Certified copies of "the deposits" for Exhibits B and E to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration numbers TXu 2-119-709 and TXu 2-302-694 which are the underlying works for these registrations for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 2. Ex. 2 — deposit for Exhibit C to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration number TXu 2-282-456 which is the underlying work for this registration for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 3. Ex. 3 - deposit for Exhibit D to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration number TX 9-082-717 which is the underlying work for this registration for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 4. Ex. 4 — "side-by-side comparisons of full biographies" for Barack Obama, Curtis Jackson (50 Cent), Adam Levine, Emma Brooks McAllister, Marko Terzo, N3on, and Angel Reese which include a screenshot of Famous Birthdays' biographies accessed by Defendants and a screenshot of Passes ' biography; 5. Ex. 5 — a redline comparison of the text for each of the biographies for Barack Obama, Curtis Jackson (50 Cent), Adam Levine, Emma Brooks McAllister, Marko Terzo, N3on, and Angel Reese based on contents from the parties' biographies; 6. Ex. 6 — paragraph 7 of the declaration of Patrick Zhang, Defendant Passes ' Chief Technology Officer who declares Defendants' Wiki biography pages are no longer publicly available. Exhibits 1-5 are incorporated by reference in the Complaint. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 6, 21, 49-98, Exs. B-E.) Because the Court may consider Exhibits 1-5 under the incorporation by reference doctrine, the Court denies Defendants' request for judicial notice of Exhibits 1-5 as moot. Exhibit 6, which consists of portions of a declaration from Defendants' Chief Technology Officer, is not incorporated by reference in the Complaint and is not a matter for which the Court can take judicial notice. See Gerritsen v. Warner Bros. Ent. Inc. , 112 F. Supp. 3d 1011 , 1021 (C.D. Cal. 2015) ; Strojnik v. Azul Hosp. Grp. , 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 207424 , 2019 WL 6467494 , at * 3 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 2, 2019) (citing U.S. v. Ritchie , 342 F.3d 903 , 907-08 (9th Cir. 2003) ). Therefore, the Court denies Defendants' request to take judicial notice of Ex. 6. B. Copyright Infringement To state a claim for copyright infringement claim, Plaintiff must allege it (1) "owns a valid copyright in [the work]" and (2) Defendants "copied protected aspects of [Plaintiff's work]." Rentmeester v. Nike, Inc. , 883 F.3d 1111 , 1116-17 (9th Cir. 2018) (citations omitted). The second element has two distinct components: "copying" and "unlawful appropriation." Id. "When the plaintiff lacks direct evidence of copying, [it] can attempt to prove it circumstantially by showing that the defendant had access to the plaintiff's work and that the two works share similarities probative of copying." Id. at 1117 . "Such proof creates a presumption of copying, which the defendant can then attempt to rebut by proving independent creation." Id. "To prove unlawful appropriation, . . . the similarities between the two works must be substantial and they must involve protected elements of the plaintiff's work." Id. "[W]hether works are substantially similar involves a two-part analysis consisting of the extrinsic test and the intrinsic test." Id. at 1118 (internal quotations and citations omitted). A plaintiff must satisfy both components, and therefore a lack of extrinsic similarity is fatal to a plaintiff's copyright case as a matter of law. White v. Twentieth Century Fox Corp. , 572 F. App'x 475 , 476-77 (9th Cir. 2014) (citing Funky Films, Inc. , 462 F.3d at 1081 ). 1. Ownership and Pre-Registration Defendants contend the Complaint does not allege the biographies referenced therein are protected by the alleged copyright registrations, and therefore Plaintiff fails to allege it owns a valid copyright in the works. 17 U.S.C. § 411(a) provides "no civil action for infringement of the copyright in any United States work shall be instituted until preregistration or registration of the copyright claim has been made in accordance with this title." (Emphasis added.) "[R]egistration ... has been made" for purposes of 17 U.S.C. § 411 "and a copyright owner may sue for infringement, when the Copyright Office registers a copyright." Fourth Est. Pub. Benefit Corp. v. Wall-Street.com, LLC , 586 U.S. 296 , 302 , 139 S. Ct. 881 , 203 L. Ed. 2d 147 (2019) . Therefore, "[b]efore pursuing an infringement claim in court, . . . a copyright claimant generally must comply with § 411(a) 's requirement that 'registration of the copyright claim has been made'" because "registration is akin to an administrative exhaustion requirement that the owner must satisfy before suing to enforce ownership rights." Id. at 301 . Here, Plaintiff fails to allege preregistration of the biographies for Marko Terzo and Angel Reese which are alleged in the Complaint prior to filing this lawsuit for copyright infringement. Therefore, Plaintiff's copyright claim cannot be based on infringement of those biographies. However, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff registered other biographies prior to filing suit and alleges there are over 100,000 examples of Defendants' infringement of Plaintiff's registered biographies ( see Compl. ¶¶ 21, 22, 47-48). Accordingly, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges preregistration and ownership of its copyrighted biographies which are the basis of its copyright infringement claim. See Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 15, 2022) . 2. Authorization under the Parties' Agreement Defendants also argue Plaintiff's copyright claim fails as a matter of law because Defendants' use and display of Plaintiff's biographies are authorized under the parties' agreement. However, the Complaint alleges "Famous Birthdays never authorized Defendants to make copies or publicly display any of the works, whether through a license or otherwise." (Compl. ¶ 128.) Defendants also contend Section 4.1 of the parties' agreement expressly permitted "non-public, internal use of [Plaintiff's] data for Business Purposes of [ Passes ]." ( Id. Ex. A, § 4.1.) However, Section 4.1 of the parties' agreement provides "[t]he data and information made available through the Services (the 'Data') are the intellectual property of Famous Birthdays" and "Customer shall not transfer, sell, or publicly display the data." ( Id. ) Therefore, accepting the Complaint's allegations as true, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges facts that Defendants' use and display of Plaintiff's biographies were not authorized under the parties' agreement. 3. Factual Compilations or Original Prose Defendants also contend Plaintiff's copyright claim fails as a matter of law because facts are not subject to copyright protection and Defendants' biographies are not derivative, and Defendants' biographies are not virtually identical nor substantially similar to Plaintiff's biographies as required for Plaintiff's biographies to be protected as a factual compilation. "[A]ll facts—scientific, historical, biographical, and news of the day . . . may not be copyrighted and are part of the public domain available to every person." Feist Publications, Inc. v. Rural Tel. Serv. Co. , 499 U.S. 340 , 348 , 111 S. Ct. 1282 , 113 L. Ed. 2d 358 (1991) (citation omitted). However, "compilations of facts generally are" copyrightable. Id. at 344 . Here, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff's biographies are drafted by its staff and are not copied or automated, Plaintiff's staff use their "expertise" in drafting the biographies including determining what details are salient, which are fun, what can be omitted, what should be highlighted, and writing it in a way that is approachable, entertaining, and snappy" (Compl. ¶¶ 19, 20). Therefore, the alleged methods used in drafting Plaintiff's biographies "do not entail simple replication of the data that [plaintiff] receives" and "does not include the entire relevant universe known to it" and therefore Plaintiff's biographies are "entitled to limited protection" because the biographies are "copyrightable as compilations" since Plaintiff's employees "use their judgment in selecting which [facts] to include" and "exclude information they deem irrelevant to the interests of" Plaintiff's readers which is sufficient to allege "at least minimal creativity." Experian Info. Sols., Inc. v. Nationwide Mktg. Servs. Inc. , 893 F.3d 1176 , 1185 (9th Cir. 2018) ; see also Harper & Row Publishers, Inc. v. Nation Enters. , 471 U.S. 539 , 547 , 105 S. Ct. 2218 , 85 L. Ed. 2d 588 (1985) ; Advanta-STAR Auto. Rsch. Corp. of Am. v. Search Optics, LLC , 672 F. Supp. 3d 1035 , 1048 ( S.D. Cal. 2023 ) . As to Defendants' contention that Plaintiff cannot prevail on its copyright claim because Defendants' biographies "are not virtually identical to Plaintiff's biographies," Defendants argues Plaintiff fails to satisfy the virtual identity requirement for its copyright claim because the Complaint alleges fewer than 80% of the works in Plaintiff's factual compilation were allegedly infringed by Defendants, citing to paragraphs 20 and 38 of the Complaint. However, paragraphs 20 and 38 do not contain such allegations. ( See Compl. ¶ 20 (alleging Plaintiff's process for drafting its biographies); id. ¶ 38 ("To accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile. The script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies. Forensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024.").) Moreover, the Complaint does not allege the entire content of all of the parties' biographies, and neither Plaintiff's Complaint nor Defendants' request for judicial notice attaches all of the biographies at issue in this lawsuit. Accordingly, the Court cannot determine at the motion to dismiss stage as a matter of law whether Defendants' biographies are "virtually identical" to Plaintiff's biographies so as to infringe on Plaintiff's copyrighted works. See Experian , 893 F.3d at 1186 . However, the Complaint alleges "[t]he biographies that [Defendant] displays in its website are virtually identical to [Plaintiff's] copyrighted Works." (Compl. ¶ 43.) The Court must accept this allegation as true at the pleading stage, and thus finds the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants' biographies are virtually identical to Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies. See also Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 15, 2022) . 4. Copying Contrary to Defendants contention, the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants copied Plaintiff's copyrighted works. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 124-26.) 5. Substantial Similarity Defendants also contend the parties' biographies are not substantially similar as a matter of law. Because the Court does not have all of Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies and all of Defendants' allegedly infringing biographies, the Court cannot determine at this stage whether the parties' works are substantially similarity under the extrinsic test. See Zindel as Tr. for David Zindel Tr. v. Fox Searchlight Pictures, Inc. , 815 F. App'x 158 , 159 (9th Cir. 2020) . 6. Fair Use Defendants contend Plaintiff's copyright claim fails because Defendants' biographies are protected under the fair use doctrine. Whether a use constitutes fair use is a mixed question of law and fact. See Harper & Row, Publishers, Inc. v. Nation Enters. , 471 U.S. 539 , 560 , 105 S. Ct. 2218 , 85 L. Ed. 2d 588 (1985) ; Leadsinger, Inc. v. BMG Music Pub. , 512 F.3d 522 , 530 (9th Cir. 2008) . Therefore, in order to conduct a fair use analysis, the court "usually must make factual findings, or rely on undisputed or admitted material facts." Browne v. McCain , 611 F. Supp. 2d 1073 , 1078 (C.D. Cal. 2009) (citing Harper & Row Publishers, Inc. , 471 U.S. at 560 ). Here, the case is at the pleading stage and Plaintiff has not had the opportunity to conduct discovery, and may be unaware of material facts relevant to Defendants' fair use defense. Therefore, an assessment of Defendants' fair use defense at the pleading stage is premature. See Esi Grp. v. Wave Six, LLC , 2018 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 227699 , 2018 WL 6588524 , at * 5 (S.D. Cal. Sept. 25, 2018) ; Auto. Data Sols., Inc. v. Directed Elecs. Canada, Inc. , 2018 WL 4742289 , at * 7 (C.D. Cal. Aug. 15, 2018) ; Browne , 611 F. Supp. 2d at 1078 ; XpandOrtho, Inc. v. Zimmer Biomet Holdings, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 46698 , 2022 WL 801743 , at * 21 ( S.D. Cal. Mar. 15 , 2022) . 7. Reproduction Rights Plaintiff argues Defendants fail to address Plaintiff's other theories for Defendants' copyright liability in addition to Defendants' alleged public display of Plaintiff's work, including Defendants' violation of Plaintiff's exclusive right to reproduce its copyrighted work in copies by transferring Plaintiff's works from one device or server to another. 17 U.S.C. § 106(1) provides that a copyright owner has the "exclusive right . . . to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies." Defendants argue the Complaint contains no allegations that Defendants breached the parties' agreement by transferring data to ChatGPT. However, the Complaint alleges: Passes had substantial access to Famous Birthdays' copyrighted Works through Famous Birthdays' website—data obtained through the parties' historical licensing agreement—which Passes used to "reproduce the copyrighted work in copies" in violation of 17 U.S.C. § 106(1) . As revealed through the direct comparisons between the Works and Passes ' stolen biographies, as well as the secondary evidence of direct copying of URLs and Guo's direct access of over 100,000 profiles, Passes copied and used, and is copying and using, Famous Birthdays' Works publicly, as well as within its platform available to subscribing customers, and internally. (Compl. ¶ 124.) Therefore, taking the allegations in the Complaint as true, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges Defendants violated Plaintiff's exclusive right to reproduction of its copyrighted works in copies to state a claim for copyright infringement. See Disney Enters., Inc. v. VidAngel, Inc. , 224 F.Supp.3d 957 , 969-70 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 12, 2016) . * * * Accordingly, the Court denies Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's copyright infringement claim. C. California Comprehensive Computer Data Access and Fraud Act, Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) The Complaint alleges Defendants violated the California Comprehensive Computer Data Access and Fraud Act ("CDAFA"), Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) . Plaintiff contends his CDAFA claim is brought for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) , (8) . Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) provides: Except as provided in subdivision (h) , any person who commits any of the following acts is guilty of a public offense: (1) Knowingly accesses and without permission alters, damages, deletes, destroys, or otherwise uses any data, computer, computer system, or computer network in order to either (A) devise or execute any scheme or artifice to defraud, deceive, or extort, or (B) wrongfully control or obtain money, property, or data. (2) Knowingly accesses and without permission takes, copies, or makes use of any data from a computer, computer system, or computer network, or takes or copies any supporting documentation, whether existing or residing internal or external to a computer, computer system, or computer network. . . . (4) Knowingly accesses and without permission adds, alters, damages, deletes, or destroys any data, computer software, or computer programs which reside or exist internal or external to a computer, computer system, or computer network. . . . (6) Knowingly and without permission provides or assists in providing a means of accessing a computer, computer system, or computer network in violation of this section. (7) Knowingly and without permission accesses or causes to be accessed any computer, computer system, or computer network. (8) Knowingly introduces any computer contaminant into any computer, computer system, or computer network. 1. Standing Defendants contend Plaintiff lacks standing to bring a § 502 claim under the CDAFA because Plaintiff has not alleged it suffered harm from Defendants' alleged violations. To bring a civil action under Cal. Penal Code § 502 for compensatory damages or injunctive relief, as Plaintiff does here, Plaintiff must suffer damage or loss as a result of a violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502 . See Cal. Pen. Code § 502(e) ; Perkins v. LinkedIn Corp. , 53 F. Supp. 3d 1190 , 1219 (N.D. Cal. 2014) . Construing the allegations in the light most favorable to Plaintiff, the Court finds the Complaint alleges sufficient facts that Plaintiff suffered damage or loss as a result of Defendants' alleged conduct in violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) . ( See Compl. ¶¶ 38, 114, 118.) See Mintz v. Mark Bartelstein & Assocs. Inc. , 906 F. Supp. 2d 1017 , 1032 (C.D. Cal. 2012) ; Facebook, Inc. v. Power Ventures, Inc. , 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 93517 , 2010 WL 3291750 , at * 4 (N.D. Cal. 2010) ; Copart, Inc. v. Sparta Consulting, Inc. , 277 F. Supp. 3d 1127 , 1162 (E.D. Cal. 2017) ; PerkinElmer Health Scis., Inc. v. Salem , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33529 , 2022 WL 1592590 , at * 3 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 24, 2022) . 2. Particularity Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to plead its § 502 claim under the CDAFA with particularity as required under Fed. R. Civ. P. 9(b) , relying on paragraphs 135 and 142 of the Complaint. Paragraph 135 of the Complaint quotes Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1) which provides it is a violation of the CDAFA if the defendant "(1) Knowingly accesses and without permission alters, damages, deletes, destroys, or otherwise uses any data, computer, computer system, or computer network in order to either (A) devise or execute any scheme or artifice to defraud, deceive, or extort, or (B) wrongfully control or obtain money, property, or data." (Compl. ¶ 135 (quoting Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1) ).) Paragraph 142 of the Complaint, which alleges "Defendants are liable for compensatory, consequential, and incidental damages. Defendants are also liable for exemplary and punitive damages in an amount sufficient to set an example of Defendants' oppressive, fraudulent, and malicious conduct under § 502(e)(4) ," pertains to recovery of punitive damages under § 502(e)(4) . Because Plaintiff's CDAFA claim does not sound in fraud, Fed. R. Civ. P. 9 's heightened pleading standard does not apply. Even if Rule 9(b) 's heightened pleading standard did apply, the Court finds Plaintiff pleads its CDAFA claim with sufficient particularity because the Complaint alleges "Guo ran 106,124 lookups in a month, a blatant breach of the License Agreement," "[t]o accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies," and "[f]orensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024." (Compl. ¶¶ 37, 38.) The Complaint thus alleges the who, what, where, and when of Defendants' alleged conduct to give Defendants sufficient notice of the basis for Plaintiff's claim, and therefore satisfies Rule 9 's heightened pleading standard. 3. Without Permission Subsections 1 , 2 , 4 , 6 and 7 of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) each include a "knowingly accesses" and "without permission" requirement. Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to allege Defendants acted "without permission" as required for its § 502 claim under the CDAFA because the parties entered into a services agreement authorizing Defendants to access Plaintiff's data, and taking permitted data using an impermissible method does not violate the CDAFA. Plaintiff argues the fact that Defendants had access to Plaintiff's data based on the parties' services agreement does not save them from liability under § 502 because Defendants "exceeded their privileges" under the parties' agreement, the parties' service agreement gave Defendants individualized notice that Plaintiff's data was protected, and the agreement and Plaintiff's terms of service combined create liability for Defendants' actions under § 502 . Here, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff "enters into licensing agreements with companies . . . through its service known as Famous Birthdays Pro" which "allows some paying clients access to [Plaintiff's] API for limited, research purposes" (Compl. ¶ 26), Defendant Passes entered into a limited services agreement with Plaintiff on March 1, 2025 "which granted Passes access to Famous Birthdays Pro," and the agreement granted Defendant Passes a license "to make non-public, internal use of the data." ( Id. ¶¶ 26, 34, 36, Ex. A.) The Service Agreement entered into between the parties attached to the Complaint provided Defendant Passes with Application Programming Interface ("API") access to Plaintiff's data, and permitted Defendant Passes to conduct "2,5000 searches per month of historical rank graphs for creators profiled on the Famous Birthdays platform," "100 Report Searches per month with up to 50 records on each report," and "access to Famous Birthday's Pro API: Which includes the same data and allotments that are available via the Famous Birthdays Pro dashboard." ( Id. Ex. A.) The Services Agreement further stated: The data and information made available through the Services (the "Data") are the intellectual property of Famous Birthdays. Famous Birthdays hereby grants Customer a license to make non-public, internal use of the data for Business Purposes of the Customer for the duration of the term of this Agreement. Customer shall not transfer, sell, or publicly display the data. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Customer may use the data in a manner that would otherwise be restricted by this Agreement where such Data is, through no fault of Customer's, unprotectable under applicable law. ( Id. ) The Complaint alleges: 1. "Notwithstanding the term in the Services Agreement limiting Passes to 2,500 lookups per month to view historical rank graphs for creators profiled on Famous Birthdays, [Plaintiff's] internal dashboard reflects that [Defendant] Guo ran 106,124 lookups in a month, a blatant breach of the License Agreement," "[t]o accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the [Plaintiff's] API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though [Defendant] Passes had no right to make copies," and "[f]orensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024." (Compl. ¶¶ 37-38); 2. "Defendants compiled a URL list and used it and a script, a computer contaminant, to scrape [Plaintiff's] Works. Defendants knew that they were wrongfully using [Plaintiff's] Works without permission, because, among other things, Defendants exceeded the scope of the Services Agreement and sought to hide their misconduct by employing the services of a chatbot," and "Defendants knowingly accessed Famous Birthdays' computer networks and API, and then used a computer, computer system, computer network, or data (their script) to wrongfully obtain and control [Plaintiff's] data, in violation of the Services Agreement and [Plaintiff's] Terms of Service." ( Id. ¶¶ 136-37.) Because the Complaint alleges facts demonstrating Defendants had access to Plaintiff's data based on the parties' services agreement, and because Plaintiff's CDAFA claim under Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) is based on Defendants' improper use of the data that Plaintiff alleges it gave Defendants access to, Plaintiff fails to state a claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) . See Oracle USA, Inc. v. Rimini St., Inc. , 879 F.3d 948 , 962 (9th Cir. 2018) , rev'd on other grounds in 586 U.S. 334 , 139 S. Ct. 873 , 203 L. Ed. 2d 180 (2019) ; Van Buren v. United States , 593 U.S. 374 , 395-96 , 141 S. Ct. 1648 , 210 L. Ed. 2d 26 (2021) ; In re Oliveras , 103 Cal. App. 5th 771 , 782-83 , 323 Cal. Rptr. 3d 303 (Cal. Ct. App. 2024) , as modified on denial of reh'g (Aug. 2, 2024); Custom Packaging Supply, Inc. v. Phillips , 2015 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 164523 , 2015 WL 8334793 , at * 3 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 7, 2015) . The Court therefore grants Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's CDAFA claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) with leave to amend to allege facts that Defendants engaged in the alleged conduct "without permission" such as by circumventing technical or code-based barriers. 4. Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) Plaintiff also asserts Defendants violated Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) of the CDAFA (which does not include a "without permission" requirement). A defendant who "[k]nowingly introduces any computer contaminant into any computer, computer system, or computer network" violates Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) . "Computer contaminant" is defined as "any set of computer instructions that are designed to modify, damage, destroy, record, or transmit information within a computer, computer system, or computer network without the intent or permission of the owner of the information." Cal. Penal Code § 502(b)(12) . Here, Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to plausibly allege a violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) because Plaintiff does not allege facts that Defendants introduced a computer contaminant into Plaintiff's networks. Plaintiff contends that an automated script designed to transmit data contained within a computer system without permission constitutes a computer contaminant. Defendants argue that the automated script identified by Plaintiff is a tool designed by Plaintiff (not Defendants) to access public information, and that Plaintiff has not alleged nor explained what computer instructions Defendants introduced into Plaintiff's systems. The Complaint alleges " Passes, and Guo in particular, used their access to Famous Birthdays' API to create and employ a script to illegally scrape over one hundred thousand of Famous Birthdays' biographies over the course of four days—in gross excess of Passes ' contractual rights," " Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies," and "by introducing their script into [Plaintiff's] networks and API to steal [Plaintiff's] bios en masse, Defendants also violated § 502(c)(1)(B)(8) ." (Compl. ¶¶ 5, 38, 141.) Construing the allegations in the light most favorable to Plaintiff, the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants built and introduced a script into Plaintiff's networks that transmitted Plaintiff's data from its database. Therefore, the Court denies Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's CDAFA claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) . D. Unfair Competition Law, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 Plaintiff's unfair competition claim is predicated on the same alleged conduct alleged for its copyright infringement and CDAFA claims. Because Plaintiff pleads sufficient facts to state a claim for copyright infringement and violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) the CDAFA, Plaintiff also pleads sufficient facts to state a claim for violation of Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 . See Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 6 ; Fleites v. MindGeek S.A.R.L. , 617 F. Supp. 3d 1146 , 1166 (C.D. Cal. 2022) ; Medina v. Wells Fargo Bank , 2020 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73658 , 2020 WL 1934980 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Jan. 16, 2020) (citing Wolski v. Fremont Inv. & Loan , 127 Cal. App. 4th 347 , 357 , 25 Cal. Rptr. 3d 500 (Cal. Ct. App. 2005) ). E. Breach of Contract "In order to establish liability on a claim for breach of contract, Plaintiff[ ] must be able to establish that (1) there was a contract, (2) Plaintiff['s] performance or excuse for nonperformance, (3) Defendant's breach, and (4) damage to Plaintiff[] that resulted from Defendant's breach." Valenzuela v. ADT Sec. Servs. Inc. , 820 F. Supp. 2d 1061 , 1073 (C.D. Cal. 2010) (citing Wall Street Network, Ltd. v. New York Times Co. , 164 Cal. App. 4th 1171 , 1178 , 80 Cal. Rptr. 3d 6 (2008) ), aff'd , 475 F. App'x 115 (9th Cir. 2012) . Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to state a claim for breach of contract because Plaintiff has not identified any term of the parties' agreement that Defendants breached, and Plaintiff fails to allege damages from any breach. Here, the Complaint adequately alleges a contract, Plaintiff's performance, Defendants' breach, and damage to Plaintiff resulting from the breach to survive a motion to dismiss. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 34, 38-41, 117-19, 151, 152, 153; id. at Ex. A at § 4.1.) See id. ; see also Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 6 . Defendants also argue Plaintiff is not entitled to unjust enrichment damages or disgorgement for its breach of cont... Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Passes, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 216479 FAMOUS BIRTHDAYS, LLC , Plaintiff, v. PASSES, INC. et al., Defendants. Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Passes, Inc. Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Passes, Inc., 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 207031 , 2024 WL 5257030 (Nov. 14, 2024) CONSUELO B. MARSHALL ORDER RE: DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO DISMISS COMPLAINT The matter before the Court is Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Complaint pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) . (Dkt. No. 44 (the "Motion").) I. BACKGROUND On September 27, 2024, Plaintiff filed the Complaint in this action which names Passes, Inc. (" Passes ") and Lucy Guo ("Guo") as defendants, and asserts the following four causes of action: (1) copyright infringement, 17 U.S.C. § 501 , against all Defendants; (2) violation of California Comprehensive Computer Access and Fraud Act, Cal. Pen. Code § 502 against all Defendants; (3) violation of California Unfair Competition Law, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 against all Defendants; and (4) breach of contract against Defendant Passes . Plaintiff's claims arise from Defendants' alleged "abuse" of the Services Agreement entered between the parties by allegedly stealing over 100,000 of Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies of celebrities and influencers, and alleged creation of a competing online website called " Passes Wiki" which featured Plaintiff's data and copyrighted biographies. II. STATEMENT OF THE LAW Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) allows a court to dismiss a complaint for "failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted." Dismissal of a complaint can be based on either a lack of a cognizable legal theory or the absence of sufficient facts alleged under a cognizable legal theory. Balistreri v. Pacifica Police Dep't , 901 F.2d 696 , 699 (9th Cir. 1990) . On a motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, courts accept as true all well-pleaded allegations of material fact and construes them in a light most favorable to the non-moving party. Manzarek v. St. Paul Fire & Marine Ins. Co. , 519 F.3d 1025 , 1031-32 (9th Cir. 2008) . To survive a motion to dismiss, the complaint "must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to 'state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.'" Ashcroft v. Iqbal , 556 U.S. 662 , 663 , 129 S. Ct. 1937 , 173 L. Ed. 2d 868 , (2009) (quoting Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly , 550 U.S. 544 , 570 , 127 S. Ct. 1955 , 167 L. Ed. 2d 929 (2007) ). A formulaic recitation of the elements of a cause of action will not suffice, and labels and conclusions are insufficient to meet the plaintiff's obligation to provide the grounds of entitlement to relief. Twombly , 550 U.S. at 555 . "Factual allegations must be enough to raise a right to relief above the speculative level." Id. If a complaint cannot be cured by additional factual allegations, dismissal without leave to amend is proper. Id. A court may consider the allegations contained in the pleadings, exhibits attached to or referenced in the complaint, and matters properly subject to judicial notice in ruling on a motion to dismiss. Tellabs, Inc. v. Makor Issues & Rights, Ltd. , 551 U.S. 308 , 322 , 127 S. Ct. 2499 , 168 L. Ed. 2d 179 (2007) ; In re Stac Elec. Sec. Litig. , 89 F.3d 1399 , 1405 n.4 (9th Cir. 1996) U.S. v. Ritchie , 342 F.3d 903 , 908 (9th Cir. 2003) . III. DISCUSSION A. Request for Judicial Notice Defendants request that the Court take judicial notice of the following: 1. Ex. 1 - Certified copies of "the deposits" for Exhibits B and E to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration numbers TXu 2-119-709 and TXu 2-302-694 which are the underlying works for these registrations for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 2. Ex. 2 — deposit for Exhibit C to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration number TXu 2-282-456 which is the underlying work for this registration for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 3. Ex. 3 - deposit for Exhibit D to the Complaint obtained from the United States Copyright Office for registration number TX 9-082-717 which is the underlying work for this registration for Plaintiff's copyright claim; 4. Ex. 4 — "side-by-side comparisons of full biographies" for Barack Obama, Curtis Jackson (50 Cent), Adam Levine, Emma Brooks McAllister, Marko Terzo, N3on, and Angel Reese which include a screenshot of Famous Birthdays' biographies accessed by Defendants and a screenshot of Passes ' biography; 5. Ex. 5 — a redline comparison of the text for each of the biographies for Barack Obama, Curtis Jackson (50 Cent), Adam Levine, Emma Brooks McAllister, Marko Terzo, N3on, and Angel Reese based on contents from the parties' biographies; 6. Ex. 6 — paragraph 7 of the declaration of Patrick Zhang, Defendant Passes ' Chief Technology Officer who declares Defendants' Wiki biography pages are no longer publicly available. Exhibits 1-5 are incorporated by reference in the Complaint. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 6, 21, 49-98, Exs. B-E.) Because the Court may consider Exhibits 1-5 under the incorporation by reference doctrine, the Court denies Defendants' request for judicial notice of Exhibits 1-5 as moot. Exhibit 6, which consists of portions of a declaration from Defendants' Chief Technology Officer, is not incorporated by reference in the Complaint and is not a matter for which the Court can take judicial notice. See Gerritsen v. Warner Bros. Ent. Inc. , 112 F. Supp. 3d 1011 , 1021 (C.D. Cal. 2015) ; Strojnik v. Azul Hosp. Grp. , 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 207424 , 2019 WL 6467494 , at * 3 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 2, 2019) (citing U.S. v. Ritchie , 342 F.3d 903 , 907-08 (9th Cir. 2003) ). Therefore, the Court denies Defendants' request to take judicial notice of Ex. 6. B. Copyright Infringement To state a claim for copyright infringement claim, Plaintiff must allege it (1) "owns a valid copyright in [the work]" and (2) Defendants "copied protected aspects of [Plaintiff's work]." Rentmeester v. Nike, Inc. , 883 F.3d 1111 , 1116-17 (9th Cir. 2018) (citations omitted). The second element has two distinct components: "copying" and "unlawful appropriation." Id. "When the plaintiff lacks direct evidence of copying, [it] can attempt to prove it circumstantially by showing that the defendant had access to the plaintiff's work and that the two works share similarities probative of copying." Id. at 1117 . "Such proof creates a presumption of copying, which the defendant can then attempt to rebut by proving independent creation." Id. "To prove unlawful appropriation, . . . the similarities between the two works must be substantial and they must involve protected elements of the plaintiff's work." Id. "[W]hether works are substantially similar involves a two-part analysis consisting of the extrinsic test and the intrinsic test." Id. at 1118 (internal quotations and citations omitted). A plaintiff must satisfy both components, and therefore a lack of extrinsic similarity is fatal to a plaintiff's copyright case as a matter of law. White v. Twentieth Century Fox Corp. , 572 F. App'x 475 , 476-77 (9th Cir. 2014) (citing Funky Films, Inc. , 462 F.3d at 1081 ). 1. Ownership and Pre-Registration Defendants contend the Complaint does not allege the biographies referenced therein are protected by the alleged copyright registrations, and therefore Plaintiff fails to allege it owns a valid copyright in the works. 17 U.S.C. § 411(a) provides "no civil action for infringement of the copyright in any United States work shall be instituted until preregistration or registration of the copyright claim has been made in accordance with this title." (Emphasis added.) "[R]egistration ... has been made" for purposes of 17 U.S.C. § 411 "and a copyright owner may sue for infringement, when the Copyright Office registers a copyright." Fourth Est. Pub. Benefit Corp. v. Wall-Street.com, LLC , 586 U.S. 296 , 302 , 139 S. Ct. 881 , 203 L. Ed. 2d 147 (2019) . Therefore, "[b]efore pursuing an infringement claim in court, . . . a copyright claimant generally must comply with § 411(a) 's requirement that 'registration of the copyright claim has been made'" because "registration is akin to an administrative exhaustion requirement that the owner must satisfy before suing to enforce ownership rights." Id. at 301 . Here, Plaintiff fails to allege preregistration of the biographies for Marko Terzo and Angel Reese which are alleged in the Complaint prior to filing this lawsuit for copyright infringement. Therefore, Plaintiff's copyright claim cannot be based on infringement of those biographies. However, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff registered other biographies prior to filing suit and alleges there are over 100,000 examples of Defendants' infringement of Plaintiff's registered biographies ( see Compl. ¶¶ 21, 22, 47-48). Accordingly, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges preregistration and ownership of its copyrighted biographies which are the basis of its copyright infringement claim. See Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 15, 2022) . 2. Authorization under the Parties' Agreement Defendants also argue Plaintiff's copyright claim fails as a matter of law because Defendants' use and display of Plaintiff's biographies are authorized under the parties' agreement. However, the Complaint alleges "Famous Birthdays never authorized Defendants to make copies or publicly display any of the works, whether through a license or otherwise." (Compl. ¶ 128.) Defendants also contend Section 4.1 of the parties' agreement expressly permitted "non-public, internal use of [Plaintiff's] data for Business Purposes of [ Passes ]." ( Id. Ex. A, § 4.1.) However, Section 4.1 of the parties' agreement provides "[t]he data and information made available through the Services (the 'Data') are the intellectual property of Famous Birthdays" and "Customer shall not transfer, sell, or publicly display the data." ( Id. ) Therefore, accepting the Complaint's allegations as true, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges facts that Defendants' use and display of Plaintiff's biographies were not authorized under the parties' agreement. 3. Factual Compilations or Original Prose Defendants also contend Plaintiff's copyright claim fails as a matter of law because facts are not subject to copyright protection and Defendants' biographies are not derivative, and Defendants' biographies are not virtually identical nor substantially similar to Plaintiff's biographies as required for Plaintiff's biographies to be protected as a factual compilation. "[A]ll facts—scientific, historical, biographical, and news of the day . . . may not be copyrighted and are part of the public domain available to every person." Feist Publications, Inc. v. Rural Tel. Serv. Co. , 499 U.S. 340 , 348 , 111 S. Ct. 1282 , 113 L. Ed. 2d 358 (1991) (citation omitted). However, "compilations of facts generally are" copyrightable. Id. at 344 . Here, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff's biographies are drafted by its staff and are not copied or automated, Plaintiff's staff use their "expertise" in drafting the biographies including determining what details are salient, which are fun, what can be omitted, what should be highlighted, and writing it in a way that is approachable, entertaining, and snappy" (Compl. ¶¶ 19, 20). Therefore, the alleged methods used in drafting Plaintiff's biographies "do not entail simple replication of the data that [plaintiff] receives" and "does not include the entire relevant universe known to it" and therefore Plaintiff's biographies are "entitled to limited protection" because the biographies are "copyrightable as compilations" since Plaintiff's employees "use their judgment in selecting which [facts] to include" and "exclude information they deem irrelevant to the interests of" Plaintiff's readers which is sufficient to allege "at least minimal creativity." Experian Info. Sols., Inc. v. Nationwide Mktg. Servs. Inc. , 893 F.3d 1176 , 1185 (9th Cir. 2018) ; see also Harper & Row Publishers, Inc. v. Nation Enters. , 471 U.S. 539 , 547 , 105 S. Ct. 2218 , 85 L. Ed. 2d 588 (1985) ; Advanta-STAR Auto. Rsch. Corp. of Am. v. Search Optics, LLC , 672 F. Supp. 3d 1035 , 1048 ( S.D. Cal. 2023 ) . As to Defendants' contention that Plaintiff cannot prevail on its copyright claim because Defendants' biographies "are not virtually identical to Plaintiff's biographies," Defendants argues Plaintiff fails to satisfy the virtual identity requirement for its copyright claim because the Complaint alleges fewer than 80% of the works in Plaintiff's factual compilation were allegedly infringed by Defendants, citing to paragraphs 20 and 38 of the Complaint. However, paragraphs 20 and 38 do not contain such allegations. ( See Compl. ¶ 20 (alleging Plaintiff's process for drafting its biographies); id. ¶ 38 ("To accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile. The script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies. Forensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024.").) Moreover, the Complaint does not allege the entire content of all of the parties' biographies, and neither Plaintiff's Complaint nor Defendants' request for judicial notice attaches all of the biographies at issue in this lawsuit. Accordingly, the Court cannot determine at the motion to dismiss stage as a matter of law whether Defendants' biographies are "virtually identical" to Plaintiff's biographies so as to infringe on Plaintiff's copyrighted works. See Experian , 893 F.3d at 1186 . However, the Complaint alleges "[t]he biographies that [Defendant] displays in its website are virtually identical to [Plaintiff's] copyrighted Works." (Compl. ¶ 43.) The Court must accept this allegation as true at the pleading stage, and thus finds the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants' biographies are virtually identical to Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies. See also Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 15, 2022) . 4. Copying Contrary to Defendants contention, the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants copied Plaintiff's copyrighted works. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 124-26.) 5. Substantial Similarity Defendants also contend the parties' biographies are not substantially similar as a matter of law. Because the Court does not have all of Plaintiff's copyrighted biographies and all of Defendants' allegedly infringing biographies, the Court cannot determine at this stage whether the parties' works are substantially similarity under the extrinsic test. See Zindel as Tr. for David Zindel Tr. v. Fox Searchlight Pictures, Inc. , 815 F. App'x 158 , 159 (9th Cir. 2020) . 6. Fair Use Defendants contend Plaintiff's copyright claim fails because Defendants' biographies are protected under the fair use doctrine. Whether a use constitutes fair use is a mixed question of law and fact. See Harper & Row, Publishers, Inc. v. Nation Enters. , 471 U.S. 539 , 560 , 105 S. Ct. 2218 , 85 L. Ed. 2d 588 (1985) ; Leadsinger, Inc. v. BMG Music Pub. , 512 F.3d 522 , 530 (9th Cir. 2008) . Therefore, in order to conduct a fair use analysis, the court "usually must make factual findings, or rely on undisputed or admitted material facts." Browne v. McCain , 611 F. Supp. 2d 1073 , 1078 (C.D. Cal. 2009) (citing Harper & Row Publishers, Inc. , 471 U.S. at 560 ). Here, the case is at the pleading stage and Plaintiff has not had the opportunity to conduct discovery, and may be unaware of material facts relevant to Defendants' fair use defense. Therefore, an assessment of Defendants' fair use defense at the pleading stage is premature. See Esi Grp. v. Wave Six, LLC , 2018 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 227699 , 2018 WL 6588524 , at * 5 (S.D. Cal. Sept. 25, 2018) ; Auto. Data Sols., Inc. v. Directed Elecs. Canada, Inc. , 2018 WL 4742289 , at * 7 (C.D. Cal. Aug. 15, 2018) ; Browne , 611 F. Supp. 2d at 1078 ; XpandOrtho, Inc. v. Zimmer Biomet Holdings, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 46698 , 2022 WL 801743 , at * 21 ( S.D. Cal. Mar. 15 , 2022) . 7. Reproduction Rights Plaintiff argues Defendants fail to address Plaintiff's other theories for Defendants' copyright liability in addition to Defendants' alleged public display of Plaintiff's work, including Defendants' violation of Plaintiff's exclusive right to reproduce its copyrighted work in copies by transferring Plaintiff's works from one device or server to another. 17 U.S.C. § 106(1) provides that a copyright owner has the "exclusive right . . . to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies." Defendants argue the Complaint contains no allegations that Defendants breached the parties' agreement by transferring data to ChatGPT. However, the Complaint alleges: Passes had substantial access to Famous Birthdays' copyrighted Works through Famous Birthdays' website—data obtained through the parties' historical licensing agreement—which Passes used to "reproduce the copyrighted work in copies" in violation of 17 U.S.C. § 106(1) . As revealed through the direct comparisons between the Works and Passes ' stolen biographies, as well as the secondary evidence of direct copying of URLs and Guo's direct access of over 100,000 profiles, Passes copied and used, and is copying and using, Famous Birthdays' Works publicly, as well as within its platform available to subscribing customers, and internally. (Compl. ¶ 124.) Therefore, taking the allegations in the Complaint as true, Plaintiff sufficiently alleges Defendants violated Plaintiff's exclusive right to reproduction of its copyrighted works in copies to state a claim for copyright infringement. See Disney Enters., Inc. v. VidAngel, Inc. , 224 F.Supp.3d 957 , 969-70 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 12, 2016) . * * * Accordingly, the Court denies Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's copyright infringement claim. C. California Comprehensive Computer Data Access and Fraud Act, Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) The Complaint alleges Defendants violated the California Comprehensive Computer Data Access and Fraud Act ("CDAFA"), Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) . Plaintiff contends his CDAFA claim is brought for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) , (8) . Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) provides: Except as provided in subdivision (h) , any person who commits any of the following acts is guilty of a public offense: (1) Knowingly accesses and without permission alters, damages, deletes, destroys, or otherwise uses any data, computer, computer system, or computer network in order to either (A) devise or execute any scheme or artifice to defraud, deceive, or extort, or (B) wrongfully control or obtain money, property, or data. (2) Knowingly accesses and without permission takes, copies, or makes use of any data from a computer, computer system, or computer network, or takes or copies any supporting documentation, whether existing or residing internal or external to a computer, computer system, or computer network. . . . (4) Knowingly accesses and without permission adds, alters, damages, deletes, or destroys any data, computer software, or computer programs which reside or exist internal or external to a computer, computer system, or computer network. . . . (6) Knowingly and without permission provides or assists in providing a means of accessing a computer, computer system, or computer network in violation of this section. (7) Knowingly and without permission accesses or causes to be accessed any computer, computer system, or computer network. (8) Knowingly introduces any computer contaminant into any computer, computer system, or computer network. 1. Standing Defendants contend Plaintiff lacks standing to bring a § 502 claim under the CDAFA because Plaintiff has not alleged it suffered harm from Defendants' alleged violations. To bring a civil action under Cal. Penal Code § 502 for compensatory damages or injunctive relief, as Plaintiff does here, Plaintiff must suffer damage or loss as a result of a violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502 . See Cal. Pen. Code § 502(e) ; Perkins v. LinkedIn Corp. , 53 F. Supp. 3d 1190 , 1219 (N.D. Cal. 2014) . Construing the allegations in the light most favorable to Plaintiff, the Court finds the Complaint alleges sufficient facts that Plaintiff suffered damage or loss as a result of Defendants' alleged conduct in violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) . ( See Compl. ¶¶ 38, 114, 118.) See Mintz v. Mark Bartelstein & Assocs. Inc. , 906 F. Supp. 2d 1017 , 1032 (C.D. Cal. 2012) ; Facebook, Inc. v. Power Ventures, Inc. , 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 93517 , 2010 WL 3291750 , at * 4 (N.D. Cal. 2010) ; Copart, Inc. v. Sparta Consulting, Inc. , 277 F. Supp. 3d 1127 , 1162 (E.D. Cal. 2017) ; PerkinElmer Health Scis., Inc. v. Salem , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 33529 , 2022 WL 1592590 , at * 3 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 24, 2022) . 2. Particularity Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to plead its § 502 claim under the CDAFA with particularity as required under Fed. R. Civ. P. 9(b) , relying on paragraphs 135 and 142 of the Complaint. Paragraph 135 of the Complaint quotes Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1) which provides it is a violation of the CDAFA if the defendant "(1) Knowingly accesses and without permission alters, damages, deletes, destroys, or otherwise uses any data, computer, computer system, or computer network in order to either (A) devise or execute any scheme or artifice to defraud, deceive, or extort, or (B) wrongfully control or obtain money, property, or data." (Compl. ¶ 135 (quoting Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1) ).) Paragraph 142 of the Complaint, which alleges "Defendants are liable for compensatory, consequential, and incidental damages. Defendants are also liable for exemplary and punitive damages in an amount sufficient to set an example of Defendants' oppressive, fraudulent, and malicious conduct under § 502(e)(4) ," pertains to recovery of punitive damages under § 502(e)(4) . Because Plaintiff's CDAFA claim does not sound in fraud, Fed. R. Civ. P. 9 's heightened pleading standard does not apply. Even if Rule 9(b) 's heightened pleading standard did apply, the Court finds Plaintiff pleads its CDAFA claim with sufficient particularity because the Complaint alleges "Guo ran 106,124 lookups in a month, a blatant breach of the License Agreement," "[t]o accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies," and "[f]orensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024." (Compl. ¶¶ 37, 38.) The Complaint thus alleges the who, what, where, and when of Defendants' alleged conduct to give Defendants sufficient notice of the basis for Plaintiff's claim, and therefore satisfies Rule 9 's heightened pleading standard. 3. Without Permission Subsections 1 , 2 , 4 , 6 and 7 of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c) each include a "knowingly accesses" and "without permission" requirement. Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to allege Defendants acted "without permission" as required for its § 502 claim under the CDAFA because the parties entered into a services agreement authorizing Defendants to access Plaintiff's data, and taking permitted data using an impermissible method does not violate the CDAFA. Plaintiff argues the fact that Defendants had access to Plaintiff's data based on the parties' services agreement does not save them from liability under § 502 because Defendants "exceeded their privileges" under the parties' agreement, the parties' service agreement gave Defendants individualized notice that Plaintiff's data was protected, and the agreement and Plaintiff's terms of service combined create liability for Defendants' actions under § 502 . Here, the Complaint alleges Plaintiff "enters into licensing agreements with companies . . . through its service known as Famous Birthdays Pro" which "allows some paying clients access to [Plaintiff's] API for limited, research purposes" (Compl. ¶ 26), Defendant Passes entered into a limited services agreement with Plaintiff on March 1, 2025 "which granted Passes access to Famous Birthdays Pro," and the agreement granted Defendant Passes a license "to make non-public, internal use of the data." ( Id. ¶¶ 26, 34, 36, Ex. A.) The Service Agreement entered into between the parties attached to the Complaint provided Defendant Passes with Application Programming Interface ("API") access to Plaintiff's data, and permitted Defendant Passes to conduct "2,5000 searches per month of historical rank graphs for creators profiled on the Famous Birthdays platform," "100 Report Searches per month with up to 50 records on each report," and "access to Famous Birthday's Pro API: Which includes the same data and allotments that are available via the Famous Birthdays Pro dashboard." ( Id. Ex. A.) The Services Agreement further stated: The data and information made available through the Services (the "Data") are the intellectual property of Famous Birthdays. Famous Birthdays hereby grants Customer a license to make non-public, internal use of the data for Business Purposes of the Customer for the duration of the term of this Agreement. Customer shall not transfer, sell, or publicly display the data. Notwithstanding the forgoing, Customer may use the data in a manner that would otherwise be restricted by this Agreement where such Data is, through no fault of Customer's, unprotectable under applicable law. ( Id. ) The Complaint alleges: 1. "Notwithstanding the term in the Services Agreement limiting Passes to 2,500 lookups per month to view historical rank graphs for creators profiled on Famous Birthdays, [Plaintiff's] internal dashboard reflects that [Defendant] Guo ran 106,124 lookups in a month, a blatant breach of the License Agreement," "[t]o accomplish this severe breach, Passes built and ran a script that pinged the [Plaintiff's] API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though [Defendant] Passes had no right to make copies," and "[f]orensic analysis revealed that each bio was brought up and scraped one by one every 2-4 seconds between April 15, 2024 and April 20, 2024." (Compl. ¶¶ 37-38); 2. "Defendants compiled a URL list and used it and a script, a computer contaminant, to scrape [Plaintiff's] Works. Defendants knew that they were wrongfully using [Plaintiff's] Works without permission, because, among other things, Defendants exceeded the scope of the Services Agreement and sought to hide their misconduct by employing the services of a chatbot," and "Defendants knowingly accessed Famous Birthdays' computer networks and API, and then used a computer, computer system, computer network, or data (their script) to wrongfully obtain and control [Plaintiff's] data, in violation of the Services Agreement and [Plaintiff's] Terms of Service." ( Id. ¶¶ 136-37.) Because the Complaint alleges facts demonstrating Defendants had access to Plaintiff's data based on the parties' services agreement, and because Plaintiff's CDAFA claim under Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) is based on Defendants' improper use of the data that Plaintiff alleges it gave Defendants access to, Plaintiff fails to state a claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) . See Oracle USA, Inc. v. Rimini St., Inc. , 879 F.3d 948 , 962 (9th Cir. 2018) , rev'd on other grounds in 586 U.S. 334 , 139 S. Ct. 873 , 203 L. Ed. 2d 180 (2019) ; Van Buren v. United States , 593 U.S. 374 , 395-96 , 141 S. Ct. 1648 , 210 L. Ed. 2d 26 (2021) ; In re Oliveras , 103 Cal. App. 5th 771 , 782-83 , 323 Cal. Rptr. 3d 303 (Cal. Ct. App. 2024) , as modified on denial of reh'g (Aug. 2, 2024); Custom Packaging Supply, Inc. v. Phillips , 2015 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 164523 , 2015 WL 8334793 , at * 3 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 7, 2015) . The Court therefore grants Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's CDAFA claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(1)-(2) , (4) , (6)-(7) with leave to amend to allege facts that Defendants engaged in the alleged conduct "without permission" such as by circumventing technical or code-based barriers. 4. Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) Plaintiff also asserts Defendants violated Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) of the CDAFA (which does not include a "without permission" requirement). A defendant who "[k]nowingly introduces any computer contaminant into any computer, computer system, or computer network" violates Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) . "Computer contaminant" is defined as "any set of computer instructions that are designed to modify, damage, destroy, record, or transmit information within a computer, computer system, or computer network without the intent or permission of the owner of the information." Cal. Penal Code § 502(b)(12) . Here, Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to plausibly allege a violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) because Plaintiff does not allege facts that Defendants introduced a computer contaminant into Plaintiff's networks. Plaintiff contends that an automated script designed to transmit data contained within a computer system without permission constitutes a computer contaminant. Defendants argue that the automated script identified by Plaintiff is a tool designed by Plaintiff (not Defendants) to access public information, and that Plaintiff has not alleged nor explained what computer instructions Defendants introduced into Plaintiff's systems. The Complaint alleges " Passes, and Guo in particular, used their access to Famous Birthdays' API to create and employ a script to illegally scrape over one hundred thousand of Famous Birthdays' biographies over the course of four days—in gross excess of Passes ' contractual rights," " Passes built and ran a script that pinged the Famous Birthdays API 106,124 times, each time with a unique URL query that would bring up a different profile," "[t]he script would then scrape each bio one by one, even though Passes had no right to make copies," and "by introducing their script into [Plaintiff's] networks and API to steal [Plaintiff's] bios en masse, Defendants also violated § 502(c)(1)(B)(8) ." (Compl. ¶¶ 5, 38, 141.) Construing the allegations in the light most favorable to Plaintiff, the Complaint sufficiently alleges Defendants built and introduced a script into Plaintiff's networks that transmitted Plaintiff's data from its database. Therefore, the Court denies Defendants' Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff's CDAFA claim for violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) . D. Unfair Competition Law, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 Plaintiff's unfair competition claim is predicated on the same alleged conduct alleged for its copyright infringement and CDAFA claims. Because Plaintiff pleads sufficient facts to state a claim for copyright infringement and violation of Cal. Pen. Code § 502(c)(8) the CDAFA, Plaintiff also pleads sufficient facts to state a claim for violation of Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200 . See Famous Birthdays, LLC v. Socialedge, Inc. , 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 92245 , 2022 WL 1591723 , at * 6 ; Fleites v. MindGeek S.A.R.L. , 617 F. Supp. 3d 1146 , 1166 (C.D. Cal. 2022) ; Medina v. Wells Fargo Bank , 2020 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73658 , 2020 WL 1934980 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. Jan. 16, 2020) (citing Wolski v. Fremont Inv. & Loan , 127 Cal. App. 4th 347 , 357 , 25 Cal. Rptr. 3d 500 (Cal. Ct. App. 2005) ). E. Breach of Contract "In order to establish liability on a claim for breach of contract, Plaintiff[ ] must be able to establish that (1) there was a contract, (2) Plaintiff['s] performance or excuse for nonperformance, (3) Defendant's breach, and (4) damage to Plaintiff[] that resulted from Defendant's breach." Valenzuela v. ADT Sec. Servs. Inc. , 820 F. Supp. 2d 1061 , 1073 (C.D. Cal. 2010) (citing Wall Street Network, Ltd. v. New York Times Co. , 164 Cal. App. 4th 1171 , 1178 , 80 Cal. Rptr. 3d 6 (2008) ), aff'd , 475 F. App'x 115 (9th Cir. 2012) . Defendants contend Plaintiff fails to state a claim for breach of contract because Plaintiff has not identified any term of the parties' agreement that Defendants breached, and Plaintiff fails to allege damages from any breach. Here, the Complaint adequately alleges a contract, Plaintiff's performance, Defendants' breach, and damage to Plaintiff resulting from the breach to survive a motion to dismiss. ( See Compl. ¶¶ 34, 38-41, 117-19 ['Copyright', 'Training Data', 'Contract'] ['ADT Sec. Servs. Inc', 'Advanta-STAR Auto. Rsch. Corp', 'Assocs. Inc', 'Bell Atlantic Corp', 'For Famous Birthdays LLC', 'Fourth Est. Pub. Benefit Corp', 'LinkedIn Corp', 'Meta', 'Nationwide Mktg. Servs. Inc', 'OpenAI', 'Oracle', 'Passes Inc', 'Ross Intelligence', 'Twentieth Century Fox Corp', 'Warner Bros. Ent. Inc']
