Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 72120 , 2026 WL 891607 (Apr. 1, 2026) Motion granted by, in part, Motion denied by, in part Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 75001 (N.D. Cal., Apr. 6, 2026) RITA F. LIN ORDER GRANTING IN PART AND DENYING IN PART FREIGHTMATE AND LACAILLADE'S MOTIONS TO DISMISS WITH LEAVE TO AMEND Re: Dkt. Nos. 35, 36, 36-1, 38-2. Plaintiff Flexport, Inc. (" Flexport ") alleges that former employees Yingwei Zhao and Bryan Lacaillade exfiltrated thousands of documents from Flexport to rapidly develop and launch a competing product for their new venture, Freightmate AI, Inc. ("Freightmate"). Flexport brings suit against Zhao, Lacaillade, and Freightmate alleging trade secret misappropriation, copyright infringement, and breach of contract. While Zhao has filed an answer to the Complaint (Dkt. No. 28), Freightmate and Lacaillade now move to dismiss the claims against them for failure to state a claim. (Dkt. Nos. 35, 36.) The motions to dismiss are GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . This order assumes the reader is familiar with the facts, the applicable legal standard, and the arguments made by the parties. I. LACAILLADE'S MOTION TO DISMISS A. Trade Secret Misappropriation Flexport fails to plausibly allege that Lacaillade either directly or indirectly misappropriated Flexport 's trade secrets. To state a claim for trade secret misappropriation, Flexport must allege that: (1) it possessed a trade secret; (2) Lacaillade misappropriated the trade secret; and (3) the misappropriation caused Flexport damage. See InteliClear, LLC v. ETC Glob. Holdings, Inc. , 978 F.3d 653 , 657-59 (9th Cir. 2020) . To plead misappropriation, Flexport must establish one of two categories of conduct: (1) acquisition of the secret by improper means, or (2) disclosure or use of the trade secret without consent. See Apple Inc. v. Rivos, Inc. , No. 5:22-cv-02637-EJD, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 140628 , 2023 WL 5183034 , at * 6 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 11, 2023) (citing 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5)(A)-(B) ). Where, as here, a plaintiff accuses a defendant of indirect misappropriation ( i.e. , that Lacaillade obtained Flexport 's trade secrets from Zhao rather than directly from Flexport ), the plaintiff must allege facts showing that the defendant "knew or had reason to know before the use or disclosure that the information was a trade secret and knew or had reason to know that the disclosing party had acquired it through improper means or was breaching a duty of confidentiality by disclosing it." California Police Activities League v. Cal. Police Youth Charities, Inc. , No. C 08-1991 PJH, 2009 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 20507 , 2009 WL 537091 , at * 3 (N.D. Cal. March 3, 2009) . 1. Direct and Indirect Misappropriation Flexport advances two misappropriation theories based on Lacaillade's alleged use of its trade secrets without consent. Both theories fail. First, Flexport argues that Lacaillade used Flexport trade secrets when he "secretly ran confidential Flexport documents 'through ChatGPT to better understand how generative AI digitizes analog shipping documents.'" (Compl. ¶ 52.) But Flexport has not alleged any facts supporting a plausible inference that these documents contained trade secrets. The Complaint does not identify what these "confidential Flexport documents" are or why they are subject to trade secret protection. Nor does the bare label of confidentiality confer trade secret status. Flexport attempts to characterize the shipping documents as "proprietary datasets . . . that allow Flexport to make and provide data-driven shipping services[.]" (Compl. ¶ 25). But again, there are no allegations characterizing the documents as a dataset, let alone one that is a trade secret. Second, Flexport contends that the Complaint supports a plausible inference that Lacaillade used the source code and other files Zhao allegedly took from Flexport to build Freightmate's product. While Flexport adequately alleges that those documents constitute trade secrets, there is insufficient basis to plausibly infer that Lacaillade used, or even knew about, the documents. As a preliminary matter, Flexport has adequately pled that the Flexport code and other documents allegedly taken by Zhao constituted trade secrets. A plaintiff must "describe the subject matter of the trade secret with sufficient particularity to separate it from matters of general knowledge in the trade or of special persons who are skilled in the trade, and to permit the defendant to ascertain at least the boundaries within which the secret lies." See Space Data Corp. v. X , No. 16-CV03260-BLF, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22571 , 2017 WL 5013363 , at * 2 (N.D. Cal. Feb. 16, 2017) . While some of Flexport 's trade secret allegations are vague, others are sufficiently specific to plausibly allege trade secrets. See, e.g. , Compl. ¶¶ 23, 54 (identifying flexport-master.zip as the file containing Flexport 's trade-secret source code); Compl. ¶¶ 26, 57 (identifying "air-freightscrapers-130524-185844.pdf," " Flexport Dangerous Goods SOP," "BOL Training.pptx," and "Shipment Operations MBR Metrics" as files that contain various business and operational trade secrets); see also WeRide Corp. v. Kun Huang , 379 F. Supp. 3d 834 , 846 (N.D. Cal. 2019) , modified in part , No. 5:18-CV-07233-EJD, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 192212 , 2019 WL 5722620 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 5, 2019) (file names were sufficient to identify alleged trade secrets contained in source code). Flexport, however, does not plausibly allege that Lacaillade—who is not alleged to have taken any Flexport trade secrets himself—used or disclosed the documents Zhao allegedly took. The Complaint only contains conclusory group allegations that "Defendants have used . . . Flexport 's trade secrets," and does not reference any specific trade secrets that Lacaillade accessed or used. ( See, e.g. , Compl. ¶¶ 58, 65, 66.) Instead, Flexport contends that the speed at which Freightmate developed their product and acquired customers was "incredible," supporting a reasonable inference that Lacaillaide used Flexport 's code or other alleged trade secrets while developing the product. (Compl. ¶ 52.) Flexport further alleges that Freightmate established partnerships with "over three times the number of [customers it had] anticipated by [by January 2025]" (Compl. ¶ 58) and launched its initial product about a month-and-a-half after incorporation (Compl. ¶ 52). But while Flexport plausibly alleges that Zhao used the code or other trade secrets while at Freightmate (as discussed further below with respect to Freightmate's motion to dismiss), that is not enough to allege that Lacaillaide himself did so. Nor has Flexport plausibly alleged that Lacaillaide knew or had reason to know that Zhao took Flexport 's trade secrets or used them. Flexport contends that the timing of Zhaos's alleged misappropriation of files—that Zhao allegedly took files in the days immediately following Lacaillade's registration of the Freightmate domain name and incorporation of the Freightmate entity—supports an inference that Lacaillade was aware of Zhao's misappropriation. (Compl. ¶¶ 36, 39, 54.) But those allegations, if true, merely support the inference that Zhao may have been aware of the domain registration and incorporation and decided to take files shortly thereafter, not Lacaillade. 2. Conspiracy Flexport also does not adequately plead liability on a conspiracy theory. To state a cause of action for civil conspiracy, a complaint must allege (1) the formation and operation of a conspiracy; (2) the wrongful act or acts done pursuant thereto; and (3) the damage resulting from such act or acts. Younan v. Equifax, Inc. , 111 Cal. App. 3d 498 , 511 n.9, 169 Cal. Rptr. 478 (1980) . For each element of conspiracy, "[b]are allegations and rank conjecture do not suffice[.]" Lachapelle v. Kim , No. 15-CV-02195-JSC, 2015 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 123859 , 2015 WL 5461542 , at * 7 (N.D. Cal. Sept. 16, 2015) . While Flexport generally alleges that Lacaillade conspired with Zhao to misappropriate trade secrets, Flexport does not specifically allege either the circumstances of the agreement or any action that Lacaillade took in furtherance of the conspiracy. The Complaint only contains conclusory allegations of conspiracy, which are insufficient to establish plausibility. ( See, e.g. , Compl. ¶ 12 ("Plaintiff believes and therefore alleges that Defendants conspired to obtain and exploit Flexport 's proprietary information . . ."); Compl. ¶ 56 ("Defendants conspired to take and took [the Flexport Platform Trade Secrets] . . . to help develop a freight management system that competes with the Flexport Platform."); Compl ¶¶ 57, 65 (" Flexport believes and therefore alleges that Defendants conspired to take and took these materials to avoid the costs involved in researching and developing expertise and experience in navigating the complex global logistics ecosystem . . .").) Flexport argues that a conspiracy can be inferred from the bare allegation that Zhao and Lacaillade agreed to form Freightmate in January 2024 and the registration of a domain name at that time. (Compl. ¶ 36.) But such an allegation is just as conclusory as the other allegations rejected as insufficient above. Moreover, even if a reasonable inference can be drawn from those allegations that Zhao and Lacaillade were considering the formation of Freightmate in January when they were still employed at Flexport, that is hardly a basis to infer that that Zhao and Lacaillade entered an agreement to misappropriate trade secrets from Flexport . Therefore, the motion to dismiss the trade secret misappropriation claims against Lacaillade is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . B. Copyright Infringement Flexport does not plausibly allege that Lacaillade infringed its copyrighted source code. Proof of copyright infringement requires a plaintiff to show that (1) they own a valid copyright in the work, and (2) the defendant copied protected aspects of the work. Skidmore as Tr. for Randy Craig Wolfe Trust v. Led Zeppelin , 952 F.3d 1051 , 1064 (9th Cir. 2020) (en banc). Secondary liability exists for copyright infringement either by "intentionally inducing or encouraging direct infringement" (contributory infringement), or "by profiting from direct infringement while declining to exercise a right to stop or limit it" (vicarious infringement). Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer Studios, Inc. v. Grokster, Ltd. , 545 U.S. 913 , 930 , 125 S. Ct. 2764 , 162 L. Ed. 2d 781 (2005) (internal citations omitted). Flexport does not dispute that Lacaillade did not directly copy the source code. Instead, Flexport argues the allegations support an inference that Lacaillade induced and/or authorized Zhao's copying of Flexport 's source code. As discussed above, the Complaint does not support an inference that Lacaillade entered into an agreement with Zhao to take Flexport 's documents or that Lacaillade controlled Zhao's actions. Flexport further asserts that Lacaillade, as Freightmate's CEO, had a duty to police Zhao's actions, and thus is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged copying. The Complaint, however, does not allege any facts from which it could be inferred that Lacaillade controlled Zhao's copying of the source code, which is alleged to have occurred prior to Zhao's employment at Freightmate, as detailed below. Therefore, the motion to dismiss the copyright infringement claims against Lacaillade is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . C. Breach of Contract Flexport has adequately alleged that Lacaillade breached his obligation under Flexport 's Employee Inventions & Proprietary Information Agreement ("Agreement") to "hold in strict confidence and not directly or indirectly disclose or use any Proprietary Information, except as required within the scope of [his] employment." (Compl. ¶ 88.) While Flexport fails to sufficiently allege that Lacaillade disclosed or used any of Flexport 's trade secrets or copyrighted code, Flexport has plausibly pled that the "confidential" shipping documents Lacaillade allegedly fed into ChatGPT constituted Flexport 's "Proprietary Information." Therefore, the motion to dismiss the breach of contract claim against Lacaillade is DENIED . II. FREIGHTMATE'S MOTION TO DISMISS A. Trade Secret Misappropriation While Flexport 's direct misappropriation theory against Freightmate fails for the same reason as for Lacaillaide, Flexport has plausibly pled that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's misappropriation of trade secrets. Specifically, although Flexport does not adequately plead vicarious liability for Zhao's initial acquisition of trade secrets, Flexport has adequately pled facts supporting a plausible inference that Zhao used those trade secrets after he started working at Freightmate. Freightmate's motion to dismiss the trade secret misappropriation claim is therefore DENIED . Flexport has not adequately alleged that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged acquisition of the trade secrets, because that conduct occurred prior to Zhao's employment at Freightmate. "[T]he future employer of a person who steals a company's trade secrets for the benefit of the future employer is [not] liable under respondeat superior, absent an allegation the future employer subsequently used the trade secrets or the wrongdoer used the trade secrets once he actually became employed." Alert Enter., Inc. v. Rana , No. 22-CV-06646-JSC, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44590 , 2023 WL 2541353 , at * 3 (N.D. Cal. Mar. 16, 2023) . Here, there are no facts in the Complaint from which it can be inferred that Zhao was an employee or agent of Freightmate as of May 30, 2024, which is the date of Zhao's last alleged download. (Compl. ¶ 54.) Flexport conclusorily alleges that Zhao "co-founded" Freightmate. (Compl. ¶ 40.) But Freightmate's Certificate of Incorporation shows that Lacaillade was its sole incorporator on May 13, 2024. (Dkt. No. 36-1 at 9.) This also contradicts Flexport 's allegations that both Lacaillade and Zhao incorporated Freightmate in Delaware (Compl. ¶ 46), though it appears Flexport no longer seeks to advance this argument. ( See Dkt. No. 39 at 7 (stating that "Lacaillade . . . formally incorporated Freightmate[.]).) In response, Flexport asks this Court to take judicial notice of Freightmate's Foreign Registration Statement filed with State of Washington , which denotes that Freightmate began doing business in Washington on May 29, 2024, and lists Zhao as a governor of Freightmate. (Dkt. No. 38-3 at 4-5). However, even if this document were admissible as a public record and sufficiently establishes Zhao's employee or agent status at the time of the alleged misappropriation, a plaintiff may not supplement its allegations via judicial notice. M. B. v. City & Cnty. of San Francisco , No. 24-CV-00301-RFL, 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 162028 , 2024 WL 4049889 , at * 1 (N.D. Cal. Sept. 3, 2024) . Flexport 's request for judicial notice is therefore denied. However, Flexport has adequately pled that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged use of the trade secrets after he had joined Freightmate. An employer is vicariously liable for misappropriation when it is committed "within the scope of employment [and] at least in part[] to benefit the employer, though the employer may forbid it." Brain Injury Ass'n of Cal. v. Yari , No. 19-CV-5912, 2020 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 120201 , 2020 WL 3643482 , at * 5 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 30, 2020) . Drawing all reasonable inferences in Flexport 's favor, Flexport has plausibly alleged that Zhao used the trade secret files in the course of his employment at Freightmate, and thus that Freightmate is vicariously liable for that conduct. The Complaint alleges that Zhao—a Principal Technical Program Manager at Flexport working on the Flexport Platform—had saved to his local computer over 70,000 confidential Flexport documents in the months leading up to his departure from Flexport, many of which plausibly contained trade secret information. (Compl. ¶ 38). The timing of Zhao's alleged downloads, volume of downloaded documents, Zhao's role as co-founder and COO, and Freightmate's rapid launch of a competing product containing functionality similar to the allegedly stolen source code support a plausible inference that Zhao took the files with the intention to use them to benefit Freightmate, and actually did so. See Neutron Holdings, Inc. v. Hertz Corp. , No. 23-CV-00934-JSC, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100085 , 2023 WL 3919465 , at * 5 (N.D. Cal. June 8, 2023) (finding vicarious liability for employee's use on similar facts). At least one of the files Zhao allegedly took, the Flexport Platform source code, includes functionality that was also offered by Freightmate. ( Compare Compl. ¶ 19 ( Flexport Platform includes features for ingestion of data), with Compl. ¶ 57 (Freightmate offers a document ingestion tool called "Docmate").) Apple Inc. v. Rivos, Inc. , No. 5:22-CV-02637-EJD, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 140628 , 2023 WL 5183034 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 11, 2023) , does not counsel otherwise. Zhao's conduct is more analogous to that of the individual defendants in Rivos —specifically, Wen, Rajamani, and Hardage—who were found to have plausibly threatened misappropriation than those whose conduct amounted to mere possession of confidential materials. Like Zhao, those three defendants were alleged to have downloaded substantial volumes of proprietary information shortly before departing Apple to join a direct competitor in a similar role. See id. at *6 . The Rivos court reasoned that "it would be unreasonable to require a plaintiff to demonstrate the precise ways in which Defendants may have used [plaintiff's] trade secrets, given that Defendants are the only ones who possess such information" and "discovery has not yet commenced." Id. The court, however, declined to infer misappropriation as to the other individuals—Pinot and Ye—who merely retained access to Apple files on regularly synced cloud storage without any accompanying allegations of recent exfiltration. Id. at *7 . By contrast, Zhao's conduct went far beyond "mere possession" for the reasons explained above. B. Copyright Infringement Flexport fails to adequately allege that Freightmate infringed its copyrights. Flexport does not allege that Freightmate directly copied its code. Instead, Flexport asserts that Freightmate is liable for the copyright infringement committed by Zhao. As discussed above, however, Flexport has not sufficiently alleged that Zhao made infringing copies of Flexport Platform Code while employed at Freightmate. Flexport 's argument that Freightmate should have policed Zhao and stopped him from downloading the copyrighted source code also fails for the same reason. Therefore, the motion to dismiss the copyright infringement claim against Freightmate is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . III. CONCLUSION Based on the foregoing, the motion to dismiss is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART as set forth above. Freightmate's request for judicial notice (Dkt. No. 36-1) is GRANTED , and Flexport 's request for judicial notice (Dkt. No. 38-2) is DENIED . If Flexport wishes to file a Second Amended Complaint correcting the deficiencies identified above, it shall do so by August 8, 2025. Flexport may not add new claims or parties without leave of the Court or stipulation by the parties pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 15 . If no such amended complaint is filed by that date, the claims that were dismissed in this Order will remain dismissed, and the case will proceed solely on the remaining claims, including the ones Defendants did not move to dismiss. IT IS SO ORDERED . Dated: RITA F. LIN United States District Judge Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 161200 FLEXPORT, INC. , Plaintiff, v. FREIGHTMATE AI, INC., et al., Defendants. Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc. Request denied by Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 21561 , 2026 WL 266453 (Feb. 2, 2026) Later proceeding at Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51700 (Mar. 11, 2026) Motion denied by Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 72120 , 2026 WL 891607 (Apr. 1, 2026) Motion granted by, in part, Motion denied by, in part Flexport, Inc. v. Freightmate AI, Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 75001 (N.D. Cal., Apr. 6, 2026) RITA F. LIN ORDER GRANTING IN PART AND DENYING IN PART FREIGHTMATE AND LACAILLADE'S MOTIONS TO DISMISS WITH LEAVE TO AMEND Re: Dkt. Nos. 35, 36, 36-1, 38-2. Plaintiff Flexport, Inc. (" Flexport ") alleges that former employees Yingwei Zhao and Bryan Lacaillade exfiltrated thousands of documents from Flexport to rapidly develop and launch a competing product for their new venture, Freightmate AI, Inc. ("Freightmate"). Flexport brings suit against Zhao, Lacaillade, and Freightmate alleging trade secret misappropriation, copyright infringement, and breach of contract. While Zhao has filed an answer to the Complaint (Dkt. No. 28), Freightmate and Lacaillade now move to dismiss the claims against them for failure to state a claim. (Dkt. Nos. 35, 36.) The motions to dismiss are GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . This order assumes the reader is familiar with the facts, the applicable legal standard, and the arguments made by the parties. I. LACAILLADE'S MOTION TO DISMISS A. Trade Secret Misappropriation Flexport fails to plausibly allege that Lacaillade either directly or indirectly misappropriated Flexport 's trade secrets. To state a claim for trade secret misappropriation, Flexport must allege that: (1) it possessed a trade secret; (2) Lacaillade misappropriated the trade secret; and (3) the misappropriation caused Flexport damage. See InteliClear, LLC v. ETC Glob. Holdings, Inc. , 978 F.3d 653 , 657-59 (9th Cir. 2020) . To plead misappropriation, Flexport must establish one of two categories of conduct: (1) acquisition of the secret by improper means, or (2) disclosure or use of the trade secret without consent. See Apple Inc. v. Rivos, Inc. , No. 5:22-cv-02637-EJD, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 140628 , 2023 WL 5183034 , at * 6 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 11, 2023) (citing 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5)(A)-(B) ). Where, as here, a plaintiff accuses a defendant of indirect misappropriation ( i.e. , that Lacaillade obtained Flexport 's trade secrets from Zhao rather than directly from Flexport ), the plaintiff must allege facts showing that the defendant "knew or had reason to know before the use or disclosure that the information was a trade secret and knew or had reason to know that the disclosing party had acquired it through improper means or was breaching a duty of confidentiality by disclosing it." California Police Activities League v. Cal. Police Youth Charities, Inc. , No. C 08-1991 PJH, 2009 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 20507 , 2009 WL 537091 , at * 3 (N.D. Cal. March 3, 2009) . 1. Direct and Indirect Misappropriation Flexport advances two misappropriation theories based on Lacaillade's alleged use of its trade secrets without consent. Both theories fail. First, Flexport argues that Lacaillade used Flexport trade secrets when he "secretly ran confidential Flexport documents 'through ChatGPT to better understand how generative AI digitizes analog shipping documents.'" (Compl. ¶ 52.) But Flexport has not alleged any facts supporting a plausible inference that these documents contained trade secrets. The Complaint does not identify what these "confidential Flexport documents" are or why they are subject to trade secret protection. Nor does the bare label of confidentiality confer trade secret status. Flexport attempts to characterize the shipping documents as "proprietary datasets . . . that allow Flexport to make and provide data-driven shipping services[.]" (Compl. ¶ 25). But again, there are no allegations characterizing the documents as a dataset, let alone one that is a trade secret. Second, Flexport contends that the Complaint supports a plausible inference that Lacaillade used the source code and other files Zhao allegedly took from Flexport to build Freightmate's product. While Flexport adequately alleges that those documents constitute trade secrets, there is insufficient basis to plausibly infer that Lacaillade used, or even knew about, the documents. As a preliminary matter, Flexport has adequately pled that the Flexport code and other documents allegedly taken by Zhao constituted trade secrets. A plaintiff must "describe the subject matter of the trade secret with sufficient particularity to separate it from matters of general knowledge in the trade or of special persons who are skilled in the trade, and to permit the defendant to ascertain at least the boundaries within which the secret lies." See Space Data Corp. v. X , No. 16-CV03260-BLF, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 22571 , 2017 WL 5013363 , at * 2 (N.D. Cal. Feb. 16, 2017) . While some of Flexport 's trade secret allegations are vague, others are sufficiently specific to plausibly allege trade secrets. See, e.g. , Compl. ¶¶ 23, 54 (identifying flexport-master.zip as the file containing Flexport 's trade-secret source code); Compl. ¶¶ 26, 57 (identifying "air-freightscrapers-130524-185844.pdf," " Flexport Dangerous Goods SOP," "BOL Training.pptx," and "Shipment Operations MBR Metrics" as files that contain various business and operational trade secrets); see also WeRide Corp. v. Kun Huang , 379 F. Supp. 3d 834 , 846 (N.D. Cal. 2019) , modified in part , No. 5:18-CV-07233-EJD, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 192212 , 2019 WL 5722620 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 5, 2019) (file names were sufficient to identify alleged trade secrets contained in source code). Flexport, however, does not plausibly allege that Lacaillade—who is not alleged to have taken any Flexport trade secrets himself—used or disclosed the documents Zhao allegedly took. The Complaint only contains conclusory group allegations that "Defendants have used . . . Flexport 's trade secrets," and does not reference any specific trade secrets that Lacaillade accessed or used. ( See, e.g. , Compl. ¶¶ 58, 65, 66.) Instead, Flexport contends that the speed at which Freightmate developed their product and acquired customers was "incredible," supporting a reasonable inference that Lacaillaide used Flexport 's code or other alleged trade secrets while developing the product. (Compl. ¶ 52.) Flexport further alleges that Freightmate established partnerships with "over three times the number of [customers it had] anticipated by [by January 2025]" (Compl. ¶ 58) and launched its initial product about a month-and-a-half after incorporation (Compl. ¶ 52). But while Flexport plausibly alleges that Zhao used the code or other trade secrets while at Freightmate (as discussed further below with respect to Freightmate's motion to dismiss), that is not enough to allege that Lacaillaide himself did so. Nor has Flexport plausibly alleged that Lacaillaide knew or had reason to know that Zhao took Flexport 's trade secrets or used them. Flexport contends that the timing of Zhaos's alleged misappropriation of files—that Zhao allegedly took files in the days immediately following Lacaillade's registration of the Freightmate domain name and incorporation of the Freightmate entity—supports an inference that Lacaillade was aware of Zhao's misappropriation. (Compl. ¶¶ 36, 39, 54.) But those allegations, if true, merely support the inference that Zhao may have been aware of the domain registration and incorporation and decided to take files shortly thereafter, not Lacaillade. 2. Conspiracy Flexport also does not adequately plead liability on a conspiracy theory. To state a cause of action for civil conspiracy, a complaint must allege (1) the formation and operation of a conspiracy; (2) the wrongful act or acts done pursuant thereto; and (3) the damage resulting from such act or acts. Younan v. Equifax, Inc. , 111 Cal. App. 3d 498 , 511 n.9, 169 Cal. Rptr. 478 (1980) . For each element of conspiracy, "[b]are allegations and rank conjecture do not suffice[.]" Lachapelle v. Kim , No. 15-CV-02195-JSC, 2015 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 123859 , 2015 WL 5461542 , at * 7 (N.D. Cal. Sept. 16, 2015) . While Flexport generally alleges that Lacaillade conspired with Zhao to misappropriate trade secrets, Flexport does not specifically allege either the circumstances of the agreement or any action that Lacaillade took in furtherance of the conspiracy. The Complaint only contains conclusory allegations of conspiracy, which are insufficient to establish plausibility. ( See, e.g. , Compl. ¶ 12 ("Plaintiff believes and therefore alleges that Defendants conspired to obtain and exploit Flexport 's proprietary information . . ."); Compl. ¶ 56 ("Defendants conspired to take and took [the Flexport Platform Trade Secrets] . . . to help develop a freight management system that competes with the Flexport Platform."); Compl ¶¶ 57, 65 (" Flexport believes and therefore alleges that Defendants conspired to take and took these materials to avoid the costs involved in researching and developing expertise and experience in navigating the complex global logistics ecosystem . . .").) Flexport argues that a conspiracy can be inferred from the bare allegation that Zhao and Lacaillade agreed to form Freightmate in January 2024 and the registration of a domain name at that time. (Compl. ¶ 36.) But such an allegation is just as conclusory as the other allegations rejected as insufficient above. Moreover, even if a reasonable inference can be drawn from those allegations that Zhao and Lacaillade were considering the formation of Freightmate in January when they were still employed at Flexport, that is hardly a basis to infer that that Zhao and Lacaillade entered an agreement to misappropriate trade secrets from Flexport . Therefore, the motion to dismiss the trade secret misappropriation claims against Lacaillade is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . B. Copyright Infringement Flexport does not plausibly allege that Lacaillade infringed its copyrighted source code. Proof of copyright infringement requires a plaintiff to show that (1) they own a valid copyright in the work, and (2) the defendant copied protected aspects of the work. Skidmore as Tr. for Randy Craig Wolfe Trust v. Led Zeppelin , 952 F.3d 1051 , 1064 (9th Cir. 2020) (en banc). Secondary liability exists for copyright infringement either by "intentionally inducing or encouraging direct infringement" (contributory infringement), or "by profiting from direct infringement while declining to exercise a right to stop or limit it" (vicarious infringement). Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer Studios, Inc. v. Grokster, Ltd. , 545 U.S. 913 , 930 , 125 S. Ct. 2764 , 162 L. Ed. 2d 781 (2005) (internal citations omitted). Flexport does not dispute that Lacaillade did not directly copy the source code. Instead, Flexport argues the allegations support an inference that Lacaillade induced and/or authorized Zhao's copying of Flexport 's source code. As discussed above, the Complaint does not support an inference that Lacaillade entered into an agreement with Zhao to take Flexport 's documents or that Lacaillade controlled Zhao's actions. Flexport further asserts that Lacaillade, as Freightmate's CEO, had a duty to police Zhao's actions, and thus is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged copying. The Complaint, however, does not allege any facts from which it could be inferred that Lacaillade controlled Zhao's copying of the source code, which is alleged to have occurred prior to Zhao's employment at Freightmate, as detailed below. Therefore, the motion to dismiss the copyright infringement claims against Lacaillade is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . C. Breach of Contract Flexport has adequately alleged that Lacaillade breached his obligation under Flexport 's Employee Inventions & Proprietary Information Agreement ("Agreement") to "hold in strict confidence and not directly or indirectly disclose or use any Proprietary Information, except as required within the scope of [his] employment." (Compl. ¶ 88.) While Flexport fails to sufficiently allege that Lacaillade disclosed or used any of Flexport 's trade secrets or copyrighted code, Flexport has plausibly pled that the "confidential" shipping documents Lacaillade allegedly fed into ChatGPT constituted Flexport 's "Proprietary Information." Therefore, the motion to dismiss the breach of contract claim against Lacaillade is DENIED . II. FREIGHTMATE'S MOTION TO DISMISS A. Trade Secret Misappropriation While Flexport 's direct misappropriation theory against Freightmate fails for the same reason as for Lacaillaide, Flexport has plausibly pled that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's misappropriation of trade secrets. Specifically, although Flexport does not adequately plead vicarious liability for Zhao's initial acquisition of trade secrets, Flexport has adequately pled facts supporting a plausible inference that Zhao used those trade secrets after he started working at Freightmate. Freightmate's motion to dismiss the trade secret misappropriation claim is therefore DENIED . Flexport has not adequately alleged that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged acquisition of the trade secrets, because that conduct occurred prior to Zhao's employment at Freightmate. "[T]he future employer of a person who steals a company's trade secrets for the benefit of the future employer is [not] liable under respondeat superior, absent an allegation the future employer subsequently used the trade secrets or the wrongdoer used the trade secrets once he actually became employed." Alert Enter., Inc. v. Rana , No. 22-CV-06646-JSC, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44590 , 2023 WL 2541353 , at * 3 (N.D. Cal. Mar. 16, 2023) . Here, there are no facts in the Complaint from which it can be inferred that Zhao was an employee or agent of Freightmate as of May 30, 2024, which is the date of Zhao's last alleged download. (Compl. ¶ 54.) Flexport conclusorily alleges that Zhao "co-founded" Freightmate. (Compl. ¶ 40.) But Freightmate's Certificate of Incorporation shows that Lacaillade was its sole incorporator on May 13, 2024. (Dkt. No. 36-1 at 9.) This also contradicts Flexport 's allegations that both Lacaillade and Zhao incorporated Freightmate in Delaware (Compl. ¶ 46), though it appears Flexport no longer seeks to advance this argument. ( See Dkt. No. 39 at 7 (stating that "Lacaillade . . . formally incorporated Freightmate[.]).) In response, Flexport asks this Court to take judicial notice of Freightmate's Foreign Registration Statement filed with State of Washington , which denotes that Freightmate began doing business in Washington on May 29, 2024, and lists Zhao as a governor of Freightmate. (Dkt. No. 38-3 at 4-5). However, even if this document were admissible as a public record and sufficiently establishes Zhao's employee or agent status at the time of the alleged misappropriation, a plaintiff may not supplement its allegations via judicial notice. M. B. v. City & Cnty. of San Francisco , No. 24-CV-00301-RFL, 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 162028 , 2024 WL 4049889 , at * 1 (N.D. Cal. Sept. 3, 2024) . Flexport 's request for judicial notice is therefore denied. However, Flexport has adequately pled that Freightmate is vicariously liable for Zhao's alleged use of the trade secrets after he had joined Freightmate. An employer is vicariously liable for misappropriation when it is committed "within the scope of employment [and] at least in part[] to benefit the employer, though the employer may forbid it." Brain Injury Ass'n of Cal. v. Yari , No. 19-CV-5912, 2020 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 120201 , 2020 WL 3643482 , at * 5 (C.D. Cal. Apr. 30, 2020) . Drawing all reasonable inferences in Flexport 's favor, Flexport has plausibly alleged that Zhao used the trade secret files in the course of his employment at Freightmate, and thus that Freightmate is vicariously liable for that conduct. The Complaint alleges that Zhao—a Principal Technical Program Manager at Flexport working on the Flexport Platform—had saved to his local computer over 70,000 confidential Flexport documents in the months leading up to his departure from Flexport, many of which plausibly contained trade secret information. (Compl. ¶ 38). The timing of Zhao's alleged downloads, volume of downloaded documents, Zhao's role as co-founder and COO, and Freightmate's rapid launch of a competing product containing functionality similar to the allegedly stolen source code support a plausible inference that Zhao took the files with the intention to use them to benefit Freightmate, and actually did so. See Neutron Holdings, Inc. v. Hertz Corp. , No. 23-CV-00934-JSC, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100085 , 2023 WL 3919465 , at * 5 (N.D. Cal. June 8, 2023) (finding vicarious liability for employee's use on similar facts). At least one of the files Zhao allegedly took, the Flexport Platform source code, includes functionality that was also offered by Freightmate. ( Compare Compl. ¶ 19 ( Flexport Platform includes features for ingestion of data), with Compl. ¶ 57 (Freightmate offers a document ingestion tool called "Docmate").) Apple Inc. v. Rivos, Inc. , No. 5:22-CV-02637-EJD, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 140628 , 2023 WL 5183034 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 11, 2023) , does not counsel otherwise. Zhao's conduct is more analogous to that of the individual defendants in Rivos —specifically, Wen, Rajamani, and Hardage—who were found to have plausibly threatened misappropriation than those whose conduct amounted to mere possession of confidential materials. Like Zhao, those three defendants were alleged to have downloaded substantial volumes of proprietary information shortly before departing Apple to join a direct competitor in a similar role. See id. at *6 . The Rivos court reasoned that "it would be unreasonable to require a plaintiff to demonstrate the precise ways in which Defendants may have used [plaintiff's] trade secrets, given that Defendants are the only ones who possess such information" and "discovery has not yet commenced." Id. The court, however, declined to infer misappropriation as to the other individuals—Pinot and Ye—who merely retained access to Apple files on regularly synced cloud storage without any accompanying allegations of recent exfiltration. Id. at *7 . By contrast, Zhao's conduct went far beyond "mere possession" for the reasons explained above. B. Copyright Infringement Flexport fails to adequately allege that Freightmate infringed its copyrights. Flexport does not allege that Freightmate directly copied its code. Instead, Flexport asserts that Freightmate is liable for the copyright infringement committed by Zhao. As discussed above, however, Flexport has not sufficiently alleged that Zhao made infringing copies of Flexport Platform Code while employed at Freightmate. Flexport 's argument that Freightmate should have policed Zhao and stopped him from downloading the copyrighted source code also fails for the same reason. Therefore, the motion to dismiss the copyright infringement claim against Freightmate is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND . III. CONCLUSION Based on the foregoing, the motion to dismiss is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART as set forth above. Freightmate's request for judicial notice (Dkt. No. 36-1) is GRANTED , and Flexport 's request for judicial notice (Dkt. No. 38-2) is DENIED . If Flexport wishes to file a Second Amended Complaint correcting the deficiencies identified above, it shall do so by August 8, 2025. Flexport may not add new claims or parties without leave of the Court or stipulation by the parties pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 15 . If no such amended complaint is filed by that date, the claims that were dismissed in this Order will remain dismissed, and the case will proceed solely on the remaining claims, including the ones Defendants did not move to dismiss. IT IS SO ORDERED . Dated: RITA F. For Bryan Lacaillade, Defendant: David R. Burtt , LEAD ATTORNEY, Mobility Legal P.C., Reno , NV . lexis_case_text: Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock, 350 F.R.D. 289 Sanctions disallowed by Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 227071 (Nov. 12, 2025) Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51429 , 2025 WL 871225 (Mar. 20, 2025) Michael H. Simon OPINION AND ORDER DENYING PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR INTERIM ATTORNEY'S FEES AND ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE Michael H. Simon , District Judge . Green Building Initiative, Inc. (" GBI ") sued Stephen R. Peacock and Green Globe Limited (collectively, "Defendants") for trademark infringement, unfair competition, breach of contract, and deceptive trade practices under Oregon law. In February 2025, GBI moved to strike two of Defendants' counterclaims pursuant to Oregon's anti-SLAPP statute, Oregon Revised Statute ("ORS") § 31.150 ("Motion to Strike"). The Court granted the Motion to Strike in May 2025 without prejudice. Now before the Court is GBI 's Motion for Interim Attorney's Fees in the amount of $29,061.50, pursuant to ORS § 31.152(3) . ECF 85 ("Fees Motion"). ORS § 31.152(3) provides: "A defendant who prevails on a special motion to strike made under ORS [§] 31.150 shall be awarded reasonable attorney fees and costs." GBI argues that, because the Court granted the Motion to Strike, it prevailed for purposes of ORS § 31.152 and therefore is entitled to reasonable fees. Defendants urge the Court to reserve ruling on the Fees Motion until the case is determined on the merits. In response, GBI argues that nothing in the Oregon statute forecloses an interim fee award before final judgment, and "[f]ederal courts applying Oregon 's anti-SLAPP statute have consistently held that fee awards are appropriate immediately following resolution of the special motion to strike." See ECF 85 at 2. In support of the latter point, GBI cites Stell v. Cardenas, No. 3:21-cv-413-HZ, 2022 WL 1696093, at *6 (D. Or. May 26, 2022) , and Page v. Parsons, 249 F. Supp. 3d 998, 1017 (D. Or. 2017) . Stell , however, is totally fake; it is an "hallucinated" case. There is no case at 2022 WL 1696093 . Nor does there exist a case with the parties "Stell" and "Cardenas" (in either orientation—in the District of Oregon or elsewhere). Moreover, Case No. 3:21-cv-413 is a real case, styled JS Halberstam Irrevocable Grantor Trust v. Davis , and it was presided over by the undersigned ("SI"), not U.S. District Judge Hernández ("HZ"). Further, that case was closed by judgment on May 9, 2022, and no decision was issued on May 26, 2022. Page v. Parsons is almost real. There is a case titled Page v. Parsons that discusses the Oregon anti-SLAPP statute, but it is not a federal case from the District of Oregon. See Page v. Parsons , 249 Or. App. 445 , 277 P.3d 609 (2012) . Page , however, does not discuss when fees are due or may be advanced under the statute. And as a matter of logic, that Oregon Court of Appeals decision cannot support the proposition that GBI cited it for—that federal courts applying Oregon 's anti-SLAPP statute have consistently held that fee awards are appropriate immediately following resolution of the special motion to strike. Indeed, a basic internet search easily reveals both the real citation for Page v. Parsons , and the real case listed at 249 F. Supp. 3d 991 (a death penalty habeas decision from the Eighth Circuit). See Deck v. Steele , 249 F. Supp. 3d 991 , 998 (E. D. Mo. Apr. 13, 2017) , rev'd and remanded, Deck v. Jennings , 978 F.3d 578 (8th Cir. 2020) . GBI 's fake case citations appear to be hallmarks of a generative artificial intelligence ("AI") tool, such as ChatGPT. For some time, it has been well known—and well-publicized—that generative AI tools "hallucinate" fake cases. See Kruse v. Karlen , 692 S.W.3d 43 , 52 (Mo. Ct. App. 2024) (noting, in February 2024, that the issue of fictitious cases being submitted to courts had gained "national attention"); see also Isabel Gottlieb & Isaiah Poritz, Legal Errors by Top AI Models "Alarmingly Prevalent," Study Says , Bloomberg Law (Jan 12, 2024) (explaining that, in a test examining more than 200,000 legal questions, AI researchers at Stanford University found that general-purpose large language models hallucinate at least 75% of the time when answering questions about a court's rulings). As another court recently explained: Attorneys should understand that chatbots, including legal 'AI' chatbots, are large-language models (LLMs), not a true 'artificial intelligence' out of the pages of science fiction. They are not designed to answer questions factually. They are designed to mimic patterns of words, probabilistically. When they are 'right,' it is because correct things are often written down in the dataset they were trained on. Seither & Cherry Quad Cities, Inc. v. Oakland Automation, LLC , 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 143941 , 2025 WL 2105286 , at * 1 (E.D. Mich. July 28, 2025) (footnote omitted). Just as well-known are the ethical obligations of attorneys who choose to use generative AI when drafting court filings. This year, the Oregon State Bar explicitly warned attorneys that: The most obvious way in which a lawyer could run afoul of Oregon RPCs [Rules of Professional Conduct] 3.3 [Candor] and 4.1 [Truthfulness] is through the submission of and reliance on unverified and fictitious cases, citations, quotes, or conclusions generated by AI. Therefore, to avoid violation of Oregon RPCs 3.3 and 4.1 , lawyers must review for accuracy any GAI output discussing case-specific facts or providing a case citation, quotation, or conclusion to ensure that the GAI did not hallucinate when providing its answer, or simply get the answer wrong. Oregon State Bar, Formal Opinion No. 2025-205, Artificial Intelligence Tools (February 2025). Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure , titled "Representations to the Court," "imposes a duty of 'reasonable inquiry'" to ensure, among other things, that "anything filed with the court is . . . [']legally tenable.'" Islamic Shura Council of S. Cal. v. FBI , 757 F.3d 870 , 872 (9th Cir. 2014) (quoting Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp. , 496 U.S. 384 , 393 , 110 S. Ct. 2447 , 110 L. Ed. 2d 359 (1990) ). Rule 11(b) provides in relevant part: By presenting to the court a pleading, written motion, or other paper—whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating it—an attorney or unrepresented party certifies that to the best of the person's knowledge, information, and belief, formed after an inquiry reasonable under the circumstances : . . . . (2) the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law[.] (emphasis added). As one court explained when addressing how Rule 11 applies when counsel submits a filing that relies on fake cases: A fake opinion is not "existing law" and citation to a fake opinion does not provide a non-frivolous ground for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law, or for establishing new law. An attempt to persuade a court or oppose an adversary by relying on fake opinions is an abuse of the adversary system. Mata v. Avianca, Inc. , 678 F. Supp. 3d 443 , 461 (S.D.N.Y. 2023) (footnote omitted). For these sorts of violations, sanctions may be imposed. Under Rule 11 , "[i]f, after notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court determines that Rule 11(b) has been violated, the court may impose an appropriate sanction on any . . . party that violated the rule or is responsible for the violation." Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(c)(1) . The Court will follow that process here. GBI is hereby ordered to show cause within two weeks as to why the Court should not issue sanctions on both GBI and its counsel for the use of fake citations in violation of Rule 11(b) . GBI is also ordered to disclose what it believes to be an appropriate sanction. GBI 's proposed sanctions should reflect the nature and seriousness of its use of fake citations and appropriate steps to ensure that this type of violation will not occur again. Further, the Court agrees with Defendants that the Fees Motion is premature. Rule 54(d)(2)(B)(i) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure provides that a motion for attorney's fees must "be filed no later than 14 days after the entry of judgment" unless a statute or court order provides otherwise. Although GBI argues that state law governs attorney's fees awards, nothing in ORS § 31.152 dictates the timing of the fee award. GBI points to no case or statute requiring the Court to order an interim fee award before the entry of final judgment, nor does GBI cite any real case where a federal court has advanced fees under similar circumstances. Additionally, awarding an interim fee award would be especially imprudent here. Because the Court granted GBI 's Motion to Strike without prejudice, Defendants may replead the stricken counterclaims. It would be a waste of the Court's and the parties' resources to calculate and advance fees pertaining to the counterclaims, especially if Defendants can successfully replead. Further, after entry of judgment, there may also be offsetting fees. The Court DENIES without prejudice GBI 's Fees Motion. ECF 75. The Court also ORDERS GBI to show cause not later than November 10, 2025, why sanctions should not be imposed under Rule 11(b) for GBI 's citation of hallucinated cases. In that filing, GBI also shall propose appropriate sanctions for GBI and its counsel, if the Court concludes that sanctions should be imposed. IT IS SO ORDERED . Peacock, 350 F.R.D. 289 GREEN BUILDING INITIATIVE, INC. , an Oregon non-profit corporation, Plaintiff, v. STEPHEN R. PEACOCK, an individual residing in California ; and GREEN GLOBE LIMITED, a company based in the United Kingdom , Defendants. Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock Sanctions disallowed by Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 227071 (Nov. 12, 2025) Green Bldg. Initiative, Inc. v. Peacock, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51429 , 2025 WL 871225 (Mar. 20, 2025) Michael H. Simon OPINION AND ORDER DENYING PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR INTERIM ATTORNEY'S FEES AND ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE Michael H. Simon , District Judge . Green Building Initiative, Inc. (" GBI ") sued Stephen R. Peacock and Green Globe Limited (collectively, "Defendants") for trademark infringement, unfair competition, breach of contract, and deceptive trade practices under Oregon law. In February 2025, GBI moved to strike two of Defendants' counterclaims pursuant to Oregon's anti-SLAPP statute, Oregon Revised Statute ("ORS") § 31.150 ("Motion to Strike"). The Court granted the Motion to Strike in May 2025 without prejudice. Now before the Court is GBI 's Motion for Interim Attorney's Fees in the amount of $29,061.50, pursuant to ORS § 31.152(3) . ECF 85 ("Fees Motion"). ORS § 31.152(3) provides: "A defendant who prevails on a special motion to strike made under ORS [§] 31.150 shall be awarded reasonable attorney fees and costs." GBI argues that, because the Court granted the Motion to Strike, it prevailed for purposes of ORS § 31.152 and therefore is entitled to reasonable fees. Defendants urge the Court to reserve ruling on the Fees Motion until the case is determined on the merits. In response, GBI argues that nothing in the Oregon statute forecloses an interim fee award before final judgment, and "[f]ederal courts applying Oregon 's anti-SLAPP statute have consistently held that fee awards are appropriate immediately following resolution of the special motion to strike." See ECF 85 at 2. In support of the latter point, GBI cites Stell v. Cardenas, No. 3:21-cv-413-HZ, 2022 WL 1696093, at *6 (D. Or. May 26, 2022) , and Page v. Parsons, 249 F. Supp. 3d 998, 1017 (D. Or. 2017) . Stell , however, is totally fake; it is an "hallucinated" case. There is no case at 2022 WL 1696093 . Nor does there exist a case with the parties "Stell" and "Cardenas" (in either orientation—in the District of Oregon or elsewhere). Moreover, Case No. 3:21-cv-413 is a real case, styled JS Halberstam Irrevocable Grantor Trust v. Davis , and it was presided over by the undersigned ("SI"), not U.S. District Judge Hernández ("HZ"). Further, that case was closed by judgment on May 9, 2022, and no decision was issued on May 26, 2022. Page v. Parsons is almost real. There is a case titled Page v. Parsons that discusses the Oregon anti-SLAPP statute, but it is not a federal case from the District of Oregon. See Page v. Parsons , 249 Or. App. 445 , 277 P.3d 609 (2012) . Page , however, does not discuss when fees are due or may be advanced under the statute. And as a matter of logic, that Oregon Court of Appeals decision cannot support the proposition that GBI cited it for—that federal courts applying Oregon 's anti-SLAPP statute have consistently held that fee awards are appropriate immediately following resolution of the special motion to strike. Indeed, a basic internet search easily reveals both the real citation for Page v. Parsons , and the real case listed at 249 F. Supp. 3d 991 (a death penalty habeas decision from the Eighth Circuit). See Deck v. Steele , 249 F. Supp. 3d 991 , 998 (E. D. Mo. Apr. 13, 2017) , rev'd and remanded, Deck v. Jennings , 978 F.3d 578 (8th Cir. 2020) . GBI 's fake case citations appear to be hallmarks of a generative artificial intelligence ("AI") tool, such as ChatGPT. For some time, it has been well known—and well-publicized—that generative AI tools "hallucinate" fake cases. See Kruse v. Karlen , 692 S.W.3d 43 , 52 (Mo. Ct. App. 2024) (noting, in February 2024, that the issue of fictitious cases being submitted to courts had gained "national attention"); see also Isabel Gottlieb & Isaiah Poritz, Legal Errors by Top AI Models "Alarmingly Prevalent," Study Says , Bloomberg Law (Jan 12, 2024) (explaining that, in a test examining more than 200,000 legal questions, AI researchers at Stanford University found that general-purpose large language models hallucinate at least 75% of the time when answering questions about a court's rulings). As another court recently explained: Attorneys should understand that chatbots, including legal 'AI' chatbots, are large-language models (LLMs), not a true 'artificial intelligence' out of the pages of science fiction. They are not designed to answer questions factually. They are designed to mimic patterns of words, probabilistically. When they are 'right,' it is because correct things are often written down in the dataset they were trained on. Seither & Cherry Quad Cities, Inc. v. Oakland Automation, LLC , 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 143941 , 2025 WL 2105286 , at * 1 (E.D. Mich. July 28, 2025) (footnote omitted). Just as well-known are the ethical obligations of attorneys who choose to use generative AI when drafting court filings. This year, the Oregon State Bar explicitly warned attorneys that: The most obvious way in which a lawyer could run afoul of Oregon RPCs [Rules of Professional Conduct] 3.3 [Candor] and 4.1 [Truthfulness] is through the submission of and reliance on unverified and fictitious cases, citations, quotes, or conclusions generated by AI. Therefore, to avoid violation of Oregon RPCs 3.3 and 4.1 , lawyers must review for accuracy any GAI output discussing case-specific facts or providing a case citation, quotation, or conclusion to ensure that the GAI did not hallucinate when providing its answer, or simply get the answer wrong. Oregon State Bar, Formal Opinion No. 2025-205, Artificial Intelligence Tools (February 2025). Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure , titled "Representations to the Court," "imposes a duty of 'reasonable inquiry'" to ensure, among other things, that "anything filed with the court is . . . [']legally tenable.'" Islamic Shura Council of S. Cal. v. FBI , 757 F.3d 870 , 872 (9th Cir. 2014) (quoting Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp. , 496 U.S. 384 , 393 , 110 S. Ct. 2447 , 110 L. Ed. 2d 359 (1990) ). Rule 11(b) provides in relevant part: By presenting to the court a pleading, written motion, or other paper—whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating it—an attorney or unrepresented party certifies that to the best of the person's knowledge, information, and belief, formed after an inquiry reasonable under the circumstances : . . . . (2) the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law or for establishing new law[.] (emphasis added). As one court explained when addressing how Rule 11 applies when counsel submits a filing that relies on fake cases: A fake opinion is not "existing law" and citation to a fake opinion does not provide a non-frivolous ground for extending, modifying, or reversing existing law, or for establishing new law. An attempt to persuade a court or oppose an adversary by relying on fake opinions is an abuse of the adversary system. Mata v. Avianca, Inc. , 678 F. Supp. 3d 443 , 461 (S.D.N.Y. 2023) (footnote omitted). For these sorts of violations, sanctions may be imposed. Under Rule 11 , "[i]f, after notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court determines that Rule 11(b) has been violated, the court may impose an appropriate sanction on any . . . party that violated the rule or is responsible for the violation." Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(c)(1) . The Court will follow that process here. GBI is hereby ordered to show cause within two weeks as to why the Court should not issue sanctions on both GBI and its counsel for the use of fake citations in violation of Rule 11(b) . GBI is also ordered to disclose what it believes to be an appropriate sanction. GBI 's proposed sanctions should reflect the nature and seriousness of its use of fake citations and appropriate steps to ensure that this type of violation will not occur again. Further, the Court agrees with Defendants that the Fees Motion is premature. Rule 54(d)(2)(B)(i) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure provides that a motion for attorney's fees must "be filed no later than 14 days after the entry of judgment" unless a statute or court order provides otherwise. Although GBI argues that state law governs attorney's fees awards, nothing in ORS § 31.152 dictates the timing of the fee award. GBI points to no case or statute requiring the Court to order an interim fee award before the entry of final judgment, nor does GBI cite any real case where a federal court has advanced fees under similar circumstances. Additionally, awarding an interim fee award would be especially imprudent here. Because the Court granted GBI 's Motion to Strike without prejudice, Defendants may replead the stricken counterclaims. It would be a waste of the Court's and the parties' resources to calculate and advance fees pertaining to the counterclaims, especially if Defendants can successfully replead. Further, after entry of judgment, there may also be offsetting fees. The Court DENIES without prejudice GBI 's Fees Motion. ECF 75. The Court also ORDERS GBI to show cause not later than November 10, 2025, why sanctions should not be imposed under Rule 11(b) for GBI 's citation of hallucinated cases. In that filing, GBI also shall propose appropriate sanctions for GBI and its counsel, if the Court concludes that sanctions should be imposed. IT IS SO ORDERED .
