lexis_case_text: Wilson v. Spathelf, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 171256 Appeal terminated, 01/06/2026 Leo A. Latella REPORT AND RECOMMENDATION I. Introduction The Plaintiff, David Mark Wilson, is a pretrial detainee currently confined at the Lackawanna County Prison. He is being held there in connection with a pending criminal prosecution in Lackawanna County on charges of aggravated assault, simple assault, resisting arrest, reckless endangerment, disorderly conduct, public drunkenness, and harassment. His criminal charges arise out of an incident that occurred in the City of Scranton on July 19, 2022, which are the same events forming the basis of this civil rights action pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1983 . Wilson is alleging excessive force on the part of five City of Scranton police officers and two Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs who arrested him on that day. He further brings suit against the prosecutor in the criminal case, alleging malicious prosecution and the public defender who was appointed to represent him, alleging ineffective assistance of counsel. In addition to Wilson's pleadings that are directed to this federal civil rights action, he has filed — inappropriately — many pleadings relevant only to his pending prosecution in Lackawanna County . The Scranton police officers filed an Answer with affirmative defenses in this action, while the Deputy Sheriffs, prosecutor, and public defender filed Motions to Dismiss. The Motions to Dismiss have been fully briefed and are ripe for disposition. For the reasons that follow, it will be recommended that the Deputy Sheriffs' Motion to Dismiss be granted in part and denied in part, and that the Motion to Dismiss filed by the prosecutor and public defender be granted. II. Procedural History The Plaintiff initiated this pro se civil rights action by filing a Complaint pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1983 on October 3, 2023, alleging, inter alia , that his July 2022 arrest was unlawful and that the officers involved — Scranton Police Department Officers Lawrence Spathelf, Leland Palmere, Isaac Troutman, Mark Miller, Timothy Merkel, and Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs Jeff Hughes and Patrick Chelland — used excessive force. He further alleges "Abuse of a Prisoner" and "Abuse of Powers and Authority: Oppression" against Assistant District Attorney Danielle Guari . (Doc. 1, p. 6). The filing fee was paid on October 17, 2023, and the Complaint was deemed filed. On November 13, 2023, Magistrate Judge Schwab conducted a preliminary review of the complaint in accordance with 28 U.S.C. § 1915A . In that Order, Judge Schwab held that Wilson had failed to state a false arrest claim on which relief can be granted as to Officer Palmere (Doc. 9, pp. 8-10) and that Plaintiff did not describe any conduct allegedly taken by the other defendants against him. Wilson was granted leave to amend along with additional guidance surrounding the claims that he was attempting to pursue. On December 4, 2023, Wilson filed an amended complaint, adding Assistant Public Defender Joseph Kalinowski ; however, the Amended Complaint failed to correct the pleading deficiencies noted by Judge Schwab and contained many of the same allegations—supported by conclusory statements but as little substance as the original Complaint. ( See generally Doc. 15). All Defendants filed motions to dismiss the Amended Complaint. After the motions were briefed, Plaintiff filed a Second Amended Complaint on June 17, 2024, (Doc. 46), mooting the then outstanding motions. ( See Doc. 48). The Second Amended Complaint is now the operative pleading document. In response to the Second Amended Complaint, Defendants Deputy Sheriff Chelland and Hughes filed a Motion to Dismiss on July 1, 2024, (Doc. 52), and supporting brief on July 15, 2024. (Doc. 56). Plaintiff filed his Brief in Opposition on August 8, 2024, (Doc. 74), and Defendants submitted their reply brief on August 22, 2024. (Doc. 79). Defendants Guari and Kalinowski filed a Motion to Dismiss on July 10, 2024, (Doc. 53) and supporting brief on July 24, 2024. (Doc. 60). Plaintiff filed his Brief in Opposition on August 8, 2024, (Doc. 75). A reply brief was not filed. The Scranton Police Officers filed an Answer with affirmative defenses on July 31, 2024. (Doc. 67). Each of the Defendants' Motions to Dismiss will be addressed in turn. III. Background The Second Amended Complaint (hereinafter referred to as the "Complaint") consists of a detailed recounting of Plaintiff's version of the July 19, 2022, events, and is summarized as follows. According to Plaintiff, Scranton Police Department Officers Palmere and Spathelf were dispatched to 456 Laurel Street due to complaints of a disorderly male believed to be under the influence of methamphetamine. (Doc. 46, ¶ 1-2). Once at the scene, the officers encountered David Wilson wearing only boxer shorts and flip-flops. From there, Wilson alleges that he raised his hands and repeatedly asked Officer Palmere, "What's the problem?" and "Am I under arrest?" ( Id. at ¶ 2). Officer Palmere allegedly remained still and did not answer. Wilson avers that he approached Palmere and asked Palmere if he could lift his sunglasses, out of "concern for the officer," to which Palmere remained unmoving and did not answer. ( Id. at ¶ 5). Wilson states that he then got closer to Palmere, and after being unable to see Palmere's eyes through the sunglasses, he told Palmere that he was "just going to lift your sunglasses, sir, to make sure you're okay." ( Id. at ¶¶ 6-7). Palmere allegedly stood still and "allowed [Wilson] to proceed without opposing or asking [him] to desist. He said no words." ( Id. ) Wilson avers that he proceeded to "gently" lift the sunglasses up, made eye contact with Officer Palmere, and placed the sunglasses back down, apologizing. Palmere allegedly continued to remain still and unmoving. ( Id. at ¶¶ 8-9). After this, according to Wilson's telling, Officer Palmere stormed at Wilson with no warning and took him to the ground. ( Id. at ¶¶ 9-10). Wilson describes this scuffle, stating, "in an attempt to break the fall or position myself to a comfortable position without resistance my hand involuntarily ended up on the butt of Palmere's weapon." ( Id. at ¶ 10). Wilson further concedes that he cursed at Palmere and stated, "You're not getting your gun" as he continued to hold onto the weapon. ( Id. at ¶ 12). At this point, Wilson describes how he was on the ground and in great discomfort, due to both the force used against him and his skin being in contact with the hot asphalt. Officer Spathelf arrived on the scene, and Wilson alleges that "without cause," Spathelf "shot" him with a taser and a stun gun. ( Id. at ¶¶ 20-21). More law enforcement arrived at that point: Officer Troutman purportedly "stepped on [Wilson's] left foot holding it in place against the hot pavement," and Deputy Sheriff Chelland "briefly stepped on [Wilson's] right foot." ( Id. at ¶¶ 26-27). At some point soon after, Wilson alleges that Deputy Sheriff Hughes "stepped on his left foot holding it in place," Officer Troutman did the same to his right foot, and Officer Palmere then allegedly dropped his full body weight onto Wilson. ( Id. at ¶¶ 31-32). Wilson contends that at some point throughout this interaction, Officer Merkel held him down against the ground ( id. at ¶ 38) and that Officer Miller grabbed Wilson by his throat. ( Id. at ¶ 40). Wilson alleges that the "violations" against him did not stop upon his arrest: "All 5 police officers listed tampered with the authentic body cam footage using recently released AI generative tools to create deepfakes to try and cover up their criminal acts of police brutality, willful misconduct, excessive force, following a [sic] unconstitutional 'arrest.'" ( Id. at p. 18, ¶ 39). Officers Palmere and Spathelf also allegedly lied on the probable cause affidavit and "at [his] habeas hearing" held on February 12, 2024. ( Id. at p. 18, ¶¶ 40-42). With respect to the attorneys involved in his criminal case, he accuses ADA Danielle Guari of manipulating Wilson into waving his preliminary hearing, using generative AI videos, and moving forward with the case knowing that the officers had used excessive force against him. ( Id. at ¶ 46, 49). He accuses his court-appointed attorney, Public Defender Joseph Kalinowski , of "protecting criminals of the govt. [sic] agencies of the state and city," and seemingly withholding the body-worn camera footage from Wilson and only sharing the "generative AI" videos. ( Id. at ¶¶ 47-48, 51-52). Wilson brings claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 for violations of the Fourth Amendment , Fifth Amendment , Sixth Amendment , and Fourteenth Amendment . He also alleges violations of the Exclusionary Rule, Brady Rule, and "Violating Generative AI Core Values to Deceive Jury." ( Id. at pp. 22-23). As to relief, he requests criminal charges be brought against each defendant; a criminal investigation be launched on AI generated videos; that he be compensated for his injuries; that he be given the right to access authentic eyewitness videos; that he be allowed to subpoena various experts; that effective counsel be appointed; and lastly, that a suppression hearing be held. ( Id. at pp. 22, 25). IV. Standard of Review Rule 8 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure states that a claim must include "a short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief." Fed. R. Civ. P. 8(a) . This requires the Plaintiff to put forth evidence that, "when taken as true, suggest[s] the required elements of a particular legal theory." McNeilly v. City of Pittsburgh , 40 F.Supp.3d 643 , 650 (W.D. Pa. 2014) . The Plaintiff must state "sufficient factual allegations which 'nudge' its claims 'across the line from conceivable to plausible.'" Id. (quoting Ashcroft v. Iqbal , 556 U.S. 662 , 680 , 129 S. Ct. 1937 , 1951 , 173 L. Ed. 2d 868 (2009) ). The purpose is to ensure "fair notice of the factual basis of a claim while raising a 'reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.'" Id. at 651 . Rule 12(b)(6) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure allows a defendant to move to dismiss a complaint for "failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted." Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) . This means that a complaint may be dismissed if it does not present sufficient facts to support a plausible claim: "Under Rule 12(b)(6) , a motion to dismiss may be granted only if, accepting all well-pleaded allegations in the complaint as true and viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff, a court finds that plaintiff's claims lack facial plausibility." Warren Gen. Hosp. v. Amgen, Inc. , 643 F.3d 77 , 84 (3d Cir. 2011) (citing Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly , 550 U.S. 544 , 555-56 , 127 S. Ct. 1955 , 167 L. Ed. 2d 929 (2007) ). The court must accept both the Plaintiff's allegations and any reasonable inferences that can be drawn as true and construe them in the light most favorable to the non-moving party. Monroe v. Beard , 536 F.3d 198 , 205 (3d Cir. 2008) . However, it need not accept "'unsupported conclusions and unwarranted inferences, or a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.'" Morrow v. Balaski , 719 F.3d 160 , 165 (3d Cir. 2013) (quoting Baraka v. McGreevey , 481 F.3d 187 , 195 (3d Cir. 2007) ). Reciting the elements that make up a cause of action and supporting them with "mere conclusory statements" is insufficient to give those statements the presumption of truth. McNeilly , 40 F.Supp.3d at 650 (citing Iqbal , 556 U.S. at 678 , 129 S.Ct. 1937 ). When faced with a motion to dismiss pursuant to 12(b)(6) , a court should conduct a two-part analysis: 1) separate the factual and legal elements of a claim; and 2) "determine whether the facts alleged in the complaint are sufficient to show that the plaintiff has a plausible claim for relief." Id. (internal citation removed). The court may consider facts alleged on the face of the complaint, as well as "documents incorporated into the complaint by reference, and matters of which a court may take judicial notice." Tellabs, Inc. v. Makor Issues & Rights, Ltd. , 551 U.S. 308 , 322 , 127 S. Ct. 2499 , 168 L. Ed. 2d 179 (2007) . This analysis requires the court to look at the claims on a case-by-case basis and rely both "on its judicial experience and common sense." McNeilly , 40 F.Supp.3d at 650 . The court will not be reaching legal conclusions, but instead is merely determining "whether the plaintiff should be permitted to offer evidence in support of the allegations." Id. (citing Maio v. Aetna , 221 F.3d 472 , 482 (3d Cir. 2000) ). Furthermore, a pro se complaint, "however inartfully pleaded," must be held to "less stringent standards than formal pleadings drafted by lawyers" and can only be dismissed for failure to state a claim if it appears beyond doubt that the plaintiff can prove no set of facts in support a viable claim for relief. Haines v. Kerner , 404 U.S. 519 , 520-21 , 92 S. Ct. 594 , 30 L. Ed. 2d 652 (1972) . V. Discussion A. The Motion to Dismiss for Defendant Deputy Sheriffs Plaintiff has averred that the Defendant Deputy Sheriffs violated his Fourth Amendment , Fifth Amendment , Sixth Amendment , and Fourteenth Amendment rights, primarily stemming from the excessive force allegation. Each will be addressed in turn. While Wilson has failed to state a claim on which relief can be granted as to Chelland, he has stated a viable claim as to Hughes. It will therefore be recommended that the Deputy Sheriffs' Motion to Dismiss be granted in part and denied in part. 1. Fourth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff alleges that both Deputy Sheriffs violated his Fourth Amendment rights by using excessive force against him during his arrest on July 19, 2022. The allegations are that Deputy Chelland "briefly stepped on [his] right foot: unreasonably" (Doc. 46, ¶ 27); Deputy Hughes "stepped on [his] left foot holding it in place as Palmere approached" ( id. at ¶ 31); and Deputy Hughes "caused unnecessary injuries due to excessive force" to his left foot. ( Id. at ¶ 33). In the Brief in Support of their Motion to Dismiss, Defendants argue that de minimis force is insufficient to support a claim for excessive force in violation of the Fourth Amendment . (Doc. 56, pp. 5-10). In his response, Plaintiff attempts to assert a new claim, namely that "both Deputy Sheriffs condoned the use of excessive force [by] failing to intervene." (Doc. 74, ¶ 5). The Defendants responded by correctly arguing that Plaintiff had not alleged failure to intervene in the Complaint, and that the claim should therefore should not be considered. (Doc. 79, p. 2). Plaintiff asserts that Defendants Deputy Sheriffs failed to intervene in the excessive force committed by the Scranton Police Officers for the first time in his Brief in Opposition. "[I]t is axiomatic that the complaint may not be amended by the briefs in opposition to a motion to dismiss." Com. of Pa. ex rel. Zimmerman v. PepsiCo, Inc. , 836 F.2d 173 , 181 (3d Cir. 1988) (quoting Car Carriers, Inc. v. Ford Motor Co. , 745 F.2d 1101 , 1107 (7th Cir. 1984) ). See also Frederico v. Home Depot , 507 F.3d 188 , 201-02 (a court will "not consider after-the-fact allegations in determining the sufficiency of [the] complaint" under Rule 12(b)(6) ). Therefore, "the new legal claims alleged in the brief in opposition . . . cannot be considered when evaluating the sufficiency of the allegations in Plaintiff's complaint at the motion to dismiss stage." Poindexter v. Starbucks York Roasting Plant , No. 1:21-CV-1847, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 7159 , 2023 WL 185096 , at * 5 (M.D. Pa. Jan. 13, 2023) . See also Motes v. United States , No. 1:23-CV-00629, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 127574 , 2025 WL 1870919 , at * 4 (M.D. Pa. July 7, 2025) (refusing to consider a new claim raised in a brief in opposition and recognizing that "Under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 8 , a complaint must adequately give defendants notice of the claims against them and the relevant theories of liability."). For this reason, only the excessive force claim will be considered for purposes of the Motion to Dismiss, and the claim of failure to intervene will not be evaluated. The crux of Plaintiff's relevant excessive force claims is that both Deputy Sheriffs stepped on his foot. As fully explained below, because Plaintiff only alleged that Chelland briefly stepped on his foot, Plaintiff has not sufficiently alleged an excessive force claim as to Chelland. His allegations against Hughes, while marginal, are a bit more robust, and the allegations just cross the line to survive a motion to dismiss. In the landmark case Graham v. Connor , the Supreme Court established that the standard for evaluating an excessive force claim is objective reasonableness under the Fourth Amendment . 490 U.S. 386 , 395 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 , 1871 , 104 L. Ed. 2d 443 (1989) . Law enforcement may only use the force reasonably necessary to effectuate an arrest. Garrison v. Porch , 376 F. App'x 274 , 277 (3d Cir. 2010) (citing Tennessee v. Garner , 471 U.S. 1 , 9-10 , 105 S. Ct. 1694 , 85 L.Ed.2d 1 (1985) ). If the force used exceeded what was reasonably necessary, the arrestee may bring suit under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 . Id. Similarly, "[t]o state a claim for excessive force as an unreasonable seizure under the Fourth Amendment , a plaintiff must show that a 'seizure' occurred and that it was unreasonable." Abraham v. Raso , 183 F.3d 279 , 288 (3d Cir. 1999) (citing Brower v. County of Inyo , 489 U.S. 593 , 599 , 109 S. Ct. 1378 , 1382-83 , 103 L.Ed.2d 628 (1989) ). Determining reasonableness requires looking at the totality of the circumstances, including the severity of the alleged crime, whether the suspect posed a threat to others, or whether the suspect resisted arrest or tried to flee. Noble v. City of Camden , 112 F. Supp. 3d 208 , 227 (D.N.J. 2015) (quoting Graham , 490 U.S. at 396 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 ). For these cases — indeed, for all civil rights claims — the plaintiff must assert personal involvement for each named defendant. Millbrook v. United States , 8 F. Supp. 3d 601 , 613 (M.D. Pa. 2014) . A civil rights claim cannot be premised solely on a theory of respondeat superior . Id. The force that is in question "must be more than a de minimus amount of force for a constitutional claim to arise." Smith v. Cpl. Louriero , No. 3:16-CV-0741, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 89856 , 2017 WL 2952819 , at * 5 (M.D. Pa. June 9, 2017) , report and recommendation adopted sub nom. Smith v. Louriero , No. 3:16-CV-0741, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 105027 , 2017 WL 2926020 (M.D. Pa. July 7, 2017) (citing Ingraham v. Wright , 430 U.S. 651 , 674 , 97 S. Ct. 1401 , 51 L. Ed. 2d 711 (1977) ). If the touching by law enforcement is found to be "minimal and routine," that fails to support an excessive force claim: such use of force cannot be said to be objectively unreasonable. Garcia v. Cnty. of Bucks, PA , 155 F. Supp. 2d 259 , 265 (E.D. Pa. 2001) . On the other hand, a plaintiff is not obligated to show injury in order to prove excessive force. "[A] § 1983 plaintiff may suffer a constitutional injury (i.e., an infringement of his right to be free from an unreasonable seizure) without suffering a physical injury." Velius v. Twp. of Hamilton , 754 F. Supp. 2d 689 , 692 (D.N.J. 2010) , aff'd , 466 F. App'x 133 (3d Cir. 2012) . See also Flood v. Schaefer , 439 F. App'x 179 , 182 (3d Cir. 2011) ("in evaluating claims of excessive force, the central issue is the force the officers employed (and whether it was reasonable under the circumstances), not the injury they caused"). With these principles in mind, the inquiry is whether the Plaintiff has alleged sufficient actions taken by each individual Deputy Sheriffs, and whether those actions were objectively unreasonable to the point that they violated Plaintiff's constitutional rights. Viewing the allegations in the light most favorable to the Plaintiff, which is the standard at the motion to dismiss stage, the Deputy Sheriffs arrived on the scene while a physical altercation was occurring between Plaintiff and a law enforcement officer that resulted in Plaintiff getting hold of Officer Palmere's firearm. (Doc. 46, ¶¶ 10-16). Plaintiff's Complaint describes a chaotic scene, and it is with that environment in mind that reasonableness will be evaluated, not with the 20/20 vision of hindsight. Graham , 490 U.S. at 396-97 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 , 1872 ("The calculus of reasonableness must embody allowance for the fact that police officers are often forced to make split-second judgments—in circumstances that are tense, uncertain, and rapidly evolving—about the amount of force that is necessary in a particular situation."). With respect to Deputy Sheriff Chelland, even Plaintiff's own description of the event allege that the touching was, if not inadvertent, at least de minimis : "As [Chelland] was listening to Palmere's story [he] briefly stepped on my foot." Plaintiff does assert that this was done "unreasonably" or forcefully. Even taking Plaintiff's allegation in the light most favorable to him, the facts as stated do not present a claim of excessive force. Given the circumstances surrounding the "stepping" and the fact that it was "brief" supports this conclusion. Plaintiff fails to state a Fourth Amendment excessive force claim as to Defendant Chelland. It will therefore be recommended that the claim against Chelland be dismissed, but, considering his Plaintiff's pro se status, he should be given leave to amend his Complaint but only as to the Fourth Amendment excessive force claim against Deputy Sheriff Chelland. Plaintiff should be reminded that "'[n]ot every push or shove, even if it may later seem unnecessary in the peace of a judge's chambers,' violates the Fourth Amendment ." Graham , 490 U.S. at 386 (internal citation omitted). Deputy Sheriff Hughes's case is less straightforward. Plaintiff asserts that Hughes stepped on his left foot to hold it in place while Palmere approached and continued his attempts to restrain Wilson. The Defendant argues that Plaintiff has failed "to allege with any specificity what injury to his left foot he allegedly sustained" (Doc. 56, p. 9) yet, as discussed above, this is not a required element of an excessive force claim. At this juncture, dismissal of Plaintiff's Fourth Amendment claim as to Hughes would be premature. An excessive force claim is "multi-facetted" and "fact-specific," and Plaintiff has pleaded a sufficient claim to allow it to proceed to the discovery phase. Velardo v. Lewko , No. 3:18-CV-1885, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 143792 , 2019 WL 5095657 , at * 8 (M.D. Pa. Aug. 22, 2019) , report and recommendation adopted , No. 3:18-CV-1885, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 156988 , 2019 WL 4391444 (M.D. Pa. Sept. 13, 2019) . "Indeed, courts have generally declined to resolve excessive force claims at the pleading stage." Ianuale v. New Jersey , No. CV 18-03069 (GC) (JBD), 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 144812 , 2025 WL 2124568 , at * 10 (D.N.J. July 29, 2025) . Additional information beyond these pleadings is required to determine the extent of Hughes's act and to evaluate its reasonableness in light of the circumstances. It will therefore be recommended that with respect to the Fourth Amendment claim, Defendant's Motion to Dismiss be granted as to Deputy Chelland and denied as to Defendant Hughes. 2. Fifth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff states in his Complaint that he was "deprived of liberty without due process of law — arrested without probable cause against police protocol and laws of criminal procedures — Palmere/Spathelf." (Doc. 46, p. 22). Defendants respond by stating that a Fifth Amendment claim against Deputies Chelland and Hughes cannot stand, as that Amendment "applies only to actions of the federal government." Hammerstone v. Solebury Twp. , No. CIV. A. 94-4515, 1994 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 15755 , 1994 WL 612794 , at * 4 (E.D. Pa. Nov. 7, 1994) . In Plaintiff's reply brief, he does not respond to Defendants' argument, and only alludes to his Fourth , Sixth , and Fourteenth Amendment claims. While it is not clear that Plaintiff even attempted to make a Fifth Amendment claim specifically against the two Deputy Sheriffs, as he made no specific allegations against them in connection with this claim, in the case that the Complaint may be construed as such, it will be addressed below. The Fifth Amendment of the Constitution states, in part, "[n]o person shall . . . be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." U.S. Const. amend. V . Courts have long held "that the fifth amendment to the constitution of the United States is a limitation only upon the powers of the general government; that is, that the amendment operates solely on the constitution itself by qualifying the powers of the national government which the constitution called into being." Talton v. Mayes , 163 U.S. 376 , 382 , 16 S. Ct. 986 , 988 , 41 L. Ed. 196 (1896) . For this reason, a Fifth Amendment claim cannot be brought against a state official, and that claim will fail. See, e.g., Bergdoll v. City of York , 515 F. App'x 165 , 170 (3d Cir. 2013) (dismissing a Fifth Amendment claim against a police officer because he was not a federal official). Similarly here, Deputy Sheriffs Chelland and Hughes are Lackawanna County Sheriffs. Because they are not federal officials, Plaintiff's Fifth Amendment claim against them as such must fail. However, reading Plaintiff's Complaint in a liberal fashion, it could be understood that he was attempting to assert a due process claim for the excessive force allegations. While Plaintiff's due process claim will be addressed in more detail infra , even if Plaintiff had erroneously cited the Fifth Amendment instead of the Fourteenth Amendment , or was asserting a claim under the Fifth Amendment made applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment , his claim would still fail. "Excessive force claims are actionable under the Fourth Amendment , not under the substantive due process clause ." Zielinski v. City of Easton , No. CIV.A. 09-2812, 2009 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 89123 , 2009 WL 3081617 , at * 4 (E.D. Pa. Sept. 24, 2009) (citing Graham , 490 U.S. at 394-95 ); see also Boguslavaskaya v. O'Neill , No. CIV.A. 09-6064, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 65619 , 2010 WL 2650420 , at * 3 (E.D. Pa. June 30, 2010) ("When facts suggest no excessive force outside an arrest, a claim under substantive due process is not facially plausible."). 3. Sixth Amendment Allegation The Sixth Amendment describes rights that are afforded to an accused in a criminal prosecution, including the right to be informed of the nature and cause of the accusation, to be confronted with the witnesses against him, and to have process for obtaining witnesses. As the text makes clear, this constitutional amendment applies primarily to defendants who are facing criminal charges. Accordingly, a person's Sixth Amendment rights attach once charges have been brought. Kirby v. Illinois , 406 U.S. 682 , 688 , 92 S. Ct. 1877 , 1882 , 32 L. Ed. 2d 411 (1972) . Any allegations surrounding police misconduct during Plaintiff's detention and arrest are therefore not relevant for Sixth Amendment purposes. With respect to Defendants Deputy Sheriffs, Plaintiff seems to assert that they violated his Sixth Amendment rights by disclosing "AI generated video footage not authentic body cam footage" (Doc. 46, ¶ 37). He further asserts that he was "deprived of witnesses favorable to the accused," including "authentic" body worn camera footage. ( Id. at pp. 22-23). However, Plaintiff's claim against the Deputy Sheriffs surrounding the alleged tampering with video footage fails at the start, as he has not alleged any personal involvement of Deputy Sheriff Chelland or Hughes. Plaintiff states repeatedly that " all 5 police officers listed tampered with the authentic body cam footage using recently released AI generative tools to create deepfakes" (Doc. 46, p. 18 at ¶ 39) (emphasis added). While a plain reading excludes the Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs from this allegation, Plaintiff goes one step further and adds that "the deputies weren't wearing [body-worn cameras]." (Doc. 74, ¶ 34). It is therefore not necessary to discuss whether manipulating body-camera footage with AI technology would constitute a Sixth Amendment violation, because Deputies Chelland and Hughes — per Plaintiff's pleadings — had no involvement in the alleged wrongdoing. See Rode v. Dellarciprete , 845 F.2d 1195 , 1207 (3d Cir. 1988) . 4. Fourteenth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff asserts a violation of his Due Process and Equal Protection rights in violation of the Fourteenth Amendment : "Denied priveleges [sic], immunites [sic], and equal protections of law - 1) unlawfully arrested 2) illegally detained and 3) a victim of police brutality. All law enforcement officers: unconstitutionally participated in an unlawful arrest using excessive force violating 4th , 5th , 14th Amendment . Denying me due process of law and equal protection of law that no state shall abridge the rights of a United States citizen[], nor shall I be deprived of my liberty and freedom." (Doc 46, p. 23, ¶ 4). From the Complaint, it is clear that the heart of his Fourteenth Amendment claim goes to the July 19, 2022, arrest and actions taken by law enforcement on that day. A due process analysis is unnecessary here. "The Supreme Court has held that when government behavior is governed by a specific constitutional amendment, due process analysis is inappropriate. Although not all actions by police officers are governed by the Fourth Amendment . . . the constitutionality of arrests by state officials is governed by the Fourth Amendment rather than due process analysis." Berg v. Cnty. of Allegheny , 219 F.3d 261 , 268-69 (3d Cir. 2000) . As discussed supra , challenging the constitutionality of force used during a stop or arrest is governed by the Fourth Amendment . See Graham , 490 U.S. at 394, 109 S. Ct. at 1871 (when an "excessive force claim arises in the context of an arrest or investigatory stop of a free citizen, it is most properly characterized as one invoking the protections of the Fourth Amendment "). Because the claims of excessive force and false arrest are cognizable under the Fourth Amendment , Plaintiff's Fourteenth Amendment due process claim fails. Bergdoll v. City of York , 515 F. App'x 165 , 170 (3d Cir. 2013) ; see also Mitchell v. Luckenbill , 680 F. Supp. 2d 672 , 685 (M.D. Pa. 2010) ("Because it is clear that the Fourth Amendment , as opposed to the Fourteenth Amendment , applies to Plaintiffs' excessive force claims, Plaintiffs' Fourteenth Amendment excessive force claims will be dismissed."). Lastly, Plaintiff alleges that he was denied equal protection of the law. (Doc. 46, p. 23). The Equal Protection Clause is a section of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution , which instructs that no state shall "deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws." U.S. Const. amend. XIV, § 1 . To prove a § 1983 equal protection case, Plaintiff must present facts that show "purposeful discrimination" and "demonstrate that they received different treatment from that received by other individuals similarly situated." Chambers ex rel. Chambers v. Sch. Dist. Of Philadelphia Bd of Educ. , 587 F.3d 176 , 196 (3d Cir. 2009) . This required alleging three elements: (1) Plaintiff was a member of a protected class; (2) Plaintiff was similarly situated to members of an unprotected class, and (3) Plaintiff was treated differently from members of that unprotected class. Green v. Chester Upland Sch. Dist. , 89 F. Supp. 3d 682 , 693 (E.D. Pa. 2015) , aff'd sub nom. A.G. v. Chester Upland Sch. Dist. , 655 F. App'x 125 (3d Cir. 2016) . Persons are considered similarly situated when they are alike "in all relevant aspects," although this does not require them to be identically situated. Suber v. Guinta , 902 F. Supp. 2d 591 , 607 (E.D. Pa. 2012) . At the initial pleading stage, plaintiff does not need to "name names" or identify "specific instances where others have been treated differently. Rather, a general allegation that plaintiff has been treated differently from others similarly situated will suffice." Tomino v. City of Bethlehem , No. 08-CV-06018, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32221 , 2010 WL 1348536 , at * 14 (E.D. Pa. Mar. 31, 2010) (citing Phillips v. County of Allegheny , 515 F.3d 224 , 245 (3d Cir. 2008) ). However, Plaintiff fails to provide any facts or argument to support his equal protection claim; in fact, stating that he was denied equal protection is the only basis for his claim. As the Defendant states, the Complaint "is devoid of any allegations that he was treated differently because of his membership in a suspect class, ... Wilson v. Spathelf, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 171256 DAVID MARK WILSON, Plaintiff, v. LAWRENCE SPATHELF, JR., ET AL., Defendants. Wilson v. Spathelf Appeal terminated, 01/06/2026 Leo A. Latella REPORT AND RECOMMENDATION I. Introduction The Plaintiff, David Mark Wilson, is a pretrial detainee currently confined at the Lackawanna County Prison. He is being held there in connection with a pending criminal prosecution in Lackawanna County on charges of aggravated assault, simple assault, resisting arrest, reckless endangerment, disorderly conduct, public drunkenness, and harassment. His criminal charges arise out of an incident that occurred in the City of Scranton on July 19, 2022, which are the same events forming the basis of this civil rights action pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1983 . Wilson is alleging excessive force on the part of five City of Scranton police officers and two Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs who arrested him on that day. He further brings suit against the prosecutor in the criminal case, alleging malicious prosecution and the public defender who was appointed to represent him, alleging ineffective assistance of counsel. In addition to Wilson's pleadings that are directed to this federal civil rights action, he has filed — inappropriately — many pleadings relevant only to his pending prosecution in Lackawanna County . The Scranton police officers filed an Answer with affirmative defenses in this action, while the Deputy Sheriffs, prosecutor, and public defender filed Motions to Dismiss. The Motions to Dismiss have been fully briefed and are ripe for disposition. For the reasons that follow, it will be recommended that the Deputy Sheriffs' Motion to Dismiss be granted in part and denied in part, and that the Motion to Dismiss filed by the prosecutor and public defender be granted. II. Procedural History The Plaintiff initiated this pro se civil rights action by filing a Complaint pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1983 on October 3, 2023, alleging, inter alia , that his July 2022 arrest was unlawful and that the officers involved — Scranton Police Department Officers Lawrence Spathelf, Leland Palmere, Isaac Troutman, Mark Miller, Timothy Merkel, and Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs Jeff Hughes and Patrick Chelland — used excessive force. He further alleges "Abuse of a Prisoner" and "Abuse of Powers and Authority: Oppression" against Assistant District Attorney Danielle Guari . (Doc. 1, p. 6). The filing fee was paid on October 17, 2023, and the Complaint was deemed filed. On November 13, 2023, Magistrate Judge Schwab conducted a preliminary review of the complaint in accordance with 28 U.S.C. § 1915A . In that Order, Judge Schwab held that Wilson had failed to state a false arrest claim on which relief can be granted as to Officer Palmere (Doc. 9, pp. 8-10) and that Plaintiff did not describe any conduct allegedly taken by the other defendants against him. Wilson was granted leave to amend along with additional guidance surrounding the claims that he was attempting to pursue. On December 4, 2023, Wilson filed an amended complaint, adding Assistant Public Defender Joseph Kalinowski ; however, the Amended Complaint failed to correct the pleading deficiencies noted by Judge Schwab and contained many of the same allegations—supported by conclusory statements but as little substance as the original Complaint. ( See generally Doc. 15). All Defendants filed motions to dismiss the Amended Complaint. After the motions were briefed, Plaintiff filed a Second Amended Complaint on June 17, 2024, (Doc. 46), mooting the then outstanding motions. ( See Doc. 48). The Second Amended Complaint is now the operative pleading document. In response to the Second Amended Complaint, Defendants Deputy Sheriff Chelland and Hughes filed a Motion to Dismiss on July 1, 2024, (Doc. 52), and supporting brief on July 15, 2024. (Doc. 56). Plaintiff filed his Brief in Opposition on August 8, 2024, (Doc. 74), and Defendants submitted their reply brief on August 22, 2024. (Doc. 79). Defendants Guari and Kalinowski filed a Motion to Dismiss on July 10, 2024, (Doc. 53) and supporting brief on July 24, 2024. (Doc. 60). Plaintiff filed his Brief in Opposition on August 8, 2024, (Doc. 75). A reply brief was not filed. The Scranton Police Officers filed an Answer with affirmative defenses on July 31, 2024. (Doc. 67). Each of the Defendants' Motions to Dismiss will be addressed in turn. III. Background The Second Amended Complaint (hereinafter referred to as the "Complaint") consists of a detailed recounting of Plaintiff's version of the July 19, 2022, events, and is summarized as follows. According to Plaintiff, Scranton Police Department Officers Palmere and Spathelf were dispatched to 456 Laurel Street due to complaints of a disorderly male believed to be under the influence of methamphetamine. (Doc. 46, ¶ 1-2). Once at the scene, the officers encountered David Wilson wearing only boxer shorts and flip-flops. From there, Wilson alleges that he raised his hands and repeatedly asked Officer Palmere, "What's the problem?" and "Am I under arrest?" ( Id. at ¶ 2). Officer Palmere allegedly remained still and did not answer. Wilson avers that he approached Palmere and asked Palmere if he could lift his sunglasses, out of "concern for the officer," to which Palmere remained unmoving and did not answer. ( Id. at ¶ 5). Wilson states that he then got closer to Palmere, and after being unable to see Palmere's eyes through the sunglasses, he told Palmere that he was "just going to lift your sunglasses, sir, to make sure you're okay." ( Id. at ¶¶ 6-7). Palmere allegedly stood still and "allowed [Wilson] to proceed without opposing or asking [him] to desist. He said no words." ( Id. ) Wilson avers that he proceeded to "gently" lift the sunglasses up, made eye contact with Officer Palmere, and placed the sunglasses back down, apologizing. Palmere allegedly continued to remain still and unmoving. ( Id. at ¶¶ 8-9). After this, according to Wilson's telling, Officer Palmere stormed at Wilson with no warning and took him to the ground. ( Id. at ¶¶ 9-10). Wilson describes this scuffle, stating, "in an attempt to break the fall or position myself to a comfortable position without resistance my hand involuntarily ended up on the butt of Palmere's weapon." ( Id. at ¶ 10). Wilson further concedes that he cursed at Palmere and stated, "You're not getting your gun" as he continued to hold onto the weapon. ( Id. at ¶ 12). At this point, Wilson describes how he was on the ground and in great discomfort, due to both the force used against him and his skin being in contact with the hot asphalt. Officer Spathelf arrived on the scene, and Wilson alleges that "without cause," Spathelf "shot" him with a taser and a stun gun. ( Id. at ¶¶ 20-21). More law enforcement arrived at that point: Officer Troutman purportedly "stepped on [Wilson's] left foot holding it in place against the hot pavement," and Deputy Sheriff Chelland "briefly stepped on [Wilson's] right foot." ( Id. at ¶¶ 26-27). At some point soon after, Wilson alleges that Deputy Sheriff Hughes "stepped on his left foot holding it in place," Officer Troutman did the same to his right foot, and Officer Palmere then allegedly dropped his full body weight onto Wilson. ( Id. at ¶¶ 31-32). Wilson contends that at some point throughout this interaction, Officer Merkel held him down against the ground ( id. at ¶ 38) and that Officer Miller grabbed Wilson by his throat. ( Id. at ¶ 40). Wilson alleges that the "violations" against him did not stop upon his arrest: "All 5 police officers listed tampered with the authentic body cam footage using recently released AI generative tools to create deepfakes to try and cover up their criminal acts of police brutality, willful misconduct, excessive force, following a [sic] unconstitutional 'arrest.'" ( Id. at p. 18, ¶ 39). Officers Palmere and Spathelf also allegedly lied on the probable cause affidavit and "at [his] habeas hearing" held on February 12, 2024. ( Id. at p. 18, ¶¶ 40-42). With respect to the attorneys involved in his criminal case, he accuses ADA Danielle Guari of manipulating Wilson into waving his preliminary hearing, using generative AI videos, and moving forward with the case knowing that the officers had used excessive force against him. ( Id. at ¶ 46, 49). He accuses his court-appointed attorney, Public Defender Joseph Kalinowski , of "protecting criminals of the govt. [sic] agencies of the state and city," and seemingly withholding the body-worn camera footage from Wilson and only sharing the "generative AI" videos. ( Id. at ¶¶ 47-48, 51-52). Wilson brings claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 for violations of the Fourth Amendment , Fifth Amendment , Sixth Amendment , and Fourteenth Amendment . He also alleges violations of the Exclusionary Rule, Brady Rule, and "Violating Generative AI Core Values to Deceive Jury." ( Id. at pp. 22-23). As to relief, he requests criminal charges be brought against each defendant; a criminal investigation be launched on AI generated videos; that he be compensated for his injuries; that he be given the right to access authentic eyewitness videos; that he be allowed to subpoena various experts; that effective counsel be appointed; and lastly, that a suppression hearing be held. ( Id. at pp. 22, 25). IV. Standard of Review Rule 8 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure states that a claim must include "a short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief." Fed. R. Civ. P. 8(a) . This requires the Plaintiff to put forth evidence that, "when taken as true, suggest[s] the required elements of a particular legal theory." McNeilly v. City of Pittsburgh , 40 F.Supp.3d 643 , 650 (W.D. Pa. 2014) . The Plaintiff must state "sufficient factual allegations which 'nudge' its claims 'across the line from conceivable to plausible.'" Id. (quoting Ashcroft v. Iqbal , 556 U.S. 662 , 680 , 129 S. Ct. 1937 , 1951 , 173 L. Ed. 2d 868 (2009) ). The purpose is to ensure "fair notice of the factual basis of a claim while raising a 'reasonable expectation that discovery will reveal evidence of the necessary element.'" Id. at 651 . Rule 12(b)(6) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure allows a defendant to move to dismiss a complaint for "failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted." Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) . This means that a complaint may be dismissed if it does not present sufficient facts to support a plausible claim: "Under Rule 12(b)(6) , a motion to dismiss may be granted only if, accepting all well-pleaded allegations in the complaint as true and viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff, a court finds that plaintiff's claims lack facial plausibility." Warren Gen. Hosp. v. Amgen, Inc. , 643 F.3d 77 , 84 (3d Cir. 2011) (citing Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly , 550 U.S. 544 , 555-56 , 127 S. Ct. 1955 , 167 L. Ed. 2d 929 (2007) ). The court must accept both the Plaintiff's allegations and any reasonable inferences that can be drawn as true and construe them in the light most favorable to the non-moving party. Monroe v. Beard , 536 F.3d 198 , 205 (3d Cir. 2008) . However, it need not accept "'unsupported conclusions and unwarranted inferences, or a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.'" Morrow v. Balaski , 719 F.3d 160 , 165 (3d Cir. 2013) (quoting Baraka v. McGreevey , 481 F.3d 187 , 195 (3d Cir. 2007) ). Reciting the elements that make up a cause of action and supporting them with "mere conclusory statements" is insufficient to give those statements the presumption of truth. McNeilly , 40 F.Supp.3d at 650 (citing Iqbal , 556 U.S. at 678 , 129 S.Ct. 1937 ). When faced with a motion to dismiss pursuant to 12(b)(6) , a court should conduct a two-part analysis: 1) separate the factual and legal elements of a claim; and 2) "determine whether the facts alleged in the complaint are sufficient to show that the plaintiff has a plausible claim for relief." Id. (internal citation removed). The court may consider facts alleged on the face of the complaint, as well as "documents incorporated into the complaint by reference, and matters of which a court may take judicial notice." Tellabs, Inc. v. Makor Issues & Rights, Ltd. , 551 U.S. 308 , 322 , 127 S. Ct. 2499 , 168 L. Ed. 2d 179 (2007) . This analysis requires the court to look at the claims on a case-by-case basis and rely both "on its judicial experience and common sense." McNeilly , 40 F.Supp.3d at 650 . The court will not be reaching legal conclusions, but instead is merely determining "whether the plaintiff should be permitted to offer evidence in support of the allegations." Id. (citing Maio v. Aetna , 221 F.3d 472 , 482 (3d Cir. 2000) ). Furthermore, a pro se complaint, "however inartfully pleaded," must be held to "less stringent standards than formal pleadings drafted by lawyers" and can only be dismissed for failure to state a claim if it appears beyond doubt that the plaintiff can prove no set of facts in support a viable claim for relief. Haines v. Kerner , 404 U.S. 519 , 520-21 , 92 S. Ct. 594 , 30 L. Ed. 2d 652 (1972) . V. Discussion A. The Motion to Dismiss for Defendant Deputy Sheriffs Plaintiff has averred that the Defendant Deputy Sheriffs violated his Fourth Amendment , Fifth Amendment , Sixth Amendment , and Fourteenth Amendment rights, primarily stemming from the excessive force allegation. Each will be addressed in turn. While Wilson has failed to state a claim on which relief can be granted as to Chelland, he has stated a viable claim as to Hughes. It will therefore be recommended that the Deputy Sheriffs' Motion to Dismiss be granted in part and denied in part. 1. Fourth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff alleges that both Deputy Sheriffs violated his Fourth Amendment rights by using excessive force against him during his arrest on July 19, 2022. The allegations are that Deputy Chelland "briefly stepped on [his] right foot: unreasonably" (Doc. 46, ¶ 27); Deputy Hughes "stepped on [his] left foot holding it in place as Palmere approached" ( id. at ¶ 31); and Deputy Hughes "caused unnecessary injuries due to excessive force" to his left foot. ( Id. at ¶ 33). In the Brief in Support of their Motion to Dismiss, Defendants argue that de minimis force is insufficient to support a claim for excessive force in violation of the Fourth Amendment . (Doc. 56, pp. 5-10). In his response, Plaintiff attempts to assert a new claim, namely that "both Deputy Sheriffs condoned the use of excessive force [by] failing to intervene." (Doc. 74, ¶ 5). The Defendants responded by correctly arguing that Plaintiff had not alleged failure to intervene in the Complaint, and that the claim should therefore should not be considered. (Doc. 79, p. 2). Plaintiff asserts that Defendants Deputy Sheriffs failed to intervene in the excessive force committed by the Scranton Police Officers for the first time in his Brief in Opposition. "[I]t is axiomatic that the complaint may not be amended by the briefs in opposition to a motion to dismiss." Com. of Pa. ex rel. Zimmerman v. PepsiCo, Inc. , 836 F.2d 173 , 181 (3d Cir. 1988) (quoting Car Carriers, Inc. v. Ford Motor Co. , 745 F.2d 1101 , 1107 (7th Cir. 1984) ). See also Frederico v. Home Depot , 507 F.3d 188 , 201-02 (a court will "not consider after-the-fact allegations in determining the sufficiency of [the] complaint" under Rule 12(b)(6) ). Therefore, "the new legal claims alleged in the brief in opposition . . . cannot be considered when evaluating the sufficiency of the allegations in Plaintiff's complaint at the motion to dismiss stage." Poindexter v. Starbucks York Roasting Plant , No. 1:21-CV-1847, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 7159 , 2023 WL 185096 , at * 5 (M.D. Pa. Jan. 13, 2023) . See also Motes v. United States , No. 1:23-CV-00629, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 127574 , 2025 WL 1870919 , at * 4 (M.D. Pa. July 7, 2025) (refusing to consider a new claim raised in a brief in opposition and recognizing that "Under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 8 , a complaint must adequately give defendants notice of the claims against them and the relevant theories of liability."). For this reason, only the excessive force claim will be considered for purposes of the Motion to Dismiss, and the claim of failure to intervene will not be evaluated. The crux of Plaintiff's relevant excessive force claims is that both Deputy Sheriffs stepped on his foot. As fully explained below, because Plaintiff only alleged that Chelland briefly stepped on his foot, Plaintiff has not sufficiently alleged an excessive force claim as to Chelland. His allegations against Hughes, while marginal, are a bit more robust, and the allegations just cross the line to survive a motion to dismiss. In the landmark case Graham v. Connor , the Supreme Court established that the standard for evaluating an excessive force claim is objective reasonableness under the Fourth Amendment . 490 U.S. 386 , 395 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 , 1871 , 104 L. Ed. 2d 443 (1989) . Law enforcement may only use the force reasonably necessary to effectuate an arrest. Garrison v. Porch , 376 F. App'x 274 , 277 (3d Cir. 2010) (citing Tennessee v. Garner , 471 U.S. 1 , 9-10 , 105 S. Ct. 1694 , 85 L.Ed.2d 1 (1985) ). If the force used exceeded what was reasonably necessary, the arrestee may bring suit under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 . Id. Similarly, "[t]o state a claim for excessive force as an unreasonable seizure under the Fourth Amendment , a plaintiff must show that a 'seizure' occurred and that it was unreasonable." Abraham v. Raso , 183 F.3d 279 , 288 (3d Cir. 1999) (citing Brower v. County of Inyo , 489 U.S. 593 , 599 , 109 S. Ct. 1378 , 1382-83 , 103 L.Ed.2d 628 (1989) ). Determining reasonableness requires looking at the totality of the circumstances, including the severity of the alleged crime, whether the suspect posed a threat to others, or whether the suspect resisted arrest or tried to flee. Noble v. City of Camden , 112 F. Supp. 3d 208 , 227 (D.N.J. 2015) (quoting Graham , 490 U.S. at 396 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 ). For these cases — indeed, for all civil rights claims — the plaintiff must assert personal involvement for each named defendant. Millbrook v. United States , 8 F. Supp. 3d 601 , 613 (M.D. Pa. 2014) . A civil rights claim cannot be premised solely on a theory of respondeat superior . Id. The force that is in question "must be more than a de minimus amount of force for a constitutional claim to arise." Smith v. Cpl. Louriero , No. 3:16-CV-0741, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 89856 , 2017 WL 2952819 , at * 5 (M.D. Pa. June 9, 2017) , report and recommendation adopted sub nom. Smith v. Louriero , No. 3:16-CV-0741, 2017 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 105027 , 2017 WL 2926020 (M.D. Pa. July 7, 2017) (citing Ingraham v. Wright , 430 U.S. 651 , 674 , 97 S. Ct. 1401 , 51 L. Ed. 2d 711 (1977) ). If the touching by law enforcement is found to be "minimal and routine," that fails to support an excessive force claim: such use of force cannot be said to be objectively unreasonable. Garcia v. Cnty. of Bucks, PA , 155 F. Supp. 2d 259 , 265 (E.D. Pa. 2001) . On the other hand, a plaintiff is not obligated to show injury in order to prove excessive force. "[A] § 1983 plaintiff may suffer a constitutional injury (i.e., an infringement of his right to be free from an unreasonable seizure) without suffering a physical injury." Velius v. Twp. of Hamilton , 754 F. Supp. 2d 689 , 692 (D.N.J. 2010) , aff'd , 466 F. App'x 133 (3d Cir. 2012) . See also Flood v. Schaefer , 439 F. App'x 179 , 182 (3d Cir. 2011) ("in evaluating claims of excessive force, the central issue is the force the officers employed (and whether it was reasonable under the circumstances), not the injury they caused"). With these principles in mind, the inquiry is whether the Plaintiff has alleged sufficient actions taken by each individual Deputy Sheriffs, and whether those actions were objectively unreasonable to the point that they violated Plaintiff's constitutional rights. Viewing the allegations in the light most favorable to the Plaintiff, which is the standard at the motion to dismiss stage, the Deputy Sheriffs arrived on the scene while a physical altercation was occurring between Plaintiff and a law enforcement officer that resulted in Plaintiff getting hold of Officer Palmere's firearm. (Doc. 46, ¶¶ 10-16). Plaintiff's Complaint describes a chaotic scene, and it is with that environment in mind that reasonableness will be evaluated, not with the 20/20 vision of hindsight. Graham , 490 U.S. at 396-97 , 109 S. Ct. 1865 , 1872 ("The calculus of reasonableness must embody allowance for the fact that police officers are often forced to make split-second judgments—in circumstances that are tense, uncertain, and rapidly evolving—about the amount of force that is necessary in a particular situation."). With respect to Deputy Sheriff Chelland, even Plaintiff's own description of the event allege that the touching was, if not inadvertent, at least de minimis : "As [Chelland] was listening to Palmere's story [he] briefly stepped on my foot." Plaintiff does assert that this was done "unreasonably" or forcefully. Even taking Plaintiff's allegation in the light most favorable to him, the facts as stated do not present a claim of excessive force. Given the circumstances surrounding the "stepping" and the fact that it was "brief" supports this conclusion. Plaintiff fails to state a Fourth Amendment excessive force claim as to Defendant Chelland. It will therefore be recommended that the claim against Chelland be dismissed, but, considering his Plaintiff's pro se status, he should be given leave to amend his Complaint but only as to the Fourth Amendment excessive force claim against Deputy Sheriff Chelland. Plaintiff should be reminded that "'[n]ot every push or shove, even if it may later seem unnecessary in the peace of a judge's chambers,' violates the Fourth Amendment ." Graham , 490 U.S. at 386 (internal citation omitted). Deputy Sheriff Hughes's case is less straightforward. Plaintiff asserts that Hughes stepped on his left foot to hold it in place while Palmere approached and continued his attempts to restrain Wilson. The Defendant argues that Plaintiff has failed "to allege with any specificity what injury to his left foot he allegedly sustained" (Doc. 56, p. 9) yet, as discussed above, this is not a required element of an excessive force claim. At this juncture, dismissal of Plaintiff's Fourth Amendment claim as to Hughes would be premature. An excessive force claim is "multi-facetted" and "fact-specific," and Plaintiff has pleaded a sufficient claim to allow it to proceed to the discovery phase. Velardo v. Lewko , No. 3:18-CV-1885, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 143792 , 2019 WL 5095657 , at * 8 (M.D. Pa. Aug. 22, 2019) , report and recommendation adopted , No. 3:18-CV-1885, 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 156988 , 2019 WL 4391444 (M.D. Pa. Sept. 13, 2019) . "Indeed, courts have generally declined to resolve excessive force claims at the pleading stage." Ianuale v. New Jersey , No. CV 18-03069 (GC) (JBD), 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 144812 , 2025 WL 2124568 , at * 10 (D.N.J. July 29, 2025) . Additional information beyond these pleadings is required to determine the extent of Hughes's act and to evaluate its reasonableness in light of the circumstances. It will therefore be recommended that with respect to the Fourth Amendment claim, Defendant's Motion to Dismiss be granted as to Deputy Chelland and denied as to Defendant Hughes. 2. Fifth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff states in his Complaint that he was "deprived of liberty without due process of law — arrested without probable cause against police protocol and laws of criminal procedures — Palmere/Spathelf." (Doc. 46, p. 22). Defendants respond by stating that a Fifth Amendment claim against Deputies Chelland and Hughes cannot stand, as that Amendment "applies only to actions of the federal government." Hammerstone v. Solebury Twp. , No. CIV. A. 94-4515, 1994 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 15755 , 1994 WL 612794 , at * 4 (E.D. Pa. Nov. 7, 1994) . In Plaintiff's reply brief, he does not respond to Defendants' argument, and only alludes to his Fourth , Sixth , and Fourteenth Amendment claims. While it is not clear that Plaintiff even attempted to make a Fifth Amendment claim specifically against the two Deputy Sheriffs, as he made no specific allegations against them in connection with this claim, in the case that the Complaint may be construed as such, it will be addressed below. The Fifth Amendment of the Constitution states, in part, "[n]o person shall . . . be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." U.S. Const. amend. V . Courts have long held "that the fifth amendment to the constitution of the United States is a limitation only upon the powers of the general government; that is, that the amendment operates solely on the constitution itself by qualifying the powers of the national government which the constitution called into being." Talton v. Mayes , 163 U.S. 376 , 382 , 16 S. Ct. 986 , 988 , 41 L. Ed. 196 (1896) . For this reason, a Fifth Amendment claim cannot be brought against a state official, and that claim will fail. See, e.g., Bergdoll v. City of York , 515 F. App'x 165 , 170 (3d Cir. 2013) (dismissing a Fifth Amendment claim against a police officer because he was not a federal official). Similarly here, Deputy Sheriffs Chelland and Hughes are Lackawanna County Sheriffs. Because they are not federal officials, Plaintiff's Fifth Amendment claim against them as such must fail. However, reading Plaintiff's Complaint in a liberal fashion, it could be understood that he was attempting to assert a due process claim for the excessive force allegations. While Plaintiff's due process claim will be addressed in more detail infra , even if Plaintiff had erroneously cited the Fifth Amendment instead of the Fourteenth Amendment , or was asserting a claim under the Fifth Amendment made applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment , his claim would still fail. "Excessive force claims are actionable under the Fourth Amendment , not under the substantive due process clause ." Zielinski v. City of Easton , No. CIV.A. 09-2812, 2009 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 89123 , 2009 WL 3081617 , at * 4 (E.D. Pa. Sept. 24, 2009) (citing Graham , 490 U.S. at 394-95 ); see also Boguslavaskaya v. O'Neill , No. CIV.A. 09-6064, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 65619 , 2010 WL 2650420 , at * 3 (E.D. Pa. June 30, 2010) ("When facts suggest no excessive force outside an arrest, a claim under substantive due process is not facially plausible."). 3. Sixth Amendment Allegation The Sixth Amendment describes rights that are afforded to an accused in a criminal prosecution, including the right to be informed of the nature and cause of the accusation, to be confronted with the witnesses against him, and to have process for obtaining witnesses. As the text makes clear, this constitutional amendment applies primarily to defendants who are facing criminal charges. Accordingly, a person's Sixth Amendment rights attach once charges have been brought. Kirby v. Illinois , 406 U.S. 682 , 688 , 92 S. Ct. 1877 , 1882 , 32 L. Ed. 2d 411 (1972) . Any allegations surrounding police misconduct during Plaintiff's detention and arrest are therefore not relevant for Sixth Amendment purposes. With respect to Defendants Deputy Sheriffs, Plaintiff seems to assert that they violated his Sixth Amendment rights by disclosing "AI generated video footage not authentic body cam footage" (Doc. 46, ¶ 37). He further asserts that he was "deprived of witnesses favorable to the accused," including "authentic" body worn camera footage. ( Id. at pp. 22-23). However, Plaintiff's claim against the Deputy Sheriffs surrounding the alleged tampering with video footage fails at the start, as he has not alleged any personal involvement of Deputy Sheriff Chelland or Hughes. Plaintiff states repeatedly that " all 5 police officers listed tampered with the authentic body cam footage using recently released AI generative tools to create deepfakes" (Doc. 46, p. 18 at ¶ 39) (emphasis added). While a plain reading excludes the Lackawanna County Deputy Sheriffs from this allegation, Plaintiff goes one step further and adds that "the deputies weren't wearing [body-worn cameras]." (Doc. 74, ¶ 34). It is therefore not necessary to discuss whether manipulating body-camera footage with AI technology would constitute a Sixth Amendment violation, because Deputies Chelland and Hughes — per Plaintiff's pleadings — had no involvement in the alleged wrongdoing. See Rode v. Dellarciprete , 845 F.2d 1195 , 1207 (3d Cir. 1988) . 4. Fourteenth Amendment Allegation Plaintiff asserts a violation of his Due Process and Equal Protection rights in violation of the Fourteenth Amendment : "Denied priveleges [sic], immunites [sic], and equal protections of law - 1) unlawfully arrested 2) illegally detained and 3) a victim of police brutality. All law enforcement officers: unconstitutionally participated in an unlawful arrest using excessive force violating 4th , 5th , 14th Amendment . Denying me due process of law and equal protection of law that no state shall abridge the rights of a United States citizen[], nor shall I be deprived of my liberty and freedom." (Doc 46, p. 23, ¶ 4). From the Complaint, it is clear that the heart of his Fourteenth Amendment claim goes to the July 19, 2022, arrest and actions taken by law enforcement on that day. A due process analysis is unnecessary here. "The Supreme Court has held that when government behavior is governed by a specific constitutional amendment, due process analysis is inappropriate. Although not all actions by police officers are governed by the Fourth Amendment . . . the constitutionality of arrests by state officials is governed by the Fourth Amendment rather than due process analysis." Berg v. Cnty. of Allegheny , 219 F.3d 261 , 268-69 (3d Cir. 2000) . As discussed supra , challenging the constitutionality of force used during a stop or arrest is governed by the Fourth Amendment . See Graham , 490 U.S. at 394, 109 S. Ct. at 1871 (when an "excessive force claim arises in the context of an arrest or investigatory stop of a free citizen, it is most properly characterized as one invoking the protections of the Fourth Amendment "). Because the claims of excessive force and false arrest are cognizable under the Fourth Amendment , Plaintiff's Fourteenth Amendment due process claim fails. Bergdoll v. City of York , 515 F. App'x 165 , 170 (3d Cir. 2013) ; see also Mitchell v. Luckenbill , 680 F. Supp. 2d 672 , 685 (M.D. Pa. 2010) ("Because it is clear that the Fourth Amendment , as opposed to the Fourteenth Amendment , applies to Plaintiffs' excessive force claims, Plaintiffs' Fourteenth Amendment excessive force claims will be dismissed."). Lastly, Plaintiff alleges that he was denied equal protection of the law. (Doc. 46, p. 23). The Equal Protection Clause is a section of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution , which instructs that no state shall "deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws." U.S. Const. amend. XIV, § 1 . To prove a § 1983 equal protection case, Plaintiff must present facts that show "purposeful discrimination" and "demonstrate that they received different treatment from that received by other individuals similarly situated." Chambers ex rel. Chambers v. Sch. Dist. Of Philadelphia Bd of Educ. , 587 F.3d 176 , 196 (3d Cir. 2009) . This required alleging three elements: (1) Plaintiff was a member of a protected class; (2) Plaintiff was similarly situated to members of an unprotected class, and (3) Plaintiff was treated differently from members of that unprotected class. Green v. Chester Upland Sch. Dist. , 89 F. Supp. 3d 682 , 693 (E.D. Pa. 2015) , aff'd sub nom. A.G. v. Chester Upland Sch. Dist. , 655 F. App'x 125 (3d Cir. 2016) . Persons are considered similarly situated when they are alike "in all relevant aspects," although this does not require them to be identically situated. Suber v. Guinta , 902 F. Supp. 2d 591 , 607 (E.D. Pa. 2012) . At the initial pleading stage, plaintiff does not need to "name names" or identify "specific instances where others have been treated differently. Rather, a general allegation that plaintiff has been treated differently from others similarly situated will suffice." Tomino v. City of Bethlehem , No. 08-CV-06018, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 32221 , 2010 WL 1348536 , at * 14 (E.D. Pa. Mar. 31, 2010) (citing Phillips v. County of Allegheny , 515 F.3d 224 , 245 (3d Cir. 2008) ). However, Plaintiff fails to provide any facts or argument to support his equal protection claim; in fact, stating that he was denied equal protection is the o ['Copyright', 'Publicity/Likeness'] ['Bell Atlantic Corp', 'Ross Intelligence', 'Violating Generative AI']
