Wang OPINION & ORDER RE: MONACO DEPOSITION ONA T. WANG , United States Magistrate Judge : The Court is in receipt of ECF Nos. 1220, 1267, 1312, 1314, and 1337. This Opinion and Order addresses only issues arising from the Rule 30(b)(6) [and Rule 30(b)(1) ] deposition of OpenAI 's designee (and current employee) John Vincent "Vinnie" Monaco. Mr. Monaco was OpenAI 's designated witness on three 30(b)(6) topics related to regurgitation and "Project Giraffe." See ECF 1274-1 at 2. Plaintiffs seek additional discovery in the form of additional deposition time of Mr. Monaco , collection and production of additional discovery, and sanctions under Rule 37(a)(5) . As discussed below, the Court finds that Mr. Monaco was not sufficiently prepared for his 30(b)(6) deposition, and that the pattern of objections and Mr. Monaco 's resulting answers impeded, delayed and frustrated the fair examination of OpenAI, the deponent. Plaintiffs' motion to compel additional discovery is GRANTED : the parties have agreed on additional deposition time of 3.5 hours for Mr. Monaco , and the Court directs the parties to meet and confer on additional discovery-based remedies in light of the parties' recent agreements concerning depositions and deposition time overall and the Court's guidance at the March 10, 2026 conference and defers, without prejudice, ruling on the nature of any sanction or other discovery remedy at this time. I. BACKGROUND The Court had previously addressed disputes regarding proposed 30(b)(6) topics for OpenAI 's deposition(s). The topics on which Mr. Monaco was designated to testify were not among those disputed topics. (ECF Nos. 1312, 1314). Mr. Monaco testified in his capacity as a 30(b)(6) witness on January 27, 2026, and testified as to his personal knowledge under Rule 30(b)(1) on January 28, 2026. Plaintiffs assert that OpenAI disclosed certain documents too late for Plaintiffs to prepare for Mr. Monaco 's 30(b)(1) deposition, and that Mr. Monaco was not sufficiently prepared for his 30(b)(6) deposition. (See, e.g., ECF 1312 at 4, testifying that he could not estimate how many evaluations OpenAI performed on its filter, could not confirm whether similar evaluations were conducted for API conversation data, could not identify the scope of content included in the filter beyond "the first few sentences of [REDACTED]," could not determine whether News Plaintiffs' or New York Times content was included in the filter, and acknowledged only that such inclusion was [REDACTED]). II. DISCUSSION When an organization, such as a corporation, partnership, or association, is issued a Rule 30(b)(6) deposition notice, the organization will designate an individual or individuals to "testify on its behalf ... about information known or reasonably available to the organization." Fed. R. Civ. P. 30(b)(6) . As a result, the organization has a duty to prepare its designated witness to answer questions to the best of the organization's knowledge. S.E.C. v. Morelli , 143 F.R.D. 42 , 45 (S.D.N.Y. 1992) ; see Fashion Exch. LLC v. Hybrid Promotions, LLC , No. 14-CV-1254 (SHS) (OTW), 2019 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 60749 , 2019 WL 1533212 , at * 1 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 9, 2019) . "It is well settled, therefore, that ' Rule 30(b)(6) deponents need not have personal knowledge concerning the matters set out in the deposition notice'...." Bush v. Element Fin. Corp. , No. 16-CV-1007 (RJS), 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 189319 , 2016 WL 8814347 , at * 2 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 13, 2016) (quoting Spanski Enterprises, Inc. v. Telewizja Polska, S.A. , No. 07-CV-930 (GEL), 2009 WL 3270794 , at * 3 (S.D.N.Y. Oct. 13, 2009) ). As relevant here, "the corporate deponent has an affirmative duty to make available" a person able "to give complete, knowledgeable and binding answers on its behalf." Reilly v. NatWest Markets Grp. Inc. , 181 F.3d 253 , 268 (2d Cir. 1999) (internal quotation marks omitted). If the designated witness lacks "personal knowledge concerning the matters set out in the deposition notice," then "the corporation is obligated to prepare them so that they may give knowledgeable answers." Spanski Enters., Inc. v. Telewizja Polska , 07-civ-930 (ALC) (GWG), 2009 WL 3270794 , at * 3 (S.D.N.Y. Oct. 13, 2009) ; see, e.g. , Eid v. Koninkliike Luchtvaart Maatschappii N.V. , 310 F.R.D. 226 , 228 (S.D.N.Y. 2015) (noting that an organization "must make a conscientious good faith endeavor ... to prepare [its designated] persons in order that they can answer fully, completely, [and] unevasively, the questions posed ... as to the relevant subject matters" (internal quotation marks omitted)). Coty Inc. v. Excell Brands, LLC , No. 15-CV-7029 (JMF), 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 170817 , 2016 WL 7187630 , at * 2 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 9, 2016) . See also Eid v. Koninklijke Luchtvaart Maatschappij N.V. , 310 F.R.D. 226 , 228 (S.D.N.Y. 2015) . (while a 30(b)(6) witness is not expected to know and testify about every detail, organization is still obligated to make "conscientious good faith endeavor" to prepare witness to "answer fully, completely, [and] unevasively, the questions posed ... as to the relevant subject matters"). While OpenAI is correct that adequate preparation "need only be reasonable and performed in good faith," (ECF 1267 at 3), that preparation "must prepare the designee to the extent matters are reasonably available, whether from documents, past employees, or other sources." Bank of New York v. Meridien BIAO Bank Tanzania Ltd. , 171 F.R.D. 135 , 151 (S.D.N.Y. 1997) . Crucially here, a 30(b)(6) designee's testimony binds the organization, and if a deponent answers on behalf of the organization that it cannot remember a certain fact, that inability to remember is imputed to the organization but may not invalidate the deposition, if the witness's preparation was adequate. See Fashion Exch. LLC at *1 (emphasis added). If a party complains that a 30(b)(6) deponent provided insufficient answers, the Court must consider whether the lack of knowledge in the answers is a result of insufficient preparation. If the Court finds that the preparation was sufficient, an organization could find themselves "bound by [their] profession[s] of ignorance." Briese Lichttechnik Vertriebs GmbH v. Langton , 272 F.R.D. 369 , 375-76 (S.D.N.Y. 2011) . Thus, there should be a strong incentive for organizations to sufficiently prepare their 30(b)(6) designees. "A district court has wide discretion in sanctioning a party for discovery abuses," whether acting pursuant to its inherent power or Rule 37 . Reilly , 181 F.3d at 267 . If a party fails to comply with a court order to produce a competent Rule 30(b)(6) witness, Rule 37 allows the court to impose sanctions. Coty Inc. , 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 170817 , 2016 WL 7187630 , at * 2 . Rule 37 gives the Court discretion to "issue further just orders," including such sanctions as "directing that the matters embraced in the order or other designated facts be taken as established for purposes of the action, as the prevailing party claims," or "prohibiting the disobedient party from supporting or opposing designated claims or defenses." Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(b)(2)(A) . Additionally, "the court must order the disobedient party ... to pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney's fees, caused by the failure, unless the failure was substantially justified or other circumstances make an award of expenses unjust." Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(b)(2)(C) . Nevertheless, for the Court to impose sanctions in connection with a Rule 30(b)(6) deposition, "the inadequacies in a deponent's testimony must be egregious and not merely lacking in desired specificity in discrete areas." Kyoei Fire & Marine Ins. Co., Ltd. v. M/V Maritime Antalya , 248 F.R.D. 126 , 152 (S.D.N.Y. 2007) ; see also, e.g. , Agniel v. Cent. Park Boathouse LLC , No. 12-CV-7227 (NRB), 2015 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 13450 , 2015 WL 463971 , at * 2 (S.D.N.Y. Jan. 26, 2015) (noting that courts are reluctant "to award sanctions on the basis of deficient Rule 30(b)(6) testimony when counsel fail to make a good faith effort to resolve the deficiencies or when the application for sanctions appears tactically motivated"). Moreover, the Court "must allow additional time [for deposition, consistent with Rule 26 ] . . . if needed to fairly examine the deponent or if the deponent, another person, or any other circumstance impedes or delays the examination." Fed. R. Civ. P. 30(d)(1) . A grant of additional time under Rule 30(d)(1) is intended to be remedial, and is not in the nature of a sanction. See Dass v. City Univ. of New York , No. 18-CV-11325 (VSB) (OTW), 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 95273 , 2023 WL 3750012 , at * 1 (S.D.N.Y. May 31, 2023) (granting additional deposition time; request was made only under Rule 30(d)(1) and not Rule 30(d)(2) , where witness provided "lengthy evasive answers on nonmaterial matters" and counsel made multiple speaking objections). A. The witness was not sufficiently prepared under Rule 30(b)(6) Upon in camera review of both the deposition transcript and video, I find that OpenAI 's witness was not properly or sufficiently prepared. At the start of the deposition, when counsel were confirming for the record that they were proceeding on the 30(b)(6) topics that day, Mr. Monaco asked, "this is the one that refers to the corporate representative portion, or is that the other one?" (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. at 18). After confirming that Mr. Monaco understood that he was appearing as OpenAI 's corporate representative, Mr. Monaco described his preparation for this deposition as follows: "So outside of my own recollection just working in this area, I reviewed [Project Giraffe and Chat GPT] source code . . . relevant to the topics you mentioned. I reviewed meeting notes from Project Giraffe. I reviewed parts of Michael Lampe's deposition transcript. I reviewed a document from Lilian Weng on Project Giraffe generally , and reviewed some metrics related to filter hits. " Jan. 27 Rough Tr. at 19. (emphases added). Mr. Monaco did not speak with anyone other than his attorneys to prepare for his deposition. Id. When asked more specifics about the source code he reviewed, some of which he wrote, he described the Project Giraffe source code as the "Project Giraffe repository," and "wouldn't be able to name the files offhand," or "say offhand [who else wrote the source code] without looking at the Git history." Id. at 19-20. When asked specifics about the meeting notes from Project Giraffe, he was unable to describe it beyond "a running document where we kept notes from the Project Giraffe meetings." Id. When asked who is included in the "we" in that answer, Mr. Monaco said, "Whoever else is in the meetings or working on Project Giraffe." Id. When asked another follow up question about the individuals who kept notes on Project Giraffe, Mr. Gratz objected to form, after which Mr. Monaco answered, "Yeah, I wouldn't be able to name every single one. It is a long-running project with a lot of people working on it." Id. When asked to name people he remembered, he named, Lampe, Weng, Vik Goel, "[a]nd, yeah, the various folks from our legal team." Id. at 20-21. When asked if he, Lampe, Weng and Goel were "the people primarily involved in Project Giraffe," Mr. Gratz objected to form. This exchange followed: MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I don't understand what "primarily involved" means. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) Did anyone other than the four of you and your counsel work on Project Giraffe? A. Yes. Q. Who were those people? A. I would have to go back to meeting notes to name others. Q. Did you, Ms. Weng, Mr. Goel and Mr. Lampe conduct most of the work for Project Giraffe? (18 second pause) A: I'd say we conducted a large portion of it. I wouldn't be able to say that it was most of the work because I may not have knowledge of all the work that went into Project Giraffe. Id. at 21. This is just one of several instances where Mr. Monaco demonstrated that his preparation was inadequate. He responded with some version of not knowing or not being able to answer "offhand" at least 18 times; and the phrase "don't know" appears more than 40 times in Mr. Monaco 's answers during his 30(b)(6) testimony. Mr. Monaco paused for about 18 seconds while considering Ms. Maisel's question above, but even after such rumination, could not provide any more information about the personnel or scope of the work involved in Project Giraffe. Later in the deposition, Mr. Stein, on behalf of Class Plaintiffs, questioned Mr. Monaco further on his preparation. Mr. Monaco responded: A: So as I stated earlier, as part of my preparation, this involves just my own recollection of work that I've done being involved in the projects that we discussed. I am not sure that it's possible to put like a time bounds on that, if that makes sense. Q. Well, the question was, how many hours did you spend preparing specifically for the 30(b)(6) component of your testimony. So rephrasing that, apart from knowledge that you may have obtained from your personal work at OpenAI, how many additional hours did you spend preparing for this 30(b)(6) testimony today? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: I would estimate, in addition to the time and experience that I've acquired just from working on these projects, in addition to that, I've spent a few hours preparing for the 30 -- the 306B [sic] testimony. Q. (By Mr. Stein) And what -- about how many hours do you mean by "a few"? A. Between two and five, approximately. Q. Are you able to provide a more precise estimate between -- than between two and five hours? A. I'm not. I didn't keep track of the -- of the exact time. Q. And you understand that you've been designated to testify as to three topics, understanding that Mr. Gratz clarified that on one of those topics, you are partially designated? A. I understand that I'm here to testify on behalf of OpenAI on the three topics. Q. And did your preparation that you estimated took between two to five hours cover all three topics? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: I believe it did, as well as my own experience working on the -- the topics that we're talking about. Q. (By Mr. Stein) And for this, does this two to five hours include any interviewing that you did in preparation for your 30(b)(6) testimony? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: So the -- the preparation involves reviewing the documents that I mentioned. Id. at 30. Mr. Monaco then confirmed that he did not interview or have conversations with any people to prepare for his 30(b)(6) deposition. Id. at 116 . While this amount of preparation might be sufficient if, for example, the witness's personal knowledge were coterminous with the organization's, Mr. Monaco repeatedly showed that he did not know basic information concerning Open AI's "general techniques for limiting or preventing the regurgitation of text training data by the Relevant LLMs, including historical practices regarding the same." ECF 1312 at 4). Indeed, Mr. Monaco disclaimed, on behalf of OpenAI , any knowledge of Project Giraffe other than the identities of four people out of "many" who worked on Project Giraffe, and had a complete failure of recollection concerning Project Giraffe and the meeting notes, notwithstanding reviewing those very notes in preparation for his 30(b)(6) deposition. B. The pattern of repeated objections and the witness's answers impeded the deposition, which may warrant sanctions under Rule 30(d)(2) . Mr. Gratz's repeated objections, which were followed by Mr. Monaco 's evasive and nonresponsive answers, also frustrated and impeded the deposition. Counsel objected more than 200 times during the 30(b)(6) deposition alone: the words "objection," "form," and "scope" appear approximately 130, 100 and 200 times, respectively. Sometimes these words were used together; at other times, counsel used only a single word of "objection" or "form" or "scope" to object. OpenAI 's responses were similarly unhelpful, for example, in a line of questioning that was within the scope of Mr. Monaco 's designated topics: Q: How much [REDACTED] is used to run [REDACTED]? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I -- I don't know offhand. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) Can you say approximately. MR. GRATZ: Same objection. THE DEPONENT: Yeah, I would be guessing if I did. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) How many [REDACTED] has OpenAI done? A. I don't know offhand. Q. Is it more than ten? A. I really don't know. Q. Who would know? A. I'm not sure if any single person knows how many. Q. No one at OpenAI knows how many [REDACTED] were run? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I would rather characterize this as, the folks that have run [REDACTED] may have recollection of the work that they've done. I don't know if there's any single person that knows how many [REDACTED] have run overall. [Q.] Have you personally run any [REDACTED]? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I'm not sure what you mean by [REDACTED]. (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. at 28-29.) From this exchange as well as the others identified above, it is not clear whether Mr. Monaco 's insufficient testimony and nonresponsive answers were due to insufficient preparation, improper preparation, a misapprehension of the requirements of Rule 30(b)(6) , or a combination of these factors. III. CONCLUSION Mr. Monaco based his testimony—which should bind the corporation—on his own hazy recollections working on Project Giraffe, a review of source code that he and others wrote (but which he does not remember), and a few other documents: "part of" Lampe's deposition transcript, Lilian Weng's notes (which may or may not be the same as the Project Giraffe "meeting notes"), and "some metrics." (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. 19-21). He spoke to no other individuals to prepare for his deposition. But when asked even the simplest questions relevant to Plaintiffs' output claims, Mr. Monaco failed to answer or provided incomplete or evasive answers. For example, despite admitting that individuals other than he, Lampe, Weng and Goel and members of the legal team worked on Project Giraffe, he was unable to name a single other person who worked on Project Giraffe. Instead, he said he "would have to go back to" the meeting notes that he purportedly reviewed in preparation for his deposition to name a single other person who worked on the project. And then, when asked if he, Lampe, Goel and Weng performed "most" of the work on Project Giraffe, he paused for 18 seconds before stating that he was unable to answer the question. A 30(b)(6) witness cannot only rely on his own recollection if that recollection fails once he is asked anything more than the simplest, most basic questions. Mr. Monaco was not adequately prepared for his deposition. Mr. Monaco did not act alone. Mr. Monaco 's answers became increasingly evasive after counsel's repeated and unnecessary objections, only a few of which were summarized here. At the March 10, 2026, conference, the Court directed the parties to meet and confer so that Plaintiffs can conduct a more fulsome deposition of OpenAI on the topics that Mr. Monaco was designated; the parties agreed to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3.5 hour deposition on April 8, 2026. OpenAI is on notice that Mr. Monaco 's preparation for his deposition in January was not sufficient, and that counsel's objections unnecessarily delayed and impeded the deposition. But, because Mr. Monaco will be produced for additional 30(b)(6) deposition time, which the Court expects will cure the deficiencies in the deposition from January, the Court defers ruling on whether any other remedial discovery or sanctions are warranted until after OpenAI 's 30(b)(6) deposition(s) are completed. If sanctions are granted, such sanctions may be monetary, or they may include a recommendation that certain answers be imputed to—or deemed admissions by— OpenAI . SO ORDERED. When asked more specifics about the source code he reviewed, some of which he wrote, he described the Project Giraffe source code as the "Project Giraffe repository," and "wouldn't be able to name the files offhand," or "say offhand [who else wrote the source code] without looking at the Git history." Id. at 19-20. When asked specifics about the meeting notes from Project Giraffe, he was unable to describe it beyond "a running document where we kept notes from the Project Giraffe meetings." Id. When asked who is included in the "we" in that answer, Mr. Monaco said, "Whoever else is in the meetings or working on Project Giraffe." Id. When asked another follow up question about the individuals who kept notes on Project Giraffe, Mr. Gratz objected to form, after which Mr. Monaco answered, "Yeah, I wouldn't be able to name every single one. It is a long-running project with a lot of people working on it." Id. When asked to name people he remembered, he named, Lampe, Weng, Vik Goel, "[a]nd, yeah, the various folks from our legal team." Id. at 20-21. When asked if he, Lampe, Weng and Goel were "the people primarily involved in Project Giraffe," Mr. Gratz objected to form. This exchange followed: MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I don't understand what "primarily involved" means. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) Did anyone other than the four of you and your counsel work on Project Giraffe? A. Yes. Q. Who were those people? A. I would have to go back to meeting notes to name others. Q. Did you, Ms. Weng, Mr. Goel and Mr. Lampe conduct most of the work for Project Giraffe? (18 second pause) A: I'd say we conducted a large portion of it. I wouldn't be able to say that it was most of the work because I may not have knowledge of all the work that went into Project Giraffe. Id. at 21. This is just one of several instances where Mr. Monaco demonstrated that his preparation was inadequate. He responded with some version of not knowing or not being able to answer "offhand" at least 18 times; and the phrase "don't know" appears more than 40 times in Mr. Monaco 's answers during his 30(b)(6) testimony. Mr. Monaco paused for about 18 seconds while considering Ms. Maisel's question above, but even after such rumination, could not provide any more information about the personnel or scope of the work involved in Project Giraffe. Later in the deposition, Mr. Stein, on behalf of Class Plaintiffs, questioned Mr. Monaco further on his preparation. Mr. Monaco responded: A: So as I stated earlier, as part of my preparation, this involves just my own recollection of work that I've done being involved in the projects that we discussed. I am not sure that it's possible to put like a time bounds on that, if that makes sense. Q. Well, the question was, how many hours did you spend preparing specifically for the 30(b)(6) component of your testimony. So rephrasing that, apart from knowledge that you may have obtained from your personal work at OpenAI, how many additional hours did you spend preparing for this 30(b)(6) testimony today? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: I would estimate, in addition to the time and experience that I've acquired just from working on these projects, in addition to that, I've spent a few hours preparing for the 30 -- the 306B [sic] testimony. Q. (By Mr. Stein) And what -- about how many hours do you mean by "a few"? A. Between two and five, approximately. Q. Are you able to provide a more precise estimate between -- than between two and five hours? A. I'm not. I didn't keep track of the -- of the exact time. Q. And you understand that you've been designated to testify as to three topics, understanding that Mr. Gratz clarified that on one of those topics, you are partially designated? A. I understand that I'm here to testify on behalf of OpenAI on the three topics. Q. And did your preparation that you estimated took between two to five hours cover all three topics? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: I believe it did, as well as my own experience working on the -- the topics that we're talking about. Q. (By Mr. Stein) And for this, does this two to five hours include any interviewing that you did in preparation for your 30(b)(6) testimony? MR. GRATZ: Form. THE DEPONENT: So the -- the preparation involves reviewing the documents that I mentioned. Id. at 30. Mr. Monaco then confirmed that he did not interview or have conversations with any people to prepare for his 30(b)(6) deposition. Id. at 116 . While this amount of preparation might be sufficient if, for example, the witness's personal knowledge were coterminous with the organization's, Mr. Monaco repeatedly showed that he did not know basic information concerning Open AI's "general techniques for limiting or preventing the regurgitation of text training data by the Relevant LLMs, including historical practices regarding the same." ECF 1312 at 4). Indeed, Mr. Monaco disclaimed, on behalf of OpenAI , any knowledge of Project Giraffe other than the identities of four people out of "many" who worked on Project Giraffe, and had a complete failure of recollection concerning Project Giraffe and the meeting notes, notwithstanding reviewing those very notes in preparation for his 30(b)(6) deposition. B. The pattern of repeated objections and the witness's answers impeded the deposition, which may warrant sanctions under Rule 30(d)(2) . Mr. Gratz's repeated objections, which were followed by Mr. Monaco 's evasive and nonresponsive answers, also frustrated and impeded the deposition. Counsel objected more than 200 times during the 30(b)(6) deposition alone: the words "objection," "form," and "scope" appear approximately 130, 100 and 200 times, respectively. Sometimes these words were used together; at other times, counsel used only a single word of "objection" or "form" or "scope" to object. OpenAI 's responses were similarly unhelpful, for example, in a line of questioning that was within the scope of Mr. Monaco 's designated topics: Q: How much [REDACTED] is used to run [REDACTED]? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I -- I don't know offhand. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) Can you say approximately. MR. GRATZ: Same objection. THE DEPONENT: Yeah, I would be guessing if I did. Q. (By Ms. Maisel) How many [REDACTED] has OpenAI done? A. I don't know offhand. Q. Is it more than ten? A. I really don't know. Q. Who would know? A. I'm not sure if any single person knows how many. Q. No one at OpenAI knows how many [REDACTED] were run? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I would rather characterize this as, the folks that have run [REDACTED] may have recollection of the work that they've done. I don't know if there's any single person that knows how many [REDACTED] have run overall. [Q.] Have you personally run any [REDACTED]? MR. GRATZ: Objection. Form. THE DEPONENT: I'm not sure what you mean by [REDACTED]. (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. at 28-29.) From this exchange as well as the others identified above, it is not clear whether Mr. Monaco 's insufficient testimony and nonresponsive answers were due to insufficient preparation, improper preparation, a misapprehension of the requirements of Rule 30(b)(6) , or a combination of these factors. III. CONCLUSION Mr. Monaco based his testimony—which should bind the corporation—on his own hazy recollections working on Project Giraffe, a review of source code that he and others wrote (but which he does not remember), and a few other documents: "part of" Lampe's deposition transcript, Lilian Weng's notes (which may or may not be the same as the Project Giraffe "meeting notes"), and "some metrics." (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. 19-21). He spoke to no other individuals to prepare for his deposition. But when asked even the simplest questions relevant to Plaintiffs' output claims, Mr. Monaco failed to answer or provided incomplete or evasive answers. For example, despite admitting that individuals other than he, Lampe, Weng and Goel and members of the legal team worked on Project Giraffe, he was unable to name a single other person who worked on Project Giraffe. Instead, he said he "would have to go back to" the meeting notes that he purportedly reviewed in preparation for his deposition to name a single other person who worked on the project. And then, when asked if he, Lampe, Goel and Weng performed "most" of the work on Project Giraffe, he paused for 18 seconds before stating that he was unable to answer the question. A 30(b)(6) witness cannot only rely on his own recollection if that recollection fails once he is asked anything more than the simplest, most basic questions. Mr. Monaco was not adequately prepared for his deposition. Mr. Monaco did not act alone. Mr. Monaco 's answers became increasingly evasive after counsel's repeated and unnecessary objections, only a few of which were summarized here. At the March 10, 2026, conference, the Court directed the parties to meet and confer so that Plaintiffs can conduct a more fulsome deposition of OpenAI on the topics that Mr. Monaco was designated; the parties agreed to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3.5 hour deposition on April 8, 2026. OpenAI is on notice that Mr. Monaco 's preparation for his deposition in January was not sufficient, and that counsel's objections unnecessarily delayed and impeded the deposition. But, because Mr. Monaco will be produced for additional 30(b)(6) deposition time, which the Court expects will cure the deficiencies in the deposition from January, the Court defers ruling on whether any other remedial discovery or sanctions are warranted until after OpenAI 's 30(b)(6) deposition(s) are completed. If sanctions are granted, such sanctions may be monetary, or they may include a recommendation that certain answers be imputed to—or deemed admissions by— OpenAI . SO ORDERED. lexis_case_text: Kowal v. Sliver VR Techs., Inc., 2026 Cal. Super. LEXIS 18659 Lawrence P. Riff NATURE OF PROCEEDINGS: Hearing on Motion for Order to Relate Cases The court's tentative ruling has been posted on the court's website for review prior to the hearing. The matter is called for hearing. After hearing argument from the parties, the Court adopts its tentative ruling as the Order of the Court as follows: Background of 25SMCV06536 Kowal v. Sliver VR Technologies On December 15, 2025, Jerry Kowal, individually and derivatively on behalf of Sliver VR Technologies, Inc. , filed this action against Theta Labs, Inc. and Mitchell Liu as well as nominal defendant Sliver VR . The complaint asserted causes of action for: 1. fraudulent misrepresentation; 2. breach of contract; 3. breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing; 4. promissory estoppel; 5. retaliation in violation of Labor Code §1102.5 ; 6. constructive wrongful termination in violation of public policy; 7. unlawful, unfair, and fraudulent business practices in violation of California Business & Professions Code § 17200 et seq. ; 8. breach of fiduciary duty (derivative); and 9. conversion (derivative). On January 22, 2026, Judge Lisa K Sepe-Wiesenfeld issued an order finding 25SMCV06536 and 25STCV36594 were not related within the meaning of California Rules of Court, rule 3.300 . On February 9, 2026, Plaintiff filed the First Amended Complaint adding causes of action for defamation, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and negligent infliction of emotional distress. The FAC alleges Defendant Liu served as CEO of both Defendant Sliver VR and its subsidiary, Defendant Theta Labs . Plaintiff's compensation included, in part, THETA tokens. The FAC alleges Liu had no intention of creating a sustainable business, but rather utilized Plaintiff's connections to build public recognition and "pump and dump" THETA tokens. Plaintiff further alleges Liu solicited employee investment, but refused to issue the corresponding stock certificates as required. In 2021, Defendant Theta pivoted away from the streaming and content delivery business and into the non-fungible token ("NFT") marketplace business. Liu allegedly directed employees to generate false bids on NFT's on its platform to manipulate the marketplace and restricted employee allocations of Theta 's new cryptocurrencies while secretly dumping Liu's unrestricted allocations. The FAC further alleges anonymous accounts on X (formerly Twitter ) began posting defamatory allegations against Plaintiff after he filed this action. Background of 25STCV36594 Berry v. Sliver VR Technologies On December 15, 2025, Plaintiff Andrea Berry sued Sliver VR Technologies, Theta Labs, Inc. , and Mitchell Liu asserting causes of action for: 1) whistleblower retaliation ( Lab. Code § 1102.5 ); 2) sex/gender discrimination in violation of FEHA ( Cal. Gov. Code §§ 12926 , 12940 and 12945); 3) failure to prevent discrimination and retaliation in violation of FEHA ( Cal. Gov. Code § 12940(k) ); 4) fraudulent concealment; 5) wrongful termination in violation of public policy; and 6) willful failure to pay wages and waiting time penalties Cal. Labor Code §§ 201 , 203 , 210, 218. The complaint alleges Berry served as the Head of Business Development and suffered retaliation for complaining about illegal practices including consumer fraud, price manipulation and insider trading, fraud against Theta 's business partners, theft, and/or infringement of intellectual property, among other things." (Berry Compl. ¶ 88.) The complaint alleges Liu engaged in a "pump and dump" scheme for THETA tokens, engaged in market manipulation on Theta 's NFT platform, obtained employee investments in POG tokens through fraud, and failed to pay Berry half of the promised POG tokens as part of Berry's compensation. This case is currently pending in Department 55 of the Stanley Mosk Courthouse. Motion On February 18, 2026, Defendants Sliver VR , Theta Labs , and Mitchell Liu filed the instant motion seeking to relate 25SMCV06536 and 25STCV36594. Opposition In opposition, Plaintiff Andrea Berry contends her choice of venue should not be overridden and the cases are not related within the meaning of California Rules of Court, rule 3.300 . Reply In reply, Defendants reiterate their argument that the cases should be related and note an order relating cases does not impact Plaintiff's choice of venue. Motion to Relate Cases Standard Pursuant to Local Rule 3.3(f)(3) , "[i]n the event that the judge designated under California Rules of Court, rule 3.300(h)(1)(A)(B)(C) to make the decision, does not order related any of the cases set forth in the Notice of Related Cases, any party may file a motion to have the cases related. Department 1 shall hear the motion, if the cases are all pending in the Central District or are pending in two or more different districts." Department 1 Shall Relate the Two Cases On January 22, 2026, Judge Lisa K Sepe-Wiesenfeld issued an order finding 25SMCV06536 and 25STCV36594 were not related within the meaning of California Rules of Court, rule 3.300 . Accordingly, the motion is properly brought in Department 1. In opposition, Plaintiff contends her "choice of venue is proper and should not be overridden" and the action was "properly filed . . . in Los Angeles County Superior Court." (Opp. at 2:18-3:9.) However, an order relating cases does not affect venue, only courthouse and department assignment. ( Glade v. Glade (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1441 , 1449 ("Even though a superior court is divided into branches or departments, pursuant to California Constitution, article VI, section 4 , there is only one superior court in a county and jurisdiction is therefore vested in that court, not in any particular judge or department. Whether sitting separately or together, the judges hold but one and the same court.").) Cases are related when they (1) involve the same parties and are based on the same or similar claims, (2) arise from the same or substantially identical transactions, incidents, or events requiring the determination of the same or substantially identical questions of law or fact, (3) involve claims against, title to, possession of, or damages to the same property, or (4) are likely for other reasons to require substantial duplication of judicial resources if heard by different judges. ( Cal. R. Ct., rule 3.300(a) .) Defendants note the cases involve the same defendants and are based upon the same or similar claims and contend " CRC 3.300 plainly does not require that all parties must be identical. Indeed, cases have been related where the plaintiffs are different" citing Legal Aid Society of San Mateo County v. Department of Finance (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 166 , 179-180 . (Mot. at 3:11-21.) However, Legal Aid Society does not aid Defendants as the Court of Appeal merely noted "[t]he two cases were ordered related pursuant to California Rules of Court, rule 3.300(h)(1) ." ( Legal Aid Society of San Mateo County v. Department of Finance (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 166 , 180 .) The Court of Appeal did not address the propriety of relation or state which of the four grounds enumerated in California Rules of Court, rule 3.300(a) applied. The cases do not involve the same parties as each is brought by a different Plaintiff. ( Cal. R. Ct., rule 3.300(a)(1) .) The cases also do not involve property claims. ( Cal. R. Ct., rule 3.300(a)(3) .) However, as argued by Defendants, the cases do arise from substantially identical incidents, or events requiring the determination of the same or substantially identical questions of law or fact, ( Cal. R. Ct., rule 3.300(a)(2) ), and are likely for other reasons to require a substantial duplication of judicial resources if heard by different judges. ( Cal. R. Ct., rule 3.300(a)(4) .) Plaintiff's attempts in opposition to highlight differences in the two cases does not render the cases unrelated. In both cases, Plaintiffs allege they relied upon Defendants' representations and omissions when purchasing POG tokens in support of their fraud claims. (Berry Compl. ¶¶ 53-63, 118-121; Kowal FAC ¶¶ 123-130.) The Kowal FAC further contains allegations regarding Liu's communications with employees, such as Berry, regarding investment into the POG tokens. (Kowal FAC ¶¶ 158, 183.) Plaintiffs' complaints both allege Liu engaged in market manipulation related to various cryptocurrencies and NFTs, and misrepresented its AI Chatbot as being proprietary software, when it was merely a ChatGPT product. (Berry Compl. ¶¶ 32-35; Kowal FAC ¶¶ 86-88.) Both complaints also contain allegations regarding an improper kickback scheme in connection with the Secret Pineapple Society NFTs, (Berry Compl. ¶¶ 42-51; Kowal FAC ¶¶ 77-81), and Berry expressly alleges she reported her concerns to Liu with Kowal. (Berry Compl. ¶ 45.) Both Plaintiffs assert whistleblower retaliation claims based upon the same perceived unlawful conduct. (Berry Compl. ¶ 88; Kowal FAC ¶¶ 158.) Having a single judicial officer preside over the significant overlapping allegations regarding Defendants' conduct promotes judicial efficiency and reduces the potential for conflicting orders, including on clearly overlapping discovery. ( Tritek Telecom, Inc. v. Superior Court (2009) 169 Cal.App.4th 1385 , 1390 ("we note that four separate but related matters were assigned to three different judges and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on discovery matters. There are rules to prevent this.").) The Court finds the cases are related within the meaning of California Rules of Court, rule 3.300 and the motion is GRANTED. Conclusion Defendants' Motion to Relate and Reassign Cases is GRANTED. The matter is called for hearing. After hearing argument from the parties, the Court adopts its tentative ruling as the Order of the Court as follows: Background of 25SMCV06536 Kowal v. Sliver VR Technologies On December 15, 2025, Jerry Kowal, individually and derivatively on behalf of Sliver VR Technologies, Inc. , filed this action against Theta Labs, Inc. and Mitchell Liu as well as nominal defendant Sliver VR . On January 22, 2026, Judge Lisa K Sepe-Wiesenfeld issued an order finding 25SMCV06536 and 25STCV36594 were not related within the meaning of California Rules of Court, rule 3.300 . On February 9, 2026, Plaintiff filed the First Amended Complaint adding causes of action for defamation, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and negligent infliction of emotional distress. The FAC alleges Defendant Liu served as CEO of both Defendant Sliver VR and its subsidiary, Defendant Theta Labs . Plaintiff's compensation included, in part, THETA tokens. The FAC alleges Liu had no intention of creating a sustainable business, but rather utilized Plaintiff's connections to build public recognition and "pump and dump" THETA tokens. Sliver VR Technologies On December 15, 2025, Plaintiff Andrea Berry sued Sliver VR Technologies, Theta Labs, Inc. , and Mitchell Liu asserting causes of action for: 1) whistleblower retaliation ( Lab. Code § 1102.5 ); 2) sex/gender discrimination in violation of FEHA ( Cal. Gov. Code §§ 12926 , 12940 and 12945); 3) failure to prevent discrimination and retaliation in violation of FEHA ( Cal. Gov. Labor Code §§ 201 , 203 , 210, 218. (Berry Compl. ¶ 88.) The complaint alleges Liu engaged in a "pump and dump" scheme for THETA tokens, engaged in market manipulation on Theta 's NFT platform, obtained employee investments in POG tokens through fraud, and failed to pay Berry half of the promised POG tokens as part of Berry's compensation. This case is currently pending in Department 55 of the Stanley Mosk Courthouse. Motion On February 18, 2026, Defendants Sliver VR , Theta Labs , and Mitchell Liu filed the instant motion seeking to relate 25SMCV06536 and 25STCV36594. Whether sitting separately or together, the judges hold but one and the same court.").) Plaintiffs' complaints both allege Liu engaged in market manipulation related to various cryptocurrencies and NFTs, and misrepresented its AI Chatbot as being proprietary software, when it was merely a ChatGPT product. lexis_case_text: Lee v. Yonja, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 79674 Lee v. Yonja, 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 154404 , 2024 WL 3914473 (Apr. 15, 2024) Mark C. Scarsi Civil Minutes - General Proceedings: (In Chambers) Order Re: Motions For Attorney'S Fees (ECF Nos. 400, 402) The Court entered a judgment dismissing all claims with prejudice with respect to Defendants Seoul Broadcasting System International, Inc. (" SBS International "), and MBC Broadcasting Inc. (" MEC America "), and without prejudice with respect to all other Defendants. (J., ECF No. 393.) Defendant MBC America moves for an order awarding it $38,572.50 in attorney's fees. ( MBC America Mot., ECF No. 400-1.) Defendants Andong MBC Corp. , Daejeon MBC Corp., MBC Chungbuk Corp., MBC Gangwon-Youngdong, MBC Gyeongnam Corp. , and Yeosu MEC Corp. (together, "the MBC Defendants"), and SBS Corp. and SBS International (together, "the SBS Defendants," and together with the MBC Defendants, "the Joint Defendants"), also move for an order awarding them $77,634.00 in attorney's fees and $509.26 in costs. (Joint Defs.' Mot., ECF No. 402.) Plaintiff filed an opposition, (Opp'n, ECF No. 403), and the Joint Defendants filed a reply, (Joint Defs.' Reply, ECF No. 406). The Court deems the motions appropriate for decision without oral argument and takes off calendar the hearing on the Joint Defendants' motion set for March 30, 2026. Fed. R. Civ. P. 78(b) ; C.D. Cal. R. 7-15 . I. LEGAL STANDARD Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 54(d)(2) authorizes a party to make a claim for attorney's fees and related nontaxable expenses by motion. Rule 54 "creates a procedure but not a right to recover attorneys' fees." MRO Commc'ns, Inc. v. Am. Tel. & Tel. Co. , 197 F.3d 1276 , 1280 (9th Cir. 1999) . Accordingly, the rule requires "an independent source of authority for an award of attorneys' fees." Id. at 1281 . As relevant to both motions, a court may award reasonable fees and costs to a prevailing party in a copyright infringement action. 17 U.S.C. § 505 ; see Fogerty v. Fantasy, Inc. , 510 U.S. 517 , 534 , 114 S. Ct. 1023 , 127 L. Ed. 2d 455 (1994) (recognizing § 505 extends to prevailing defendants). "The touchstone of the decision to award attorneys' fees is whether the successful defense, and the circumstances surrounding it, further the Copyright Act 's 'essential goals.'" Tresóna Multimedia, LLC v. Burbank High Sch. Vocal Music Ass'n , 953 F.3d 638 , 653 (9th Cir. 2020) (quoting Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. , 576 U.S. 197 , 209 (2016) ). Courts may consider the degree of success obtained, frivolousness, motivation, the objective unreasonableness of the losing party's factual and legal contentions, and considerations of compensation and deterrence. Id. These considerations are not exclusive, and "exceptional circumstances are not a prerequisite to an award." Fantasy, Inc. v. Fogerty , 94 F.3d 553 , 559 (9th Cir. 1996) (internal quotation marks omitted). II. DISCUSSION Defendants argue that the Court should award them fees because they all are prevailing parties with respect to Plaintiff's copyright claims. MBC America and SBS International prevailed on motions for summary judgment. ( See generally MBC America MSJ Order, ECF No. 371; SBS MSJ Order, ECF No. 367.) MBC America contends that it obtained total success on the merits of Plaintiff's copyright claim at summary judgment, that Plaintiff's claims were objectively unreasonable at the outset of litigation and became increasingly frivolous as the litigation progressed because Plaintiff failed to produce any admissible evidence in support of his claims, that Plaintiff's lack of evidentiary basis suggests an improper motivation in his pursuit of the litigation, and that considerations of compensation and deterrence favor a fee award to discourage Plaintiff from pursuing copyright claims without admissible evidence. (MBC America Mot. 3-6.) SBS International advances similar arguments, asserting that Plaintiff's claims against it relied on false evidence and that Plaintiff pursued the action for the purpose of harassment. (Joint Defs.' Mot. 15-16.) SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants are also prevailing parties because the Court granted their motions to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction. (Order on Mots. to Dismiss 9, ECF No. 348.) SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants contend that Plaintiff's jurisdictional theory was objectively unreasonable and frivolous because Plaintiff should have known that "merely uploading a video to the internet from a foreign country, without more, is not sufficient for jurisdiction." (Joint Defs.' Mot. 14.) For the reasons discussed below, the Court declines to exercise its discretion to award attorneys' fees and costs. A. Degree of Success The Court first considers the degree of the success of the Defendants who procured a grant of summary judgment. Both MBC America and SBS International argue that they obtained total success at summary judgment, and therefore fees are appropriate. (MBC America Mot. 3-4; Joint Defs.' Mot. 13-14.) However, fees in copyright cases are more easily justified if the victor succeeded on the merits. See Epikhin v. Game Insight N. Am. , No. 14-CV-04383-LHK, 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44170 , at * 12-13 (N.D. Cal. Mar. 31, 2016) (concluding success on the merits weighs in favor of an attorneys' fees award more than technical success); Fogerty , 94 F.3d at 560 (noting that fees are less justified if a defendant does not prevail on the merits). Moreover, the degree-of-success factor alone is not dispositive to the issue of fees. Actuate Corp. v. Aon Corp. , No. C 10-05750 WHA, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 118346 , at * 4 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 21, 2012) ("While success may be a prerequisite to obtaining fees, it does not automatically support awarding fees in the absence of additional considerations."). Therefore, with respect to MBC America and SBS International , the Court finds that this factor only weighs slightly in favor of a fee award. The Court finds that this factor weighs slightly against the other defendants, SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. While the Court dismissed these defendants, this dismissal was without prejudice. (Order on Mots. to Dismiss 10 & 10 n.2.) Because SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants prevailed only on jurisdictional grounds, they potentially remain subject to suit in a federal district court if Plaintiff can plead and prove facts sufficient to support a court's exercise of personal jurisdiction over them—or to suit in a South Korean court. See, e.g. , Michael Grecco Prods. Inc. v. ImageSelect B.V. , No. LA CV22-07398 JAK (JPRx), 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 240309 , at * 9 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 4, 2024) (concluding that the degree-of-success factor "weigh[ed] slightly against" a defendant who prevailed on jurisdictional grounds but was dismissed without prejudice). B. Objective Unreasonableness and Frivolousness The Court "examine[s] objective reasonableness next, because that factor is given 'substantial' weight.'" Tresóna Multimedia, LLC , 953 F.3d at 653 (quoting Shame on You Prods., Inc. v. Banks , 893 F.3d 661 , 666 (9th Cir. 2018) ). "[A] legal argument that loses is not necessarily unreasonable." Shame on You Prods., Inc. , 893 F.3d at 666 . A claim is objectively unreasonable where the party advancing it "should have known from the outset that its chances of success in th[e] case were slim to none." SOFA Ent., Inc. v. Dodger Prods., Inc. , 709 F.3d 1273 , 1280 (9th Cir. 2013) . A frivolous claim is also a basis for an attorney's fees award. See Fogerty , 510 U.S. at 534 n.19 . "A frivolous claim is one in which the factual contention is clearly baseless, such as factual claims that are fantastical or delusional scenarios." Bisson-Dath v. Sony Comput. Ent. Am. Inc. , No. CV-08-1235 SC, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 103159 , at * 5 (N.D. Cal. July 24, 2012) (internal quotation marks omitted). The Court first addresses Plaintiff's claims against MBC America and SBS International . At the outset, the Court notes that MBC America and SBS International both opposed granting Plaintiff leave to amend his fourth amended complaint, (MBC America Opp'n to Mot. for Leave to Amend, ECF No. 257; Joint Defs.' Opp'n to Mot. for Leave to Amend, ECF No. 258), but the Court granted Plaintiff leave to amend because it was possible for at least some of Plaintiff's claims to be pleaded sufficiently if certain defects were cured by amendment, (Order Gr. Leave to Amend 4-5, ECF No. 264). At summary judgment, the Court ultimately concluded that Plaintiff had "fail[ed] to adduce sufficient evidence to sustain his burden of production as to a material element of his copyright infringement claim." (MBC America MSJ Order 7; SBS MSJ Order 10.) Notwithstanding questions raised with respect to the authenticity and accuracy of certain exhibits and evidence submitted by Plaintiff over the course of the litigation, ( see, e.g. , MBC America MSJ Order 6; SBS MSJ Order 8), the Court finds that Plaintiff's legal argument was not objectively unreasonable or frivolous. Rather, it appears that Plaintiff struggled throughout discovery and the motions for summary judgment to understand his evidentiary burdens and the rules governing federal litigation. Further, as the Court analyzes infra , the Court does not find evidence in the record suggesting that Plaintiff sued Defendants in bad faith. As a result, both these factors weigh against fees. The Court reaches the same conclusion with respect to Plaintiff's theory of personal jurisdiction over the remaining defendants, SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. These Defendants assert that Plaintiff should have known that his theory of personal jurisdiction was deficient based on other actions Plaintiff has pursued in the Central District of California in which foreign defendants were dismissed for lack of personal jurisdiction. ( See Joint Defs.' Mot. 14 (citing Lee v. TV Chosun Corp. , No. CV 22-0933 DSF (KSx), 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 209775 , 2022 WL 17262129 , at * 3-4 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 18, 2022) ).) Although Plaintiff's jurisdictional theory was flawed, the Court declines to find that it was objectively unreasonable or frivolous given that additional factual allegations concerning acts by SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants that were expressly aimed at California might have cured these deficiencies. Accordingly, the Court concludes that these factors weigh against fees with respect to SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. C. Bad Faith "Under this factor, the existence of bad faith or an improper motive in bringing or pursuing an action weighs in favor of an award of fees to a prevailing party. A finding of bad faith can be based on actions that led to the lawsuit, as well as on the conduct of the litigation." Tolkien Tr. v. Polychron , No. 2:23-cv-04300-SVW-E, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 226135 , at * 30 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 14, 2023) (internal quotation marks omitted). There is insufficient evidence in the record to conclude that Plaintiff brought this suit against Defendants in bad faith. MBC America does not provide the Court with any compelling evidence to the contrary, instead relying on attorney argument that Plaintiff's failure to proffer admissible evidence in support of his claims "reflect[ed] an improper motivation to extract settlement leverage or prolong litigation." (MBC America Mot. 5-6.) The Joint Defendants offer additional bases for their assertions of bad faith. Counsel for the Joint Defendants avers that "[o]ver the past several years, Plaintiff has made repeated approaches to each of the Defendants, threatening to file suit in the U.S. unless they would agree [to] pay him several annual licensing fees of several thousand dollars per year," and that Plaintiff consistently refused to meet and confer on dispositive motions in this action. (Lee Decl. ¶ 12, ECF No. 402-2.) The Joint Defendants also contend that Plaintiff likely used generative artificial intelligence ("AI") to draft at least some of his filings, asserting that an analysis using the platform GPTZero "determined that both of Plaintiff's oppositions to [IBK and SBS 's] corporate disclosure statements were 100% likely to be fully AI generated" and that Plaintiff's response to an answer by SBS International "was 98% likely to be fully AI generated, and 2% likely to be a mix of AI-generated and human-written content." ( Id. ¶ 11.) While the Court is sympathetic to the issues the Joint Defendants raise with respect to Plaintiff's deficient filings and possible reliance on AI during the litigation, the Court does not believe that this alone is evidence of bad faith. Of course, use of AI does not absolve parties of their obligations under Rule 11 , but the Joint Defendants do not assert that any of Plaintiff's filings that possibly relied on AI were false; rather, they submit that the filings were "low-effort" and a "nuisance." (Joint Defs.' Mot. 16.) A finding of bad faith requires more. In addition, the Joint Defendants suggest that Plaintiff may have relied on "possibly self-manufactured" evidence in the form of bootleg DVDs to support his claims at summary judgment. ( Id. at 15.) In its summary judgment orders, the Court concluded that these DVDs were "obviously bootlegs," but it did not conclude that the DVDs were personally made by Plaintiff. (MBC America MSJ Order 6; SBS MSJ Order 8.) While the Joint Defendants posit that "there is reason to suspect that these DVDs were manufactured by Plaintiff himself, given the identical appearance and formatting of the SBS and MBC DVDs," (Joint Defs.' Mot. 15), the Court declines to find bad faith on this speculative basis. The Court understands Plaintiff's pursuit of this litigation, and other litigations in this District, as his legitimate attempt to protect the subject works from alleged copyright infringement. See Yonay v. Paramount Pictures Corp. , No. CV 22-3846 PA (GJSx), 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100335 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. May 31, 2024) (finding no bad faith because the court believed "[p]laintiffs' claims were motivated by an honestly held belief"). Accordingly, this factor weighs against an award of fees. D. Considerations of Compensation and Deterrence Finally, in deciding whether to award attorneys' fees under the Copyright Act , courts assess "the need to advance considerations of compensation and deterrence." Tresóna Multimedia, LLC , 953 F.3d at 653 (internal quotation marks omitted). "When analyzing whether awarding attorneys' fees under the Copyright Act is proper, the most important factor is whether an award will further the purposes of the Act." Microsoft Corp. v. Softwareau.com , No. CV 15-08019-AB (JEMx), 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 206769 , at * 12-13 (C.D. Cal. June 22, 2016) (cleaned up). The Court does not find that this "most important" factor weighs in favor of awarding fees. The purpose of the Act is to discourage copyright infringement, but in this case the Court's rulings narrowly focused on whether Plaintiff had adduced evidence sufficient to carry his burden at summary judgment, and whether the Court could properly exercise personal jurisdiction over SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. Given that Plaintiff may have legitimate legal claims to assert, the Court fails to see how an award of fees would further the goals of the Copyright Act . Moreover, the Court does not find that fees are necessary to deter Plaintiff from further action. The Court dismissed Plaintiff's claims against MBC America and SBS International with prejudice. ( See generally J.) While the Court dismissed Plaintiff's claims against SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants without prejudice, because Plaintiff may have had a legitimate legal claim, he remains free to reassert those claims related to the alleged infringement in another proceeding in a court of competent jurisdiction. The Court finds that this factor weighs against granting a fee award. III. CONCLUSION On balance, the factors do not support an award of attorneys' fees and costs under 17 U.S.C. § 505 . The motions are denied. IT IS SO ORDERED. Lee v. Yonja, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 79674 Lee v. Yonja Lee v. Yonja Lee v. Yonja, 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 154404 , 2024 WL 3914473 (Apr. 15, 2024) Mark C. Defendant MBC America moves for an order awarding it $38,572.50 in attorney's fees. ( MBC America Mot., ECF No. 400-1.) Defendants Andong MBC Corp. , Daejeon MBC Corp., MBC Chungbuk Corp., MBC Gangwon-Youngdong, MBC Gyeongnam Corp. , and Yeosu MEC Corp. (together, "the MBC Defendants"), and SBS Corp. and SBS International (together, "the SBS Defendants," and together with the MBC Defendants, "the Joint Defendants"), also move for an order awarding them $77,634.00 in attorney's fees and $509.26 in costs. (Joint Defs.' Mot., ECF No. 402.) Plaintiff filed an opposition, (Opp'n, ECF No. 403), and the Joint Defendants filed a reply, (Joint Defs.' Reply, ECF No. 406). The Court deems the motions appropriate for decision without oral argument and takes off calendar the hearing on the Joint Defendants' motion set for March 30, 2026. Fed. R. Civ. P. 78(b) ; C.D. Cal. R. 7-15 . I. LEGAL STANDARD Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 54(d)(2) authorizes a party to make a claim for attorney's fees and related nontaxable expenses by motion. Rule 54 "creates a procedure but not a right to recover attorneys' fees." MRO Commc'ns, Inc. v. Am. Tel. & Tel. Co. , 197 F.3d 1276 , 1280 (9th Cir. 1999) . Accordingly, the rule requires "an independent source of authority for an award of attorneys' fees." Id. at 1281 . As relevant to both motions, a court may award reasonable fees and costs to a prevailing party in a copyright infringement action. 17 U.S.C. § 505 ; see Fogerty v. Fantasy, Inc. , 510 U.S. 517 , 534 , 114 S. Ct. 1023 , 127 L. Ed. 2d 455 (1994) (recognizing § 505 extends to prevailing defendants). "The touchstone of the decision to award attorneys' fees is whether the successful defense, and the circumstances surrounding it, further the Copyright Act 's 'essential goals.'" Tresóna Multimedia, LLC v. Burbank High Sch. Vocal Music Ass'n , 953 F.3d 638 , 653 (9th Cir. 2020) (quoting Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. , 576 U.S. 197 , 209 (2016) ). Courts may consider the degree of success obtained, frivolousness, motivation, the objective unreasonableness of the losing party's factual and legal contentions, and considerations of compensation and deterrence. Id. These considerations are not exclusive, and "exceptional circumstances are not a prerequisite to an award." Fantasy, Inc. v. Fogerty , 94 F.3d 553 , 559 (9th Cir. 1996) (internal quotation marks omitted). II. DISCUSSION Defendants argue that the Court should award them fees because they all are prevailing parties with respect to Plaintiff's copyright claims. MBC America and SBS International prevailed on motions for summary judgment. ( See generally MBC America MSJ Order, ECF No. 371; SBS MSJ Order, ECF No. 367.) MBC America contends that it obtained total success on the merits of Plaintiff's copyright claim at summary judgment, that Plaintiff's claims were objectively unreasonable at the outset of litigation and became increasingly frivolous as the litigation progressed because Plaintiff failed to produce any admissible evidence in support of his claims, that Plaintiff's lack of evidentiary basis suggests an improper motivation in his pursuit of the litigation, and that considerations of compensation and deterrence favor a fee award to discourage Plaintiff from pursuing copyright claims without admissible evidence. (MBC America Mot. 3-6.) SBS International advances similar arguments, asserting that Plaintiff's claims against it relied on false evidence and that Plaintiff pursued the action for the purpose of harassment. (Joint Defs.' Mot. 15-16.) SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants are also prevailing parties because the Court granted their motions to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction. (Order on Mots. to Dismiss 9, ECF No. 348.) SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants contend that Plaintiff's jurisdictional theory was objectively unreasonable and frivolous because Plaintiff should have known that "merely uploading a video to the internet from a foreign country, without more, is not sufficient for jurisdiction." (Joint Defs.' Mot. 14.) For the reasons discussed below, the Court declines to exercise its discretion to award attorneys' fees and costs. A. Degree of Success The Court first considers the degree of the success of the Defendants who procured a grant of summary judgment. Both MBC America and SBS International argue that they obtained total success at summary judgment, and therefore fees are appropriate. (MBC America Mot. 3-4; Joint Defs.' Mot. 13-14.) However, fees in copyright cases are more easily justified if the victor succeeded on the merits. See Epikhin v. Game Insight N. Am. , No. 14-CV-04383-LHK, 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 44170 , at * 12-13 (N.D. Cal. Mar. 31, 2016) (concluding success on the merits weighs in favor of an attorneys' fees award more than technical success); Fogerty , 94 F.3d at 560 (noting that fees are less justified if a defendant does not prevail on the merits). Moreover, the degree-of-success factor alone is not dispositive to the issue of fees. Actuate Corp. v. Aon Corp. , No. C 10-05750 WHA, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 118346 , at * 4 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 21, 2012) ("While success may be a prerequisite to obtaining fees, it does not automatically support awarding fees in the absence of additional considerations."). Therefore, with respect to MBC America and SBS International , the Court finds that this factor only weighs slightly in favor of a fee award. The Court finds that this factor weighs slightly against the other defendants, SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. While the Court dismissed these defendants, this dismissal was without prejudice. (Order on Mots. to Dismiss 10 & 10 n.2.) Because SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants prevailed only on jurisdictional grounds, they potentially remain subject to suit in a federal district court if Plaintiff can plead and prove facts sufficient to support a court's exercise of personal jurisdiction over them—or to suit in a South Korean court. See, e.g. , Michael Grecco Prods. Inc. v. ImageSelect B.V. , No. LA CV22-07398 JAK (JPRx), 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 240309 , at * 9 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 4, 2024) (concluding that the degree-of-success factor "weigh[ed] slightly against" a defendant who prevailed on jurisdictional grounds but was dismissed without prejudice). B. Objective Unreasonableness and Frivolousness The Court "examine[s] objective reasonableness next, because that factor is given 'substantial' weight.'" Tresóna Multimedia, LLC , 953 F.3d at 653 (quoting Shame on You Prods., Inc. v. Banks , 893 F.3d 661 , 666 (9th Cir. 2018) ). "[A] legal argument that loses is not necessarily unreasonable." Shame on You Prods., Inc. , 893 F.3d at 666 . A claim is objectively unreasonable where the party advancing it "should have known from the outset that its chances of success in th[e] case were slim to none." SOFA Ent., Inc. v. Dodger Prods., Inc. , 709 F.3d 1273 , 1280 (9th Cir. 2013) . A frivolous claim is also a basis for an attorney's fees award. See Fogerty , 510 U.S. at 534 n.19 . "A frivolous claim is one in which the factual contention is clearly baseless, such as factual claims that are fantastical or delusional scenarios." Bisson-Dath v. Sony Comput. Ent. Am. Inc. , No. CV-08-1235 SC, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 103159 , at * 5 (N.D. Cal. July 24, 2012) (internal quotation marks omitted). The Court first addresses Plaintiff's claims against MBC America and SBS International . At the outset, the Court notes that MBC America and SBS International both opposed granting Plaintiff leave to amend his fourth amended complaint, (MBC America Opp'n to Mot. for Leave to Amend, ECF No. 257; Joint Defs.' Opp'n to Mot. for Leave to Amend, ECF No. 258), but the Court granted Plaintiff leave to amend because it was possible for at least some of Plaintiff's claims to be pleaded sufficiently if certain defects were cured by amendment, (Order Gr. Leave to Amend 4-5, ECF No. 264). At summary judgment, the Court ultimately concluded that Plaintiff had "fail[ed] to adduce sufficient evidence to sustain his burden of production as to a material element of his copyright infringement claim." (MBC America MSJ Order 7; SBS MSJ Order 10.) Notwithstanding questions raised with respect to the authenticity and accuracy of certain exhibits and evidence submitted by Plaintiff over the course of the litigation, ( see, e.g. , MBC America MSJ Order 6; SBS MSJ Order 8), the Court finds that Plaintiff's legal argument was not objectively unreasonable or frivolous. Rather, it appears that Plaintiff struggled throughout discovery and the motions for summary judgment to understand his evidentiary burdens and the rules governing federal litigation. Further, as the Court analyzes infra , the Court does not find evidence in the record suggesting that Plaintiff sued Defendants in bad faith. As a result, both these factors weigh against fees. The Court reaches the same conclusion with respect to Plaintiff's theory of personal jurisdiction over the remaining defendants, SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. These Defendants assert that Plaintiff should have known that his theory of personal jurisdiction was deficient based on other actions Plaintiff has pursued in the Central District of California in which foreign defendants were dismissed for lack of personal jurisdiction. ( See Joint Defs.' Mot. 14 (citing Lee v. TV Chosun Corp. , No. CV 22-0933 DSF (KSx), 2022 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 209775 , 2022 WL 17262129 , at * 3-4 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 18, 2022) ).) Although Plaintiff's jurisdictional theory was flawed, the Court declines to find that it was objectively unreasonable or frivolous given that additional factual allegations concerning acts by SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants that were expressly aimed at California might have cured these deficiencies. Accordingly, the Court concludes that these factors weigh against fees with respect to SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. C. Bad Faith "Under this factor, the existence of bad faith or an improper motive in bringing or pursuing an action weighs in favor of an award of fees to a prevailing party. A finding of bad faith can be based on actions that led to the lawsuit, as well as on the conduct of the litigation." Tolkien Tr. v. Polychron , No. 2:23-cv-04300-SVW-E, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 226135 , at * 30 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 14, 2023) (internal quotation marks omitted). There is insufficient evidence in the record to conclude that Plaintiff brought this suit against Defendants in bad faith. MBC America does not provide the Court with any compelling evidence to the contrary, instead relying on attorney argument that Plaintiff's failure to proffer admissible evidence in support of his claims "reflect[ed] an improper motivation to extract settlement leverage or prolong litigation." (MBC America Mot. 5-6.) The Joint Defendants offer additional bases for their assertions of bad faith. Counsel for the Joint Defendants avers that "[o]ver the past several years, Plaintiff has made repeated approaches to each of the Defendants, threatening to file suit in the U.S. unless they would agree [to] pay him several annual licensing fees of several thousand dollars per year," and that Plaintiff consistently refused to meet and confer on dispositive motions in this action. (Lee Decl. ¶ 12, ECF No. 402-2.) The Joint Defendants also contend that Plaintiff likely used generative artificial intelligence ("AI") to draft at least some of his filings, asserting that an analysis using the platform GPTZero "determined that both of Plaintiff's oppositions to [IBK and SBS 's] corporate disclosure statements were 100% likely to be fully AI generated" and that Plaintiff's response to an answer by SBS International "was 98% likely to be fully AI generated, and 2% likely to be a mix of AI-generated and human-written content." ( Id. ¶ 11.) While the Court is sympathetic to the issues the Joint Defendants raise with respect to Plaintiff's deficient filings and possible reliance on AI during the litigation, the Court does not believe that this alone is evidence of bad faith. Of course, use of AI does not absolve parties of their obligations under Rule 11 , but the Joint Defendants do not assert that any of Plaintiff's filings that possibly relied on AI were false; rather, they submit that the filings were "low-effort" and a "nuisance." (Joint Defs.' Mot. 16.) A finding of bad faith requires more. In addition, the Joint Defendants suggest that Plaintiff may have relied on "possibly self-manufactured" evidence in the form of bootleg DVDs to support his claims at summary judgment. ( Id. at 15.) In its summary judgment orders, the Court concluded that these DVDs were "obviously bootlegs," but it did not conclude that the DVDs were personally made by Plaintiff. (MBC America MSJ Order 6; SBS MSJ Order 8.) While the Joint Defendants posit that "there is reason to suspect that these DVDs were manufactured by Plaintiff himself, given the identical appearance and formatting of the SBS and MBC DVDs," (Joint Defs.' Mot. 15), the Court declines to find bad faith on this speculative basis. The Court understands Plaintiff's pursuit of this litigation, and other litigations in this District, as his legitimate attempt to protect the subject works from alleged copyright infringement. See Yonay v. Paramount Pictures Corp. , No. CV 22-3846 PA (GJSx), 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 100335 , at * 4 (C.D. Cal. May 31, 2024) (finding no bad faith because the court believed "[p]laintiffs' claims were motivated by an honestly held belief"). Accordingly, this factor weighs against an award of fees. D. Considerations of Compensation and Deterrence Finally, in deciding whether to award attorneys' fees under the Copyright Act , courts assess "the need to advance considerations of compensation and deterrence." Tresóna Multimedia, LLC , 953 F.3d at 653 (internal quotation marks omitted). "When analyzing whether awarding attorneys' fees under the Copyright Act is proper, the most important factor is whether an award will further the purposes of the Act." Microsoft Corp. v. Softwareau.com , No. CV 15-08019-AB (JEMx), 2016 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 206769 , at * 12-13 (C.D. Cal. June 22, 2016) (cleaned up). The Court does not find that this "most important" factor weighs in favor of awarding fees. The purpose of the Act is to discourage copyright infringement, but in this case the Court's rulings narrowly focused on whether Plaintiff had adduced evidence sufficient to carry his burden at summary judgment, and whether the Court could properly exercise personal jurisdiction over SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants. Given that Plaintiff may have legitimate legal claims to assert, the Court fails to see how an award of fees would further the goals of the Copyright Act . Moreover, the Court does not find that fees are necessary to deter Plaintiff from further action. The Court dismissed Plaintiff's claims against MBC America and SBS International with prejudice. ( See generally J.) While the Court dismissed Plaintiff's claims against SBS Corp. and the MBC Defendants without prejudice, because Plaintiff may have had a legitimate legal claim, he remains free to reassert those claims related to the alleged infringement in another proceeding in a court of competent jurisdiction. The Court finds that this factor weighs against granting a fee award. III. CONCLUSION On balance, the factors do not support an award of attorneys' fees and costs under 17 U.S.C. § 505 . The motions are denied. IT IS SO ORDERED. Beom SU Lee, Plaintiff, Pro se, Los Angeles , CA . For MBC CHUNGBUK, Defendant: Jumin Lee , Bird Marella Rhow Lincenberg Drooks and Nessim LLP , Los Angeles , CA . For SBS INTERNATIONAL INC. , Erroneously Sued As, SBS INTERNATIONAL INC. , Seoul Broadcasting System International Inc. , Defendants: Timothy B Yoo , LEAD ATTORNEY, Jumin Lee , Bird Marella Rhow Lincenberg Drooks and Nessim LLP , Los Angeles , CA . For MBC America, Defendant: Jacob B. Bach , ACI Law Group , La Palma , CA . For Stephen Lobbin , Mediator ( ADR Panel ): Stephen M. Lobbin , LEAD ATTORNEY, SML Avvocati PC , San Diego , CA . Mark C.
